### Skimo — 2014-07-17T15:52:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-134740>

Hello everyone,

I just introduced myself here :
[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/on-a-quest-to-a-better-self.11345/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/on-a-quest-to-a-better-self.11345/)

To sum it up, I am 33, french, diagnosed with hypothyroidism and treated for it since many years.

I was 288lbs the 6th of june and started a low carb diet then.
I am 272lbs today and I have read every thing I could since about nutrition and dieting and I intend to go on this way...

I stubbled upon Dr Kruse's blog thanks to Werebear, bought the Epi Paleo Rx Book and I am implementing the Leptin Rx into my daily routine.

Some of my favorites low carb veggies are nightshades (green bell pepper and eggplant) and I used to replace dairy with soy product, but I stopped. I am sad to see blueberries quit the list of possible foods, but raspberries are nice too anyway...

For breakfast, I am progressively reaching 50gr of protein within 30min of waking, last week I was at three eggs and 50gr of bacon fried in coconut oil, now I am at four eggs and 100gr of bacon and I will try 6eggs and 100gr of bacon soon.

I have stopped exercising since three days. I was doing cardio intervals and strength training every other day 6 days a week in the morning and I hope I will be able to resume exercising when I have all the signs of leptin sensitivity. I already went to visit a casual sport's club near my work to be able to workout at lunch breaks. I should have the time then when theorically I may not be hungry anymore. Actually I wasn't hungry at midday today, my lunch is still in its box...

Having dinner 4 to 5 hours before bedtime is a little tricky, but I think that getting to work earlier to leave earlier will be the solution.

I did some shopping two days ago and bought canned fish (mackerel and sardine), farmers eggs, organic burgers and some buttered snails...
I am not living near the ocean or the sea, but Burgundy is famous for its snails. It isn't in the meat list, but I would think it should be high since they are gasteropodes like many seafoods.
I also have canned mussels and cod liver on my shopping list.

I have always been an indoor person, my grand mother had to throw me out during summer time so I would "get some sun"... I was always walking on the shady side of the street, getting sun burns the first time of the year I was exposed to the sun during my teens. Since three days, I am getting on the sunny side of the street, and I take a sunbath outside at midday. I am getting a little pink when I come back inside, but no sunburn so far. Getting out in the morning is the next step...

DBF prefers to have lights on when it gets dark outside, to go on playing on his computer. Being obese himself at high risk for diabetes, he would certainly benefit a Leptin Rx, but he has to decide it by himself and I can't do anything for him. For now, I eat salad, he eats grain...
For the moment, the sun set when I go to sleep so it isn't a problem, I just turn on the light when going out of the room to get in bed.
But I will have to find blue lights blockers for both of us... at least I can help with that...

I'll report my progress soon.

Have a nice day everyone!

### Skimo — 2014-07-18T07:04:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-134787>

Caroline, I am just beginning to read about non native EMF and blue lite, I have decided to invest into blue blockers before the autumn, and I guess I will have to return the blue tooth ear set I just bought for my phone...
I have a long road ahead of me to fix myself, I'll focus first on that. I am trying to work on not worrying too much for the ones I love, it's not easy, but if I can't do anything, worrying is useless... writing it down helps too...

Bonjour Lahelada!
I easily fall asleep around 10pm and I tend to wake up before my alarm clock around 6am, but from time to time I awaken earlier between 3 and 5am, it's worse when I suffer from stress. Today I awoke at 4am, it was too warm in our bedroom... guess I have some cortisol peak related to those early awakening, if I try and fall back asleep one hour later I am just a mess in the morning...

Jude, with butter, parsley and garlic. Last time I ate some at a restaurant baked and served in mushroom caps, it was delicious and totally low carb

### Skimo — 2014-07-19T07:56:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-134879>

Yesterday I awoke really early (4am30).

I was hungry at lunch so I ate smoked mackerel, corn salad and a sheep greek yogurt.

I took a second cold bath after dinner. This time it felt harder to get in but once there I added ice and almost fell asleep... I went to bet at 9pm and woke up at 5am this morning, it's early, but with the warm weather we have those days, I enjoy the fresh hours of the day.

I went up to 6eggs and 100gr of bacon this morning so I am above 50gr of protein. I'll see how I feel at midday.

### Skimo — 2014-07-19T10:52:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-134892>

Corn salad is the translation I have found for this green leaf : [http://store.underwoodgardens.com/C...nella-locusta/productinfo/V1055/#.U8ohcrGRMTA](http://store.underwoodgardens.com/Corn-Salad_-Lambs-Lettuce_Mache-Valerianella-locusta/productinfo/V1055/#.U8ohcrGRMTA)
It's my favourite and bought already clean and prepared in a package, it has a long lifetime in the fridge.
I intend to try to grow some in september... even if my attempt at growing veggies isn't so much of a success this year...

I have bought some goat and sheep yogurts to replace the soy ones I used to eat after reading about the soy restriction in the lepin reset protocol. I didn't see that they weren't allowed, are they?
I even researched about the restriction about dairy and cheese except french ones, and I was really curious why, I have dug out that french dairy cattle were selected for A2 genotype over A1 which is great. I can even ask a colleague who used to work for a cattle insemination center about it to be sure.

If you know where I can find inexpensive blue blocker glasses I am interested, I only could find some expensive ones and I was still pondering wether to buy filters for screens + blue blockers lights or even blue blocker prescription glasses when I have the funds... until then inexpensive one would be great to try!

Thank you Alex for your kind words!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-22T07:04:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-135167>

Passion cannot be faked or bought. Authenticity is the one human trait that is illogical because when it is felt and perceived as genuine, despite rhyme or reason, our gut seems to sense it before our brain does. If you don’t believe in what you say or live, people will perceive it before your thought is completed. **People do not care what you know, until they know how much you care. _Just love him for where he is now._**

When I get criticized for any reason, I have pre-decided to use the bricks thrown at me as cornerstone foundations to my core beliefs. When I burn bridges, I let those fires burn the path I am on. No matter the poor choices I have made, I am never too old, never too bad, never too late, and never too sick to start from scratch once again. I have learned that when I have stumbled, I was still moving forward. Was it ideal or optimal? No, it was not, but I learned a lot from it. I learn far more from failures than success and this is why I tell everyone embarking on change they must become comfortable with discomfort.

All great changes are preceded by chaos in my opinion. I have told my closest friends that I am a creature to evolved to love chaos. This is what forms me.............it teaches me and sculpts me away from ideas that are poor for me.

### Flora — 2014-07-23T16:16:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-135302>

Hi Skimo
Great to see you here - I too live in France (tho' am English).
If you haven't managed to find amber glasses yet, I bought a pair online here
[http://www.smaf-touseau.com/fr/search?motcle=LUNETTES+SUPER+FIT+TEINTEES+ORANGE](http://www.smaf-touseau.com/fr/search?motcle=LUNETTES+SUPER+FIT+TEINTEES+ORANGE)+
They are good quality, although not quite as dark a colour as the Uvex ones from the USA (which I got my son to send me from there as Amazon won't deliver them to France). They are 20 EUR.

I felt just the same about the weightloss too - couldn't wait for something to happen! But as time has gone on and I started to see other wins with my health it has become less and less of an issue and, amazingly, in the past couple of weeks I have started to get smaller! Not by that much and I don't know the amount as DH threw away the scales! but all my clothes are getting looser now and I've had to buy some in a smaller size - yay!
But even better than that are all the other benefits of living epi-paleo and following this amazing site. I don't post much but I read and listen to everything avidly and am inspired every day to focus and move forward.
I too have hypothyroid issues, although I don't have any prescription, but on my latest labs I saw improvements - so baby steps...
I have been really tight on wearing the amber glasses and cutting blue light for the past year and my sleep has improved enormously - I really feel that's an important part to work on - makes a huge difference.

Anyway you seem to be doing great so _bon courage!_

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-28T14:51:23+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-135833>

When you sleep well optimal is yours for the taking. That is the story of OSF 7.

### Inger — 2014-07-29T22:40:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-135949>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=135296)

> I would not weigh myself......all it does is release more cortisol

yeah...lol that s why I never weigh myself anymore! No thanks

what can help before we get skinny (or it helps me..) is starting to love the curves.. and instead of wanting to get skinny fast just learning to carry the body better (maybe do some yoga or dancing or stretching or whatever, play in the water...) and that alone makes one more attractive! because it is not about the weight, believe me!
I am not overweight but there are days i still can have some body image issues (when i am stressed in particular) and i need to talk myself out of them. I always think about women i have known that were not skinny by far but that i found really attractive... it helps a lot

Thank you for your support!

I have been quite tired for two days, but now I feel better... I also have received my blue blocker glasses. DBF tried his and removed them after 5 minutes... the one I chose aren't a perfect fit over glasses, but it isn't so bad... Let's see how protecting my eyes in front of my computer will help...

### Skimo — 2015-02-04T15:06:17+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/on-a-quest.11346/post-154713>

I am still doing my best to follow the Epi-Paleo diet and I am down to 236,5lbs today.

Yesterday I did a blood test.
Triglycerides 1.37 g/L (<1.5)
Cholesterol 2.1 g/L (<2)
LDL 0.62 g/L
HDL 1.21 g/L

Glycemia 0.89 g/L (0.74 to 1.06)

TSH 1,71 (0.4 to 3.6)
T4 21.71 (<21)

Sedimentation rate 46mm (<10)
CRP 47,2mg/L (<5)

I have a history of high CRP/SR. To explore it, my MD had already me do :
\- a protein electrophoresis > non specific hypergammaglobulinemia
\- intradermal tuberculination test > positive
\- thorax xrays > negative
\- gamma interferons > negative (so tb was excluded as a potential diagnosis)
\- abdominal ultrasounds > ok

and sent me to an hospital specialist would told me my chronic fatigue was in my head... and not to worry about my high CRP (jerk...)

I am treated for hypothyroidism which explains the high T4, everything else was normal.

I am doing my best to stay Epi-paleo and I have cut down any pro-inflammatory veggie/berry... I really hoped it would help to reduce my CRP/SR...

I was a little down yesterday afternoon, wondering if it wasn't the beginning of a cold that would explain the inflammation markers... but I feel better today even if I didn't sleep so well this night... stress and worries aren't good for me...

Any ideas?
I may ask my MD to retest CRP/SR in one month just in case...

# Thread: Are we adaptable?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-07-18T03:09:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/post-134773>

Do we know how to adapt? Do you know how to adapt?

Is this something that is innate in us? or do we have to learn how to?

Is this just being really conscious? and going with the flow of our life? if we are paying attention?

I am finding that quietly paying attention and observing and just being - is bringing me far more than I ever thought possible.

I keep using the word intuition lately ....... but I think it is more that I am in a state of being and accepting and paying attention....

and then there is the oysters!!!!!

what say you?

### JasonB — 2014-07-18T19:32:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/post-134811>

If you don't adapt, you get stressed and then you get sick.

### sjoshua — 2014-07-21T22:10:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/post-135112>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=134773)
>
> Is this something that is innate in us? or do we have to learn how to?

Yes, but also yes... since we are good at forgetting. Err, moreso stopped following nature's instruction manual since human's decided we could 'do it better' or something

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=134773)
>
> Is this just being really conscious? and going with the flow of our life? if we are paying attention?

Being really conscious = paying better attention irregardless of desire

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=134773)
>
> I am finding that quietly paying attention and observing and just being - is bringing me far more than I ever thought possible.
>
> I keep using the word intuition lately ....... but I think it is more that I am in a state of being and accepting and paying attention....
>
> and then there is the oysters!!!!!
>
> what say you?

Don't think, do. Flow cannot happen when you are making a decision!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-23T05:48:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/post-135249>

Our brain is wired to pay attention to the more negative things.....by design. Once you realize this design limit you can teach yourself to focus on your attitude to change your paradigm.

### caroline — 2014-07-23T07:40:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-we-adaptable.11347/post-135271>

I loved the article you linked to about "don't give a shit" this hit me between the eyes in such a profound way.

I spend way too much time thinking about why something happened - if I have a plan of action - then fine - go for it ...if not - I have to say "I don't give a shit" and move on...

I take my power back......

This is just another way of looking at what you told me in Fla.

These are two awesome phrases that say everything in very simple and direct terms ..... impossible to mis construe by anyone.

I am discovering that being direct and to the point is everything ..... saves so much time ....and there is no misunderstanding about what I mean.

# Thread: Casey's Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caseys-journal.11350/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caseyjmc — 2014-07-18T09:27:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caseys-journal.11350/post-134792>

I am 59 but feel more like 40. My wife and I run a home based company that sells one product along with its accessories. We've been married 31 years and have a joy-filled life. We have 2 adult married sons and three grandchildren who live a few blocks from us. We see them during the week and enjoy every minute.

I've spent a lifetime trying to lose weight. At times hugely successful but always started eating poorly and gained it back. 3 years ago I had some kind of epiphany and began eating smaller portions of everything. I went from 235lbs to 170 in a little over a year; then got into the sugar again and gained 15lbs. I started doing low-carb but the weight kept creeping up. At 190lbs I started LCHF according to The Diet Doctor [www.dietdoctor.com](http://www.dietdoctor.com), but the weight kept rising. 8 months into LCHF, some friends who are following Haylie Pomroy's Fast Metabolism Diet, lost 115lbs between them in 8 months. My wife and I decided to join them to see what would happen. In six weeks, my wife has lost 10 lbs. I've lost nothing. So I started trying to find out why, and I ran into The Leptin Diet.

I do not eat sugar or refined carbs. I do not eat fake foods, nor diet foods. I follow the plan I'm following to the T, so I have been frustrated that I didn't lose on Low Carb, LCHF (keto), nor the Fast Metabolism Diet. I drink plenty of water, I sleep well (only need 6-7 hours per night).

Enter the Leptin Diet. There seem to be many different versions. All of them seem to agree with one basic idea—BAB with 50-75 g. of protein. Not certain as to which one to follow, I began with BAB. 4 days later I had dropped 4 lbs. A week later I am up a lb.

I honestly don't know if I have the stamina to follow this plan, but I am going to take your advice and start with baby steps. I am weary of limiting my carbs to <25 per day. It didn't work before, but I'm open to the possibility that I wasn't eating enough protein (in fact, I was guided to reduce protein since it turns to glucose when there is too much, and ends up as if I had been eating carbs, so they say!)

Anyway, that's where I'm at right now. Thanks to all of you who respond to my questions and offer me encouragement.

### bionaut — 2014-07-18T13:51:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caseys-journal.11350/post-134799>

Do you get sleepy at sunset and getup at dawn feeling refreshed? Does your room have any lights or led's and do you have any electrical equipment in your proximity?

A leptin reset will stimulate autophagy (pruning of bad mitochondria). This takes time. I have been on the reset myself for about 6 weeks. Today was the first day I woke up full of energy. It takes a while but your sleep effieciency and circadian timing needs to improve before exercise and diet will help you. You have come to the right place for help but expect it to be a weight loss over the long haul.

As for the stamina to continue, eating that BAB consistently at the "same time" will reign in your neuropeptides in your brain. This will boost your stamina. You just need to stay the course.

### caseyjmc — 2014-07-18T16:21:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/caseys-journal.11350/post-134801>

> [bionaut said:](/goto/post?id=134799)
>
> Do you get sleepy at sunset and getup at dawn feeling refreshed? Does your room have any lights or led's and do you have any electrical equipment in your proximity?
>
> A leptin reset will stimulate autophagy (pruning of bad mitochondria). This takes time. I have been on the reset myself for about 6 weeks. Today was the first day I woke up full of energy. It takes a while but your sleep effieciency and circadian timing needs to improve before exercise and diet will help you. You have come to the right place for help but expect it to be a weight loss over the long haul.
>
> As for the stamina to continue, eating that BAB consistently at the "same time" will reign in your neuropeptides in your brain. This will boost your stamina. You just need to stay the course.

Interesting! I have been nodding off at sunset, which right now is about 9:00 pm, and I have been waking between 5-6am. This is unprecedented for me.

I'll keep up BAB and work toward the rest, like getting my iphone and iPad out of the bedroom at night.

Thanks for your encouragement.

# Thread: Slim and LR questions
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slim-and-lr-questions.11352/>
**Subforum:** The Leptin Rx
**Posts:** 16
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Mr.Bliss — 2014-07-18T17:38:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slim-and-lr-questions.11352/post-134802>

Hello friends!

My name is Moritz, I'm from Germany and this is my first posting in this forum. (My questions are highlighted in red for a better overview)

I have read many of the blog entries and also Jack's book (good way to improve my English skills btw!) and several other paleo books and also 'Mastering Leptin' by B.J. Richards.

For the past 2 months I have done the Leptin RX now but I don't know if everything I do is correct and since I'm not trying to lose but gain weight I don't know exactly how to figure out that I'm on the right track or what are the signs for LS in underweight people.

For my BAB I use whey (Jack said it's ok for underweight people), ghee, coconut oil and berries.
In the Leptin FAQ post Jack's answers to the question:

**'How strict is the 50 grams of carbohydrates per day limit?**
It is strict for the overweight and not strict for the underweight.'

And Paul Jaminet says one should eat 150g of carbs a day. Can I do eat so many carbs as a skinny guy or does it change hormone patterns in a negative way that I can't gain weight and build muscles?

Jack also says 'If you're underweight, eat more hashbrowns cooked in pastured butter.' (The Leptin RX: FAQs) (but I thought potatoes should be eliminated in the Epi-Paleo diet? (Epi-Paleo RX page 239))

and

'The ability to eat some starches, however, possibily may be recovered once fasting blood glucose is consistently below 88.' (Epi-Paleo RX page 119)

So does this mean starches are safe for a healthy metabolism?
I don't know my fasting blood glucose but I have been following a ketogenic diet for 1 year. I also don't have carb cravings.
But I feel better with some starches.

I have a better sleep when I eat starches in the afternoon and also Paul Jaminet recommends to eat carbs later in the day.
Also on page 179f in 'Mastering Leptin' it says that carbs stimulate the parasympathetic nerves and increase serotonin production and depress NPY to induce food satisfaction.
And since serotonin is needed for melatonin production this explains why I feel so good with some starches at dinner.

But now I'm confused because in the FAQs Jack says:

'The most carbs should be eaten when cortisol is highest, in the morning. Dinner should be the meal with the least amount of carbs.'
That's the opposite recommendation. Or is it just for overweight but skinny people can eat starches later in the day? (Carb backloading anyone?)

Currently I eat 3 or 2 meals a day:

-   06:30 am: BAB shake (100g Whey, ghee, coconut oil, resistant starch, berries or piece of fruit) Should I add some starches like tapioka pearls to the shake?

-   12:00 Green stuff/salad with salmon

-   06:00 pm: starches and fat. Just little protein because I have better sleep if I don't eat so much protein at the end of the day. Protein makes me feel less energetic the next morning.

Occasionally I eat organ meats, bone broth, sea food and sea veggies and spirulina. Also I want to try out noni juice as a strong adaptogen but I don't know how to implement it into the Leptin diet since this juice contains lots of liquid sugar.

I still have some problems with energy levels and exercising drains me out too much and also my body doesn't really build muscles even with HIIT so I think something is still not working correctly in my body.

I'm planning to take the following supps.

•Fish oil, Vitamin D, K2, codliver oil

•Magnesium, potassium, zinc, selenium, Q10

•B vitamins: B1, B2, B3, biotin, folate and B12

•L-glutamine, effective microorganisms, curcumin

•antioxidants: vitamin C, red palm oil for vitamin E, gluthatione

•L-carnitine

I guess my mitochondria don't work perfectly so I want to give them all these nutrients from supplements. My mitochondria might suffer from too much nitrosative stress which could explain my energy level and inability to gain weight. NO-stress blocks mitochondrial energy production which leads to loss of muscle tissue so I will take all these antioxidants and vitamin B12 which reduces the NO-stress.

So I think these are my main problems: hormonal imbalances and mitochondria problems. Maybe leaky gut but I will also fix this.

And one question to cold adaption. I don't have a bath tub so are cold showers in the morning enough?

And which lab tests would you recommend for me? rT3, testosteron, groth hormone, pregnenolone, DHEA? Maybe I have problems with cortisol, too.

Ok that's all my questions for now.
Thanks guys and have a great day!
Mo

### caroline — 2014-07-19T02:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slim-and-lr-questions.11352/post-134845>

Hi Mo! wow - that is a lot of questions! so I will ask you some....

how is your non native EMF and blue lite situation? This will likely be your biggest problem

Do you drink R/O water?

Do you ground outside? barefoot? with as little clothes as possible?

Dr. kruse always tells us that we need to eat real food .... supplements in their natural package!

You needs tons of seafood and primarily oysters.....

Please remember that being overweight - or being anorexic is part of the same problem - just oppposite ends of the spectrum

Cold baths are optimal - but if all you have is a shower - go for it! Do you have an icevest?

You shouldn't be exercising right now.......

and

'The ability to eat some starches, however, possibily may be recovered once fasting blood glucose is consistently below 88.' (Epi-Paleo RX page 119)
Once you fix the circadian mismatch or redox shift (EE12) then your mitochondria function at cytochrome one can recover via autophagy (OSF 7) and you can resume a seasonal protocol for carbs.

I don't know my fasting blood glucose but I have been following a ketogenic diet for 1 year. I also don't have carb cravings.

Currently I eat 3 or 2 meals a day:

-   06:30 am: BAB shake (100g Whey, ghee, coconut oil, resistant starch, berries or piece of fruit) Should I add some starches like tapioka pearls to the shake?

I still have some problems with energy levels and exercising drains me out too much and also my body doesn't really build muscles even with HIIT so I think something is still not working correctly in my body.

•Fish oil, Vitamin D, K2, codliver oil

### caroline — 2014-07-20T01:06:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/slim-and-lr-questions.11352/post-134946>

Dr. K. suggests we buy bottled water .... apparently an R/O system for your house screws your plumbing.

Podcasts are listed on the home page ..... they are for everyone!

don't worry about spending money on stuff right now... there are lots of things you can do. A lot of us here don't have much money ...sad to say!

You need to get rid of wifi in your house. You need to get all electronics out off your bedroom and pull the breaker if you can.

Turn off all fake lite after sundow...

This is what your day should look like....

upon rising - go outside and look at the sun - barefoot and as little clothes as possible

have a BAB within 30 minutes of rising - following the Leptin reset using the epi-paleo template ..... leave at least 4 hours between meals and before bed - NO SNACKING!

drink R/O water, bottled spring water

limit non native EMF and bluelite .... you need blueblocker glasses

get outside as much as possible - barefoot

check out the easy start guides - especially C/T

as you can see - none of this costs money ...and will make a tremendous difference!

Yes I have read the leptin blogs and also the Epi-Paleo RX book but I can't find recommendations for the other two meals. I hope it's not annoying to you that I ask that but I simply can't find dinner recommendations.
The reason why I'm so concerned about carbs is because there is so much info about carbs and gutflora. I really get your point that carbs are not important for a healthy metabolism and hormonal system and the reason why people struggle with ketosis is a broken metabolism with LR and other issues.
But what about the gut flora? The gut bacteria can't do glyconeogenesis, right? Does it rely on some carbs as essential nutrients?
I've also read the book by Jan Kwasniewski who promotes a ketogenic diet. But in the following link Paul Jaminet writes about serious health issues people struggle with after following this diet for years:
[http://perfecthealthdiet.com/category/diets/optimal-diet/](http://perfecthealthdiet.com/category/diets/optimal-diet/)

And also the following link is really interesting. It says that carnivorous animals eat more carbs than most people think because very fresh raw meat contains lots of glycans which vanish after some hours of oxygen exposure. Also inuit eat more of these animal carbs and aren't constantly in a state of ketosis:

[http://freetheanimal.com/2014/03/disrupting-carbs-prebiotics.html](http://freetheanimal.com/2014/03/disrupting-carbs-prebiotics.html)

[http://freetheanimal.com/2014/03/disrupting-masai-carbs-prebiotics.html](http://freetheanimal.com/2014/03/disrupting-masai-carbs-prebiotics.html)

So I'm asking if a ketogenic diet is just important for the leptin reset because an ongoing ketosis for years and decades could harm the body. Please correct me if I'm wrong. I'm open-minded and don't want to follow a selective perception pattern so I include all the arguments from the ketosis promoting guys and also from the carb promoting guys.

Also inuit eat more of these animal carbs and aren't constantly in a state of ketosis:
>

There are many members on this forum that eat their food, including various organ and muscle meats, raw.

> [Mr.Bliss said:](/goto/post?id=135066)
>
> So I'm asking if a ketogenic diet is just important for the leptin reset because an ongoing ketosis for years and decades could harm the body. Please correct me if I'm wrong. I'm open-minded and don't want to follow a selective perception pattern so I include all the arguments from the ketosis promoting guys and also from the carb promoting guys.

I've posted it in other threads, but a good recommendation is to try and consider that essentially everything we as humans 'know' about health, diet, nutrition is based on flawed methodologies and missing information. Unless we are looking at the full picture of health and environment, then any diet can be good or bad... Its all about context. I've only been pursuing knowledge related to health for a few years now, so takes this for what its worth, but in my experience the other methodologies such as paleo, primal, slow-carb all have holes in their explanation of how and why their templates work. Jack has done a great job at filling in these holes in his blogs, and although there is still much to learn I have yet to notice any insufficiently explained aspects. Everyone has to find their own path though!

@[nonchalant](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/191/) summed up the answer to this quite well. Unless 'you' are in the exact same environment, occupying the same space (which is impossible, since adding mass to the same environment would have changed its impact on the surrounding contents...), eating the exact foods, and have had the exact timeline (bear in mind, all of these are impossible feats) then you cannot expect the same results as someone else.

### bionaut — 2014-07-18T19:07:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/some-dental-questions.11354/post-134809>

I am contemplating braces. I have this silly cavity in the back right of my mouth about the head of a pin, about a 1/4 cm and not very deep. As I learned transition metal chemistry I realized that getting this thing filled may not be a good idea and told my doc not to fill it. 2 years later, it looks the same.

So what I am wondering, I know it is possible to heal a cavity. Has anyone done it on the forums?
I am getting plenty of sun and following the protocols and seeing improvement in everything else. I am contemplating a mouth light or laser, either in the blue or red spectrum, coupled with CT to move things along.

Secondly, lets say I decide to have it filled. Are there safer filling compounds than your standard variety?

# Thread: Don't let the perfect be the enemy of the good
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 56
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### WereBear — 2014-07-18T19:58:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-134812>

That is one of my favorite Voltaire quotes, and it's been a good motto to encourage all kinds of ventures in my life.

Especially something as challenging as an Optimized Life.

Almost two weeks in, and I'm loving the seafood, have bought a can of oysters (will try that this weekend) and am drinking lots of cold water. I need to move up the pyramid and incorporate more crab, shrimp, and oysters. Found a local source for offal, so will explore that next week.

I ordered an EMF blocker for the bottom of my laptop. Just being cognizant of how much EMF there is around me has been very useful, I hope. I have my iPod touch in Airplane mode almost all the time now, got some sunglasses readers for reading in bed, and keep my cell phone far away if I'm not using it. This morning I had to make a customer service call where I was on hold, and I put it on speaker for the duration. Will be experimenting with better ways of handling it, since proximity is such an issue. I have hands free in the car, which is great, and I don't make a habit of talking while I'm driving, either. Parked is another matter

Now my lunch hour is taken in a nearby park, where I found a bench that has no wifi signals reaching it. I take off my shoes and have my feet in the grass the whole time, sitting in the sun.

After researching the earthing mats on the forum, I still went ahead and got a couple, we have thirty days to see if anything good happens, since my husband has a chronic illness and it's difficult for him to get down the three flights of stairs to get outdoors. We are trying to make that situation better, too, but in the meantime, it might help. And I'll try one at my desk at work.

We have winters that get down to -40. Won't be sitting in the park then, I'm sure.

### WereBear — 2014-07-19T01:49:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-134836>

The earthing mats I ordered have arrived, but I've done a lot more research in the interim... and from reactions here, seems like NOT a good idea at this point. I'm able to return them, and I'm flipped into pessimism about them.

On the other hand I've had oysters for dinner.

I need a recipe.

### caroline — 2014-07-19T03:31:18+0300

so - your DH isn't able to get out much? that must be very concerning...is there a move in your future?

you are doing great with the seafood!

### WereBear — 2014-07-22T01:57:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-135150>

We hope to move once our fledgling business starts rolling. In fact, my illness dropped me out of a business class we were taking to apply for a loan, but we kept the textbooks and made contacts, so we can pick up where we left off.

My research indicated amber lenses blocked the blue rays. So far so good, though I haven't given them much of a workout since I get sleepy shortly after sunset

### WereBear — 2014-07-29T03:07:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-135890>

My Schumann resonance generator arrived last Friday, and while I can't exactly _afford_ it, it fits in my budget far better than the magnetic mattress pads. I know the effects are subtle, but I basically added an hour to my sleep time since it arrived, and I feel muscles relaxing that really needed it. We'll see how things develop, but this is a move I feel very good about.

I discovered that making my oyster into Angels on Horseback (wrapped in bacon) works very well. Yum! Also have some very nice German prosciutto that I can wrap around oysters, too. I thought I'd be missing dairy, but I don't. Also found a recipe for making my own mayo with olive/hemp oil, and I feel well enough to implement that move. I think this will be a fine upgrade.

And now it's Tuesday, and I realized I hadn't thought about my bug bite in all that time. Because, except for a mild red mark, **it is gone**.

### WereBear — 2015-05-06T04:07:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-163417>

Looking back, I've been undercutting my sleep for _decades_:

-   my teens were high stress -- used to stay up late just to get quiet time for myself, then drag myself out of bed early for ungodly early high school
-   20's I spent commuting -- once again, up too early and went to bed too late in order to have my own time
-   30's I was coding and we all know the vampire hours that seems to encourage
-   40's I had another period of very high stress, college and work, and late nights studying

So the good sleep I've getting now is just amazing. It's like an exquisite meal or a wonderful movie. I wake up in the morning feeling rejuvenated!

I have four decades to catch up on

### WereBear — 2015-06-20T19:15:01+0300

However, I am bouncing back faster than I hoped, so it's just a case of Doing Too Much.

I've let some things slide, too, partly because I was exhausted, and partly because I wasn't sure they were working for me. Now I know I must keep up with:

-   Lipo-C
-   Skipping dinner unless I'm really hungry -- if the feeling passes, it can be ignored
-   Zero balancing (cancelled appointment because of time pressure -- should have kept it)
-   More grounding -- we've had a lot of rain

My bedtime is at 10pm so I need to eat by six at the latest since Dr. Kruse recommends a space of 4-5 hours before sleep. If I'm hungry then, and can't be distracted from it by a fascinating book or interesting project, then I know it will just get worse, so I eat then.

But hunger that comes to be later than six, I can take some niacin and vitamin c, and it goes away. Like my body was saying it just needed nutrients... not food

### WereBear — 2015-06-21T17:50:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-169168>

I had an eating disorder as a highly stressed teen, and part of my (self-engineered) recovery was to figure out when I was _really_ hungry. If we are not used to sorting out our body signals, so many things can get mis-interpreted as hunger. Some strategies:

-   Could be thirst. Drink a big glass of water.
-   Could be mineral deficiency. I used chromium picolinate for months, still take chelated magnesium, and use sea salt on my meat and spirulina in my morning smoothie.
-   Could be boredom. Do something fun!
-   Could be lack of pregnenolone (this has been a wonder supplement for me.)

I felt well enough to volunteer for a weekend away for work, and it was probably too much. I also went off my usual routines and found out just how much they do for me. So I wound up, at 2AM on the second night, gripped in an awful panic attack situation, with my mind conjuring up ridiculous scenarios of altered reality to explain why I felt like the next-to-last-teen in a slasher movie. Then I realized I had skipped my pregnenolone for the last two days! 75mg under the tongue, and five minutes later I felt the relief coming at me. Ten minutes later I was asleep! From a state where I was ready to rip out my own organs to _make it stop_.

Niacin and vitamin c are things I take all day, anyway. Dr. Abram Hoffer used high dose niacin for his PTSD patients. While not formally diagnosed, I think I qualify on the chronic stress front, and it does wonders for sleep!

All these strategies helped me realize, as dinnertime approached, that I wasn't really hungry, and I was turning into a dinner skipper, just like Dr. Kruse said I should if I work the protocols correctly. For me, it's having the Big Ass Breakfast, and a late Big Ass Lunch.

### WereBear — 2015-11-04T15:19:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-181136>

Okay, I'm back. Shingles is a hell of a detour.

Don't know what I did wrong, or if it was simply the YEARS of struggling that caught up with me under extra pressure. Our little business had gone LLC, and there was tons of paperwork and delays and then I was hit by a trooper car going way over the speed limit and more stress and money and constant dealing with the fallout like wearing sandspur socks.

The shingles derailed me for weeks, though I only took a week off when it first happened. Amazing thing: I was given gabapentin for the nerve pain, which is lingering. (Of course, of _course_.) But it seems to be "connecting some wiring" for me. A stubborn shoulder problem that occurred when I fixed my posture is responding to my movements again. Partner said the look in my eyes that says ME is back.

I seem to be handling the medication well and that is such a good thing because without it, the neuralgia comes back. So I'm on it for a while yet.

It was helpful to getting me back to 80% which is the best I have achieved, but then I found USP progesterone. Which is another story.

### caroline — 2015-11-29T09:48:29+0200

### WereBear — 2015-11-29T18:59:53+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-182838>

I have no local sources, the chain drugstores don't seem to carry it, though I understand GNC does. These chain drugstores carry wild yam tablets and such, and I always avoided them because they messed up my stomach so badly.

I started with ProGest, from Amazon, and then moved to ProgesterAll, from Dr. John Lee's site (he has passed on, but was the first doctor to recognize estrogen dominance.) The ProgesterAll has a much more silky feel and gets absorbed faster, and is appropriate for my face.

I have also been reading the ProgesteroneTherapy.com website, which advises a "loading" technique for faster results, where one uses lots at first. Which is where I'm at right now. She sells NatPro, which is more concentrated, and as soon as her warehouse is re-stocked, I'll try that, too.

I use a blob the size of two large grapes, twice a day, and more when it's stressful. And it seems to work very well!

It has done incredible things for my sleep. That wonderful tingly feeling I got from the Leptin Reset when I first woke up? Now it is intensified and it lasts for hours.

If I have a terrible bunch of frustrations to deal with, I add more cream and feel much better. I was always a cool head in a crisis, handling stress with belated anxiety perhaps, but totally able to cope. The hormone storms that messed me up so badly introduced me to the concept of "GET ME AN AXE" which was quite foreign to my personality.

With the cream, I'm back to easily managing low carb meals and can easily skip dinner. Maybe I can get rid of the 20 extra torso pounds that had refused to budge no matter what I did.

This might be the missing element I needed. I've had a severe case of endometriosis since puberty (sign of estrogen dominance, so I read) and so it's possible I never really had enough progesterone in the first place.

### WereBear — 2015-12-25T23:27:23+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-184954>

I just love the effects of progesterone. Dagnab! I'm two and a half months into using it.

I've eliminated my use of anti-inflammatories for sleep. I was using white willow bark & devil's claw, then I went to niacin, but since progesterone is supposed to suppress the inflammatory effects of estrogen, I dropped these supplements altogether.

The sleep I've been getting is now flatout incredible. On vacation this week, I'm racking up 10-11 hours a night. Of course I have a deficit. It is also of amazing quality, and I wake up with a "healing glow" that helps me feel so rejuvenated.

I think this might be my missing piece that will get me back to 100% of what I can be. This is also my 1 & 1/2 year anniversary of working on my Optimization, and I have made fantastic progress. I do think the progesterone is mopping up the last of the inflammation problems that had wreaked such havoc on my system.

It is especially welcome because I am working over one job when the organization pulled in a bigger territory and I have been juggling two, along with extra work and the stress. So if things calm down I might do even better.

### WereBear — 2016-04-08T15:51:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-192347>

Well, things haven't calmed down that much. Why do I always think they will? I guess hope is that thing without feathers.

However, this progesterone immersion seems to have paid off. I found a great company, Ora's Naturals, and I get 10%. This is a financial boon and I have also cut down on my dose, because I'm actually feeling better, sleeping great, and seeing how I do with a small maintenance dose.

Started drinking apple cider vinegar diluted in water with all my meals, for the "natural"Metformin effect."

I'm also trying Maca, which has all kinds of confusing information about it out there. But I'm convinced a lot of the confusion out there regarding Adrenal Fatigue is that their true problem is Cortisol Resistance, which was the key to my getting better, and starting the Kruse Protocols. Nothing is going to get better until cortisol actually gets into the cells.

Maca is variously described as a stimulant, a "toner," and an adaptogen. So I'll see how it works for me.

Financial crunch means I'm weeding out my supplements and focusing on ones with a clear benefit I can _feel_.

Doing more walks as the days have been getting longer, and getting my morning light on my face as I walk to work. Sardine and pickle lunches! Cheap and go together wonderfully.

### WereBear — 2016-08-18T16:11:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-199767>

I have been optimizing as best I can, and I improve, and then I hit a wall. I have a few key supplements that are doing wonders, but I have hit a wall. I have great sleep now, but that also has hit a wall in how much it lets me recover. I even went to therapy, cleaned up some sources of stress, and then I hit a wall again.

Recently, I learned the _name_ of the wall. It is undiagnosed **Asperger's Syndrome**.

When the flow of strangers through my office receded, so did my condition. When the demands of my job pushed me out among lots of people and noise and activity, upping my sensory input, so did my condition worsen. Asperger's was the missing planet that influences the orbits of the entire solar system of my health.

I am in the process of getting a formal diagnosis, which is itself a source of stress. Because I live in a remote area, I had to drive four hours there, recover in a motel room, take the all day assessment tests, recover in a motel room, and then drive back the third day through a driving rainstorm at seventy miles an hour, which wound up taking six hours.

By the next weekend, I was so drained that simply trying to go out to breakfast (I am so good at my job that our first two choices were packed ) I had what is called a **shutdown**, where my brain _simply stopped working_. I was too dizzy to stand up, everything was too loud and bright, it wasn't low blood sugar (though it felt like it) because eating some fruit didn't help, and I had to go home and lie on my bed under a pile of quilts with a pillow over my head for three hours to recover.

Not eager to trip _that_ particular set of events again, I can tell you. Home sensory deprivation chambers are very expensive  and my makeshift one is kind of cumbersome

Personally, I have no problem being an Aspie. I'm a writer and explainer and I had no idea social skills, which came to me in high school, were not the instincts of the Neurotypical, but instead a series of calculations and instructions (much like running a computer program, which is what I used to write) that took up brainpower. I figured it out so well no one could tell, but I also painted myself into a corner at getting so good at social my job pushed me into _more and more_ social, which eventually overwhelmed me.

It is rather common for High Functioning Aspies to not know until midlife, since the diagnostic criteria didn't get out into medical science until 1994, when a lot of us had already passed through the educational system. By this time, we have a enormous number of coping strategies, but this takes a lot of bandwidth. Any ebbing of energy (in my case, the Menopause from Hell,) and this elaborate structure starts to crumble. I need to shore it back up again. Getting a diagnosis means my insurance _should_ pay for a weighted blanket, targeted Cognitive Behavioral Therapy, and I'm also trying to get some massage therapy prescribed for me. I must talk to my boss about ajusting what is expected of me. All of these should help a lot, now that I know.

Most of all; _it explains so much_.

### Debntx — 2017-03-22T16:13:22+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-210884>

hi, just wanted to chime in on the weighted blanket thing - which i didn't even know existed.
I found out through my youngest child (now 20) who was diagnosed autistic later in life - anyway, i bought one for myself recently - less than 2 weeks ago - immediately made a difference that couldn't be explained any other way- wow, i sleep much better - it's weird how something so simple made a huge difference for me. i didn't study why - maybe later

thank you for validating my thinking - it's not just my imagination - i can trust my experience but i like hearing it from others as well - in regards to weighted blanket.

i just want to add about my office situation - i work in a call center and during the first part of day - people all around me in my small group- talking talking talking very distracting for me - then they leave (they're great people by the way) - but i feel so much better... feels/sounds like cacaphony... wildly overwhelming - then they leave - much better -  strangely i do put on a fan for the noise - but people sounds nah uh (i actually do quite fine)
because my job is on the phone all day talking to people delivering news they may not like (delays in service) - that takes a lot of mental energy - harder to be available for my coworkers too for chitchat but i am trying to be better at it - makes the day go better when i can work it in

i potentially could be in that category not diagnosed but "realizing" later in life- don't care if insurance pays for anything for me - the less information i give them or use the better i am - just the way i see it

at work they see me as "different" - made worse by my new "habits" (remove shoes when outside - get more sun - eat outside - wear long sleeves especially for inside, mostly small things etc...) - - but they probably blame it on age

good luck with everything - i know it's a challenge - it was nice to read about your experiences some of which I can really relate to -

Did you notice a difference using the weighted blanket? quality of sleep?

### Sue-UK — 2017-03-24T13:50:52+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-210994>

> [Debntx said:](/goto/post?id=210884)
>
> hi, just wanted to chime in on the weighted blanket thing - which i didn't even know existed.
> I found out through my youngest child (now 20) who was diagnosed autistic later in life - anyway, i bought one for myself recently - less than 2 weeks ago - immediately made a difference that couldn't be explained any other way- wow, i sleep much better - it's weird how something so simple made a huge difference for me. i didn't study why - maybe later
>
> thank you for validating my thinking - it's not just my imagination - i can trust my experience but i like hearing it from others as well - in regards to weighted blanket.

I've been fascinated by the senses since DS2 started developing sensory problems at about 2. I've learned from Jack to relate it now to the mitochondria, the hydrogen bonding network, light water and magnetism. I think there's a lot more senses than those we typically think of - including the sense of where we are in time and space, and gravitation sensing. Then I think a sense can by "typical" or hyper or hyposensitive. So for example one person may need a stronger stimulus like a bear hug, another would find that painful. So a weighted blanket could be working on one or several hyper or hyposensitive senses. For example, one possibility is that it could subconsciously make someone feel safer, better grounded, more aware of where they are in time and space, when the visual sense light stimulus relied on in the day is missing. Feeling safer = better sleep.

since i got the magnetico my sleep improved a lot then i did raise my visco foam mattress at the head by placing some buckwheat pillows to raise it a few inches - improvement there as well , then add the blanket - took my sleep to another level all together.

### JanSz — 2017-03-24T17:33:08+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dont-let-the-perfect-be-the-enemy-of-the-good.11355/post-211012>

When I see you guys discussing weighted blankets,
that reminds me discussion long time ago.
Likely started by @[Jack Kruse](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1031/) , related to birth via C-section.
Or very quick (easy) vaginal birth.

There is a need for the baby to go thru the squeezing.
When that is missing problems may arise in the future.

In that context, there I recall example that is some cases veterinarians keep colts within a tight net for some time after they are born.

..

# Thread: Yet another misfolding proteins condition: Pre-eclampsia
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Gracelind — 2014-07-18T21:15:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-134820>

[https://www.sciencenews.org/article/pregnancy-disorder-shares-aspects-alzheimer’s](https://www.sciencenews.org/article/pregnancy-disorder-shares-aspects-alzheimer%E2%80%99s)
**Misfolded proteins found in urine of women with preeclampsia**
Preeclampsia, a life-threatening condition that strikes during pregnancy, may have a lot in common with Alzheimer’s and mad cow diseases, a new study suggests.

[http://stm.sciencemag.org/content/6/245/245ra92](http://stm.sciencemag.org/content/6/245/245ra92)

"The presence of β-amyloid aggregates in placentas of women with preeclampsia and fetal growth restriction further supports the notion that this condition should join the growing list of protein conformational disorders."

Researchers have noted the similarities between Alzheimer's and Inclusion Body Myositis in the characteristics of β-amyloid, β-secretases BACE1 and BACE2, and misfolded proteins. People with IBM hope that AD research will help them, too, even with the difference in sites of β-amyloid deposition (vacuoles in muscle vs brain). Now with another condition to study, a breakthrough in treatment for one condition may provide hope for the others.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-18T21:17:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-134822>

OSF 3-5 lays it out.

### Gracelind — 2014-07-19T00:54:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-134827>

Thanks, Jack.

Time for a revisit to earlier OSF blogs. Once a series has been well unfolded it's time well-spent to do a review to weave the strands together. I always see something new and find important concepts reinforced on re-readings.

The abstracts didn't mention prions re: pre-eclampsia but there's an implication in the reference to Mad Cow Disease.

My twin nephews delivered weeks early due to increasing severe pre-eclampsia in their mother. They shared an amniotic sac and one was starting to steal nutrition from the other. Major stress for mother and fetuses. They're all healthy as can be now, but they got pulled through a mighty tight knothole during those weeks.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-19T02:07:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-134839>

prion diseases are also protein mis folding diseases........its the quantum mechanism at play of evolution.

> Once a series has been well unfolded it's time well-spent to do a review to weave the strands together. I always see something new and find important concepts reinforced on re-readings.

Well said, Grace.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-21T17:50:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-135087>

I think Grace's disease neuro-degeneration and pre eclampsia all share a common tie.......when youre missing electrons your proteins change size and shape and disease emerges.

### Gracelind — 2014-07-23T05:06:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-135245>

My disease is clearly autoimmune. In a vault somewhere are color slides and electron microscope photos showing the attack of the antibody cells (stained purple) and the muscle fibers (stained pink) that are being invaded. I still don't know why one bilateral set of muscles is attacked and another isn't--except that clearly conditions are the same within each pair. By this I mean, for example that neck flexors on both sides were early targets and are nearly gone yet neck extensors are still strong.

Could it be that the cell membrane instability (detected in my EMG) is a result of lack of the right kind of lipids (I ate little fish) or the misfolding of proteins either in the membranes or within cell structure? Maybe both? Back in 2002 one of the med school fellows explained to me a theory that as proteins become misfolded they are no longer able to be released from the cell so waste builds up inside. Cells can no longer function properly. He, of course, was looking at the biology level and not down at the electron level.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-23T05:42:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-135248>

WNT signaling and CM lipid chemistry are where the key to your disease lies in my humble opinion. And WNt signaling has 3 paths and one specifically is tied to your illness. Know that DHA helps that pathway immensely.

### nonchalant — 2014-07-23T16:50:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-135305>

> Back in 2002 one of the med school fellows explained to me a theory that as proteins become misfolded they are no longer able to be released from the cell so waste builds up inside. Cells can no longer function properly. He, of course, was looking at the biology level and not down at the electron level.

Sounds like another definition of poor autophagy.

### Jason Coates — 2016-10-06T18:07:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-202397>

### Jack Kruse — 2016-10-06T19:52:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/yet-another-misfolding-proteins-condition-pre-eclampsia.11356/post-202409>

### CK in LA — 2014-07-22T01:46:18+0300

(never had that), a lowering of mental energy, and irritable. I am bummed by the resistance I encounter every time I try to convert to this concept, and yet I want to be one of those who will one day be able to say, "it worked for me too." Will study and learn more in order to work through this involuntary resistance.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-22T06:59:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ck-in-la.11357/post-135166>

You default to what you know and what you are now. When you live with what you are you can never get to what you might be. If you want to get to where you want to be you must innovate your choices. I do not sugar coat things here for people because I am interested in results and not mental masturbation. You must own 100 percent of your focus when you consider change. Talent does not separate people, endurance does. Too often people just quit when the situation becomes tough. Ironically, they forget that endurance leads us to success in our endeavors. Patience is the antidote of ego in times of trial. Every violent storm gives way to sunlight, and every dark evening fades to dawn. Do you wait for it, or seek to circumvent things? Bravado may stir the crowd, but courage needs no audience. Some days you will feel no song in your heart, but what stops you from singing? People who have been to hell and back seem to know that a hunger for a taste of the future returns for all those who overcome difficulty. It's not about what you're capable of, it's about what you are willing to endure. Today, realize the sky is not your limit.......**_you are.
_**
_**The most useful piece of learning for the uses of life is to unlearn what is untrue.
**_
It is not about what you feel or what you think you need it is about what you have to do **_when you begin._**
In every end, there is also a beginning. When you begin to change you must be willing to be a beginner every single morning.

Too often, we and the people around us, become creatures to how we think chronically, without ever realizing it. This thinking is what creates ruts and plateaus in our life. Change is best carried out, not when we are in a rut, but when we are at the **edge of our comfort zone**. Even our friends and family can be enablers to our bad habitual thinking about change. People who lack the clarity, courage, or determination to follow their own dreams will often find ways to discourage yours. **When you change for the better, the people around you will be inspired to change also. But only after doing their best to make you stop**. Live your truth and don’t ever stop! I tell you this now so you read it and understand it consciously, because this unconscious thought is behind why you do not appear to want change. Once you perceive that this might be a correct statement, you will then begin to look at your circumstances from a new perspective. That new perspective is critical in seeing your life in a new way.

You must break free of the shackles of your old mind and embrace all your fears. On the other side of all your fears is the freedom in your life to make choices to give you an Optimal Life.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-22T07:08:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ck-in-la.11357/post-135168>

Life is lived out of control. It is insecure, selfish, impatient, and filled with mistakes. But we're addicted to that narrative. We're all out of control at times and hard to handle. But do you love life and people? If you do, you have a chance in this world. What the eyes are blind too, the heart can't touch. Touch has a memory. Do you touch others daily? If not why not? Sometimes, reaching out and taking someone's hand is the beginning of a journey. At other times, it is allowing another to take yours. How it unfolds is your story to write.

### Celeste — 2014-07-22T17:04:38+0300

Curious if when we get to these places, at the edge of our comfort zone, our bodies' responses, something I've always thought was my body trying to tell me something, but might be a way our body might be colluding with our mental resistance?

### SeaHorse — 2014-07-22T17:52:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ck-in-la.11357/post-135193>

> [Celeste said:](/goto/post?id=135190)
>
> Curious if when we get to these places, at the edge of our comfort zone, our bodies' responses, something I've always thought was my body trying to tell me something, but might be a way our body might be colluding with our mental resistance?

I think CT is a great example of this….at first you may experience the shock of cold as a "get out before it's too late" signal and then other systems kick in and you find a different sense of being…which doesn't always mean comfortable, but with CT it can start to feel great over time….so then you have a smaller and smaller resistance to the jump in temperature and a greater and greater pull towards that blast through your comfort zone. And then, of course, you're in new territory and start exploring again.

It's so interesting to develop this "listening" to your body because the _status quo_ messages and the _deep impulse to find a better way of being_ messages can be talking to you at the same time….I believe that as you become healthier the messages get more distinct and discernible and the status quo starts to lose out.

### Josh — 2014-07-22T20:04:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ck-in-la.11357/post-135207>

Once upon a time in a land not so far away.....

Once, when Vishtaspa, king of Persia, was returning from a victorious campaign, he came near to the place where Zarathustra lived. He decided to visit the mystic. The king said to Zarathustra. "I have come that you may explain to me the laws of nature and the universe. I cannot tarry as I am on my way home from a war and important matters of state await me at my palace."
Looking at the king, Zarathustra smiled and took a grain of wheat from the earth and gave it to him. "In this small grain of wheat," he declared, "are contained all the laws of the universe and the forces of nature
The king was much astonished by this answer, which he didn't understand, and when he saw smiles on the faces of those around him, he was angry and threw the grain upon the ground thinking that he was being mocked. And to Zarathustra he said, "I was foolish to waste my time by coming here to see you."
Years passed. The king was successful as a ruler and a warrior, and led a life of luxury and apparent contentment, but at night when he went to bed, strange thoughts came into his mind and troubled him: "I live in luxury and abundance in this splendid palace, but how long shall I enjoy this -- this abundance, this power, this wealth -- and what will happen to me when I die? Can my power and my riches save me from illness and death? Is everything lost with the coming of death?"
No one in the palace could answer these questions, but meanwhile, the fame of Zarathustra grew. So putting aside his pride, he dispatched a great caravan of treasure to Zarathustra and with it an invitation and a request. "I regret." he wrote, "that when I was impatient and thoughtless in my youth, I asked you to explain the great problems of existence in a few minutes of time. I have changed and do not want the impossible now, but I am still deeply interested to know the laws of the universe and the forces of nature, even more so than when I was a young man. Come to my palace. I pray you. Or if that is not possible, then send to me the best of your disciples that he may teach me all that he can about these questions."
After an interval, the caravan and the messengers returned. These told the king that they had found Zarathustra, who sent him greeting, but returned the proffered treasure. The treasure, Zarathustra had said, was of no use to him because he has attained to the ultimate treasure. Moreover, Zarathustra had sent the king a gift wrapped in a leaf and had asked the messengers to tell him that this was the teacher who could teach him everything.
The king opened it and found the same grain again -- the same grain of wheat that Zarathustra had given him before. He thought there must be something mysterious and magical in it, so he put it in a golden box and hid it among his treasures. Almost every day he looked at it expecting some miracle to happen, such as the turning of the grain of wheat into something or someone that would teach him all he wished to know.
Months went by, and then years, but nothing happened. At last the king lost patience and said. "It seems that Zarathustra has deceived me again. Either he is making a mock of me or else he does not know the answers to my questions, but I will show him that I can find the answers without his help." So the king sent a caravan to a great Indian mystic, Tshengregacha, to whom came disciples from all over the world, and with the caravan went the same messengers and the same treasure that he had once sent to Zarathustra.
After many months, the messengers returned from India with the philosopher, but the philosopher said to him, "I am honored to be your teacher but in
frankness must tell you that I come chiefly to your country that I may meet the great Zarathustra."
Then the king took the golden box containing the grain of wheat and answered, "I asked Zarathustra to teach me. See, this is what he sent me. Here is the teacher who shall teach me the Laws of the universe and the forces of nature. Is this not ridiculous?"
The philosopher looked long at the grain of wheat, and silence fell upon the palace while he meditated. At length he said, "I do not regret my many months of journeying, for now I know that Zarathustra is in truth the great teacher that I have long believed him to be. This tiny grain of wheat can indeed teach us the laws of the universe and the forces of nature, for it contains them in itself right now. You must not keep the grain of wheat in its golden box. You are missing the whole point.
"If you plant this little grain in the earth, where it belongs, in contact with the soil, the rain, the air, the sunshine, and the light of the moon and the stars, then like a universe in itself it will begin to grow bigger and bigger. Likewise you, if you would grow in knowledge and understanding, must leave your artificial life and go where you will be close to all the forces of nature and of the universe, to the sum total of things. Just as inexhaustible sources of energy are ever flowing towards the grain planted in the earth, so will innumerable sources of knowledge open and flow towards you till you become one with nature and the organic universe. If you watch the growth of this seed of grain, you will find that there is an indestructible and mysterious power in it -- the power of life. The grain disappears, and in that disappearance there is victory over death."
"All that you say is true," answered the king, "yet in the end the plant will wither and die and will be dissolved into the earth."
"But not," said the philosopher, "until it has done an act of creation and has turned itself into hundreds of grains, each like the first. The tiny grain disappeared as it grew into a plant, and you too as you grow must turn yourself into something and someone else. Life always create more life, truth more abundant truth, the seed more abundant seeds. The only art one needs to know is the art how to die. Then one is reborn. I propose that we journey to Zarathustra himself that he may teach us more of these things."
In a few days, they came to the garden of Zarathustra. His only book was the great book of nature, and he taught his disciples to read in it. The two visitors learned another great truth in Zarathustra's garden that life and work. Leisure and study, are one and the same: that the right way to live is a simple, natural life -- a creative life within which individual growth is a single, total dynamism. They spent a year in the garden, learning to read the laws of existence and of life from the vast book of nature. At the end of that time, the king returned to his own city and asked Zarathustra to set out systematically the essence of his great teaching. Zarathustra did so, and the result was the great book of the Zend-Avesta, the great book of the Parsis.

### CK in LA — 2014-07-24T04:19:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ck-in-la.11357/post-135369>

The book, Epi-Paleo Rx arrived this afternoon and I began reading it. For the last two days, I researched leptin resistance and familiarized myself with the subject. Now, I am ready to delve deeper and with a little less resistance. It will be interesting to see if, as the resistance to this new lifestyle/transformation decreases, the leptin resistance does too. In any event, yesterday's breakfast was still from the old menu with modification. . .I had greek 0% fage, raspberries, whey, stevia and a tintsy wintsy bit of splenda (I know its terrible! it is due to my addiction to sweets, but hope to phase it out) in a smoothie. Lunch was 8 oysters and a plate of ceviche but I still nibbled on about 1 oz of almonds and on a malt ball. For dinner: made my own talapia ceviche and had fermented veggies with it, but I still had an apple, and about an ounze of 85%. dark chocolate.

Today, breakfast: was still the smoothie. Lunch: a generous serving of ceviche with a scrambled egg, and half an avocado. Took a short nap and when I woke up had cravings, so I ate a medium sized apple. Hate to admit this but my Calorie Diary App is set for an 850 calorie intake per day, and so far today, I am over by -88 calories and I did not have dinner yet. On the one hand, I am trying to let go of diet mentality and integrate Jack's (will quote to do no harm) "Dont worry about the amount you eat.....just eat a lot of protein and fat. The rest happens naturally if you stop over thinking it." On the other, I cannot let go and trust that my body will lose weight. I have not let go of sugar (fruit, and artificial/deadly sweetner) and my fear that if I eat fat (coconut oil) I will not fare like others and therefore I take only a teaspoon per meal. I do not know how to do it any other way. I did rub the coconut oil on my face though.

My belly fat and hips are very upsetting to me. When I am thin its less of a source of shame. I weigh 135 lbs (last I weighed a few days ago) and should be in the very low teens or under to feel good. My history is complicated around all of this and I will not go into it. This entry is to address the conditioning that is interfering with further progress, it is not meant to be a report, or a way of sharing myself. I can think of better things to say, but I am interested in addressing where I am undeveloped and wish to expand from a limitation that hampers me terribly. I feel that honesty and humility are indispensable to growth and change. With love CK

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-24T04:22:47+0300

### Ksilva — 2014-07-19T04:15:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-lyme-disease-and-weight-problem.11358/post-134859>

I signed up for the forum here after reading an article on Lyme and leptin resistance. I would like help, because I have been gaining weight nonstop for over one year straight no matter what I do. I have tried low carb, cutting out sugar gluten soy dairy, many supplements...nothing. Eating less just tells my metabolism "alright, less food, slow down, no need to release this 35 lbs of fat or anything". I am so sad...doctors tell me diet and exercise as if I am literally retarded. Please can someone help?

I was also diagnosed with PCOS two months ago, and Lyme one month ago. Never weight problem in my life before this. Now I cannot even walk.

Have you read the easy start guides on the home page? the leptin reset? etc.

Do you have labs? there is a lab section to post them.

Have you listened to Dr. K's podcasts? they are listed on the home page. His last one was great - succinct and to the point about mitochondria.

Just holler if you need help!

Please post a human pic .... Dr. K's new rule ...no pic- no Jack. He likes to see who he is talking to - as do we all

### Lahelada — 2014-07-19T22:59:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hi-lyme-disease-and-weight-problem.11358/post-134940>

First of all no you dont sound like a lunatic. When I was reading your story I was thinking whatever happened to "Do no harm." Your docs probably meant well but alas it all made thjngs worse.
I would suggest to start reading here starting with the leptin reset and leaky gut. The antibiotics you are taking will do a number on your friendly gut population so think about adding probiotic foods. Also have a read of the Tensegrity blogs. You will find some interesting advice re chewing food,glycans etc. A lot of people here eat raw food to repopulate their gut. Dont worry if you cannot envisage this yet. Try slowly with additive free salami,raw ham and olives. If you can eat oysters raw preferrably but steamed or grilled is okay. If there is any superfood it is oysters closely followed by pork.
This should tide you over and hopefully get you feeling a bit better until OSF8 comes out.

Last but hugely important how is your EMF exposure ? Lots of electronic stuff constantly plugged in ? Lts of artificial light in nighttime hours? I am thinking about that with regards to improving your sleep.

I am not happy about the antibiotics, I wish there were another way. My gut is already in shambles. I am taking a probiotic twice a day to make up for it, totaling about 160 billion bacteria.

My light exposure is high but I have just ordered some amber glasses to block it out...they will arrive in probably ten days.

Okay, I am trying not to get overwhelmed as I always do now... So, I have made the journal... Now...

\- read easy start guide for leptin
\- read OSF 8 blog when it comes out
\- read leaky gut
\- read Tensegrity blogs
