Just like buying HiFi today... Streaming and connecting your gadgets is for the big herd but meantime the Audiophile world is taking two paths. One, digital with tons of DA converters of Nasa specs but also those who go vinyl with Class A amplifiers.

So many do like i do, purchase HiFi from the late 7oies or 80ies. Hunting pick-ups from that era to get a sound that was so much more analog that today.

I think this will apply to many other aspects of technology soon, we go back some steps and pick it up from there.

**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-07-26T15:08:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness.11398/post-135623>

I have been thinking about what this word actually means for a long time now .....I think I know.

Dr. K. has been using it a lot for quite awhile now......so while I think I know ...I am also thinking it means something more, something much deeper, something with many more layers than I imagine.

Tonite I ran smack into this word in 3D....

I went to a concert given by two young men. One played the guitar - a very handsome Italian man. He was very expressive and quite lovely.

But ..... it was the other young Man that completely and totally mesmerised me. I could not take my eyes off him! He took my breath away.

He is a tall, very lean Italian - totally bald with a very soft demeanor. He played an accordion of sorts - not something strapped to his chest .... but a small one that sat on his thigh - maybe a concertina? It is called a Bandoneon.

Their music has a lot of influence from Argentina - Buenos Aires in particular ...so think Tango etc.

These men were sitting. The one who played the Bandoneon was totally in motion - every part of him expressed the music - every single note. His lips, his nose, his eyebrows, his eyes, his neck, his shoulders and every other body part......even if you used your imagination - it would be hard to comprehend.

these two amazing musicians were also completely tuned in to each other - and the audience....

and so tonite - I think I got a real life demonstration and in living, breathing colour - of what it is to be conscious.

They displayed a hunger for life and a love for the tango ..... extra ordinary!

is that a synonym for being conscious? Is this what Dr. K. is talking about?

or - is there even more?

### Martin — 2014-07-26T15:14:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness.11398/post-135624>

I've noticed how many musicians appear to lose themselves in the moment. Sometimes I can get near to that point when I play and a little crack appears in my everyday existence.

### caroline — 2014-07-26T15:21:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness.11398/post-135625>

a little crack? I will be right over ......

going to bed now .... need my beauty sleep...talk soon.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-26T15:37:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness.11398/post-135626>

sounds more like coherence.

### caroline — 2014-07-27T01:22:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/consciousness.11398/post-135660>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=135626)
>
> sounds more like coherence.

so - I haven't got it right yet?

there was something else I forgot to mention...
These men where totally in black from head to toe...
The stage was completely black..

when they came on stage the tall, lean man had on white sneakers - I kept thinking how bizarre. The other man had on black dress shoes.

I soon came to realise that he was also doing a pantomine of the music.......his free leg was always in motion - in the air or off to the side of he would bend his leg way back and bring his knee to his chest..... so hence the white sneakers.

so he was expressing the music in several different ways - while he was playing the notes ....and mostly didn't take his eyes off the other musician who was also focused on him
.... and then there was a whole other level of audience participation!
It was a small group - about 100 I suppose. Italians are very expressive!

I walked out beside the Italian cousel general and I was beaming and so was he ...and he said it was wonderful wasn't it? I lamented the fact they spoke only in Italian ...and he said that is too bad.
I told him it was really okay - I would just make it up!

# Thread: The current biologic modeling in science is dangerous.
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-current-biologic-modeling-in-science-is-dangerous.11399/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-26T22:58:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-current-biologic-modeling-in-science-is-dangerous.11399/post-135655>

Models are useless but modern science continues to believe it is best science practice. You need to be aware of why it is not to evaluate the "said published science. The key problem with this is the use of 'modeling.' is it proves nothing. In other words, no one has proven anything untrue; they just have a model that suggests another possibility. Either possibility could be true. Actual tests would need to be done to be able to say what is true.

One of the many things wrong with modern science is this tendency to take a model as science, even models that are complete crap at predicting anything. It will only get worse with the rise of big data. This means the literature will become more useless as time goes on. Guyenet's brand of science is a perfect modern paleo example. What good is your literature if your targets are wrong? Who cares what happens in a mouse when youre studying obesity. A mouse is a nocturnal mammal that has very little in common with a human.

The accepted error rate in modeling is 30%. Did you know that? How good would I be inside a head if I had a 30% error rate. Ask yourself that the next time you base an opinion a paper based upon modeling. It is a poor use of science all the time because it shows nothing but other possible outcomes......not the one that matters.

The truth happens to individuals not to crowds.....This is another reason why evidence based population data is a hazard for an individual. Counterintuitive? Yep........but that is how nature is too.
Our greatest fear should not be of our failures but of succeeding at things in life that really do not matter.

A model can never fully replace reality......and when youre talking human health reality using a mouse rat or anything but a living human is bound to give you the WRONG ideas of what is correct for your health. The key is understanding fundamentally how things work. Biology believes this is a macro biochemistry story. The dogma says anything larger than atoms are too messy for us to deal with or understand. The interactions are too intricate and complex for scientist to be able to sort out the quantum signals of an object, chemical, or enzyme. So they ignore it. Conventional scientists believe today that the quantum-classical boundary can never be breached. Too bad they do not know that buckyball fullerene's have already been shown to be quantized and this molecules are massive compared to most biochemical substrates.
Biologists take cells apart to study them. It is a reductionist game or model they are using. It is fundamentally wrong. We need an integrative science, like QED. Studying dead cells can lead to a lot of good info but it also can not teach us about the quantum coherent processes that emerge only within the matrix of a living system. Here is my main point: Biology is not about applying quantum mechanics as it is already known through the experiments and experience of physics, but rather about an attempt to extend quantum mechanics in a manner that physicists have not yet tried. Nature contains many examples of these anomalies. That is the domain of quantum biology.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-26T22:59:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-current-biologic-modeling-in-science-is-dangerous.11399/post-135656>

Many scientists are strong reductionists who believe that biology or physics alone determines outcomes in the real world. This is demonstrably a falsehood – for example, the computer on which I am writing this could not possibly have come into being through the agency of physics or biology alone. That means there is a quantum biologic overlap. This is fundamentally what biology is missing due to their modeling beliefs. Physics has the exact same problem at the Hadron collider. The Standard Model of physics is under big attack now after 80 yrs.

Quantum biology will revolutionize medicine shortly. It will be biophysicists and bioenergenics scientists who lead the paradigm change. Condensed matter physics and quantum optics are where we need to concentrate first because it is clear this is what the mitochondria, brain and heart all operate around already in the early experiments. fMRI, MEG, and EEG data show it. Moreover, it is where relatively modest investment is producing fabulous experiments and testable theories already. The problem is no one in medicine reads physics to know what is going on or what has already been proven by experiment in biophysics or bioenergenics.. The relation of physics to biology, medicine, and neuroscience is a fantastic area that should be strongly developed. That is what we should be modeling instead of what we are today.
I am under no obligation to make sense to people in medicine. My epistemology is elevate their thinking above where it is now. And by elevating, I need them to sink to the quantum scale to make the leap to optimal for patients.

### caroline — 2014-07-27T02:27:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-k-says.11400/post-135671>

Dr. K. says we should all re- wild ourselves ...

What stellar advice!

so what should we do that is off the charts crazy and fun ...and out of our comfort zone????

What are we going to do today that is out of our comfort zone?

Dr. K. says we tend to keep living the same small life over and over.....we go for comfort every time.

I know I sure do ...and I hate that ......

I changed a few things yesterday .... and will continue to move forward everyday....

What is the point of a life half lived?
This was sparked, for me, yesterday ..... Jack was going out to dinner alone. I thought to myself that is great for him - he is a guy! I hate going out alone.

But then I thought - he is going alone ...but he will wind up chatting to all kinds of people. I can do that!

and so I did!

what are you going to do? What remarkable experience are you going to have today?

### JoeBranca — 2014-07-31T21:20:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-k-says.11400/post-136321>

a paradox comment... to say less and do more. i notice how important ideas and thoughts about what ought to be and what can be are too often shipwrecked on the rocks of "talking it up" without jumping into action. of course mindful discussion and deliberations are important in the process towards working it out, but there is always a point where an idea or a plan hits that shining moment where it resonates with our rewilding imperative, so the chatter needs to stop and the brick laying begin, or i can feel a false sense of accomplishment by only working out the thought experiment. perhaps even writing this is an example of that tendency that needs to die off.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-07T02:46:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-k-says.11400/post-137045>

You want to re-wild yourself? ......then do one thing to change yourself now. Everybody wants to change the world but few are ready to change themselves.

Today's PSA: People don't change because they do not understand the risk probabilities of them remaining the same. When you neglect probability you remain stationary. We worry about getting killed by a flesh eating bacteria at the beach as opposed to something far more probable, like suffering cardiac disease by eating like all of our friends or using non native EMF consistently. Probability neglect is our inability to properly grasp a proper sense of peril and risk — which often leads us to overstate the risks of relatively harmless activities, while forcing us to overrate more dangerous ones. Biology and medicine makes this error often and does not realize it. The ancestor of every action in life was born of a thought from an unseen force. Biology does not believe in unseen forces outside of Xrays. Biology believes if they cannot measure it, it does not exist. Quantum physics says nothing can be truly measured, so what thought is holding you captive so you can not assess your real versus perceived risks in life?

[http://www.thejayfk.com/?p=3399&utm...al&utm\_source=twitter.com&utm\_campaign=buffer](http://www.thejayfk.com/?p=3399&utm_content=buffer12d54&utm_medium=social&utm_source=twitter.com&utm_campaign=buffer)

This ability to segment/compartmentalize ones life is an indicator of a "\*NTJ" personality, ref MBTI personality sorter. (Clinton is an other good example of a smart guy with a high level of social compartmentalization.) Their ability to link previously unlinked knowledge/theories is mirrored in their personal lives, which is why his personal adventures are predictable. This personality also loves deeply, but invisibly. (They don't necessarily say "I love you." If they've committed to you, that should be enough, in their eyes. Sex is an activity separate from relationships and commitment.)

IMHO heightened dopamine is a part of this "condition", which leads to many of the early observed intellectual talents and later observed personal problems as the dopamine drops. (A hint of this was that he began speaking late...like Einstein, and other aspies.)

Marie

### Inger — 2014-09-27T15:38:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-k-says.11400/post-142029>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=142000)
>
> Awesome, awesome, awesome!!!
>
> what is everyone else doing to re wild themselves?????
>
> I am doing stuff that I wouldn't have thought possible four months ago .....

Well..... I just moved my bedroom to the horse barn  dragged my magnetico out there too..... I put it on wooden bars right on the floor.....
It is dirty and not much bedroom like out there... but I know my body could not care less. There is no wifi signal out there and that is what counts more than any optical beauty to me
I am not going to sleep ONE MORE NIGHT in the wifis from the neighbor. That is decided today. Now, after a few days with less time in the nature.... I can certainly feel it is no good.... when I am all day long in the wild I cannot feel so much but in stormy fall and winter i am going to be more inside and that is why I have to avoid the wifi for any cost at night.
I can hear it ring in my ears...... i am sure it is the wifi.. it is almost not noticeable... but it is there.. and it worries me.

I was thinking about doing like Peter, get sleep out in the woods once in a while... but then I decided (the wetter is pretty unpredictable here, lots of rain and storms... and i would need to buy a new tent... or make me a hut.. but not sure about leaving them up because it is someones private property.. the forest... so i would have to take the tent down every morning, and because i have the horse barn why not use it) I will not sleep at all in the bedroom anymore. And spend as much time on my terrace as possible, the signals are weak out here, thankfully. I actually am such a lucky chick to have my horse barn.... one of three rooms in there is mine.. I can do want I want there - sleep too...lol
Cannot wait for the day when my neighbors are going to notice... and ask why! I sure am going to tell them the truth...LOL your wifi are killing me... hehe....

i am excited to try my new bedroom tonight.... I have slept great inside too, but I know the wifi still affects me....

Well.. actually I do moved out there because of pain! Mental pain to be honest. I am not as clever as you maybe think Peter...  . I lost my peace.. but in a way... it was good too... it made me move out to the horse barn..lol I should have moved out there long ago but oh my how our want for comfort sits deep..... for me anyways!
I tell myself all the time, your want for comfort is killing you... slowly!!!! and making you unhappy, ugly and sick... do you want that????? There is no other way to real joy and health... than through the narrow door..... How many times have I not experienced that... and still I can have a hard time doing it sometimes.
Just like... the horse barn is a few meters away from the house.. so I have to walk down there... and the neighbors can see me from their windows.. with blueblockers on (Because there are a lamp that goes on when someone moves close to it by the carport....)... in the darkness. instead of just stepping into the cozy bedroom and drop into my bed...
But so what? I have already told them a lot so they know I am different...lol
It is like my mind is a master in telling me... oh.. this and that... it is too hard... it just sees all the problems, not the benefits... err! This is a big problem with my brain! And why I know I am far from having a optimal one yet.

I do woke a few times in the night, the cats of my landlord lives in the barn and made some noise but I fell to sleep immediately again... and when I woke up.... it felt nice... and I can tell it affected me good mentally to sleep in that barn

### Christos — 2014-10-01T04:48:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/d-k-says.11400/post-142359>

> [JoeBranca said:](/goto/post?id=142271)
>
> Christos, any tips or online resources you'd recommend to get started on bow making? i have some extra bamboo i'd like to try this out.

I used to gather about 20 small diameter (few centremeteres) and 1.5-2m long bamboo sticks. Id then string them together real tight. Then you bend the bamboo so theres a slight bend in it. Have you seen predator? Arnold makes a bow from mutiple sticks and bends it over his neck to make it curved, this is what I did. HAHA. Then you make a small incision in one of the bamboo sticks on either end. A simple string with loops tied on the end can then fit into these incisions. The key is getting the string length right. I used to use other bamboo sticks as arrows, with real feathers and nails/carved wood as spikes. You can also make some pretty epic spears out of thicker bamboo...

I used to use other bamboo sticks as arrows, with real feathers and nails/carved wood as spikes. You can also make some pretty epic spears out of thicker bamboo...

I think Yew and I told you ....when we went on the behind the scenes tour of the Sydney Fish market ...They assured us that there is no decline at all....

### ssj3 — 2014-07-27T10:34:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effects-of-a-dying-sea.11401/post-135700>

> [caroline said:](/goto/post?id=135691)
>
> I think Yew and I told you ....when we went on the behind the scenes tour of the Sydney Fish market ...They assured us that there is no decline at all....

Did you expect the truth??? As a regular fisher, I can say that there has certainly been a decline... I remember the days when you could go out and catch KG Whiting, Garfish, etc. and regularly return with bag limits (and back then it was double or triple what it is these days). Now you have to really work for your 30 KG... even tommies (or herring as you like to call them in the eastern states) are not in abundance. Besides environmental impacts the pros have really fished themselves out of a livelihood... some pros that I now are thinking of giving it away... they would return with 10 tonnes of snapper (and more), not anymore. And yes we do have the best managed fishery in the world.. no BS!

### Josh — 2014-07-27T21:02:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effects-of-a-dying-sea.11401/post-135728>

Before the extinction, there is the issue of the removal of much of the coastal Mangrove habitat along the African coast. Mangroves are the hatcheries for many fish and marine species. It is a specialized habitat. I have heard that along one section of the coast someone restored these habitats and the marine resources began to rebound.

### HoneyChild — 2014-07-30T13:43:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effects-of-a-dying-sea.11401/post-136073>

I was reading a text book that was sent to me 'The frozen planet' and found this somewhat amusing story. The ship's cat ran off with and ate a newly discovered fish. It was another 60 years before another specimen was found.

> HMS _Erebus_ and HMS _Terror_ had been to the Antarctic with James Clark Ross (1839–43) long before Franklin took them to their doom in the North-West Passage. Ross’s expedition was hugely successful and he discovered the location of the magnetic South Pole at the southern end of the notional dipole magnet in the Earth (Figure 3.9). He also made many geographical discoveries including Ross Island on which the location of current largest base in Antarctica resides, and Mount Erebus which is the most spectacular volcano on the continent. Ross took civilian experts to describe and write about their discoveries and they produced vast scientific volumes to record their results. It is no exaggeration to say that science in the polar regions was leading global understanding. Unfortunately the official reports from Ross’s expedition reveal that the pathway to knowledge is sometimes more complicated than originally envisaged:
>
> "When the ships were in the high latitude of 77° 10' S, and long. 178½° \[W\], a fish was thrown up by the spray in a gale of wind, against the bows of the Terror, and frozen there. It was carefully removed … and a rough sketch was made of it by the surgeon, John Robertson, Esq., but before it could be put into spirits, a cat carried it away from his cabin, and ate it. The sketch is not sufficiently detailed \[so\] … we have introduced a copy of the design merely to preserve a memorial of what appears to be a novel form."
>
> Richardson (1844)
>
> This was a tragedy as the fish was a species new to science that had very unusual features including long fins and very pale flesh. Sixty years were to pass before a scientist on the expedition led by Adrien de Gerlache on the ship _Belgica_ rediscovered and named the fish Robertson had drawn (Figure 3.10).
>
>
>
> **Figure 3.10**  The cod icefish rediscovered and published in 1904 by Louis Dollo. The original caption in the expedition report read ‘Mangé par le chat de l’équipage de la Terror’ or ‘Eaten by the _Terror_’s cat!’
>
> The fish was a so-called icefish – a member of a remarkable species able to live in the very cold Antarctic waters. Animal blood is a fluid which carries nutrients around the body and it consists mainly of liquid called plasma, red and white blood cells and another cell type called platelets. In most animals, the red blood cells carry the oxygen throughout the body, but as Richardson noted, the icefish was very pale, which means it has very few red blood cells. It does, however, have unusual plasma which can carry oxygen and contains antifreeze proteins as well. Chapter 4 will show that the Southern Ocean has a high concentration of dissolved oxygen. This together with the antifreeze and the special plasma means that the pale-coloured icefish with few red blood cells can nevertheless extract enough oxygen to thrive in the cold polar waters.

### PaulG — 2014-07-31T07:51:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effects-of-a-dying-sea.11401/post-136280>

[Graphene](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphene) may be the way of the future for desalination and a few other applications such as Quantum computing.

> It is the basic structural element of other allotropes, including graphite, [charcoal](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Charcoal), [carbon nanotubes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_nanotube) and [fullerenes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fullerene). It can also be considered as an indefinitely large [aromatic](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Aromaticity) molecule, the limiting case of the family of flat [polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Polycyclic_aromatic_hydrocarbon)

..Where have I heard about Carbon Nanotubes, Fullerenes and topological insulators before....but Graphene I think was new in [OSF-3](http://jackkruse.com/osf-3-darwin-was-wrong/)

> Jack Kruse said:
>
> **WHY IS DHA SO POWERFUL? PI ELECTRON CLOUDS= QUANTUM MAGIC**
>
> The DHA double bonds consist of a sigma bond and a pi bond. By subtracting 80 kcal/mole (the sigma bond energy) from 145 kcal/mole (the C=C bond energy) we can calculate that the pi bond has a dissociation energy of about 65 kcal/mole. This is much less than the sigma bond energy, and, therefore, pi bonds are more reactive than sigma bonds. This means that, all things being equal, the **pi electron bonds of DHA are more likely to undergo activation by light.** Your brain is just like a star. It works optically and photonically. This is why optokinetics works to change neural circuits. The brain is all about light because 35-40% of all lipids in membranes in the brain are DHA. Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a mechanism describing energy transfer between two[chromophores](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chromophore). In the [conjugated](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conjugated_system) chromophores, like DHA, the [electrons jump between energy levels](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Molecular_electron_transition) that are extended [pi orbitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Pi_bond). This is created by a series of alternating [single and double bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chemical_bond). A conjugated system is a system of connected [p-orbitals](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/P-orbital) with [delocalized electrons](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Delocalized_electron) in compounds with alternating single and multiple [bonds](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Covalent_bond), which, in general, may lower the overall energy of the molecule and increase stability. The largest artificial conjugated systems are found in [graphene](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphene), [graphite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graphite),[conductive polymers](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Conductive_polymer), and [carbon nanotubes](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Carbon_nanotube). Most of these are being used in quantum computer design and in artificial intelligence using helium.

[http://www.technologyreview.com/vie...-are-promising-to-revolutionise-desalination/](http://www.technologyreview.com/view/519161/how-carbon-wonder-materials-are-promising-to-revolutionise-desalination/)

> Graphene was the first material predicted to realize a topological insulator (TI), but unfortunately the gap is unobservably small due to carbon's weak spin-orbital coupling (SOC). Based on first-principles calculations, we propose a stable sp-sp2 hybrid carbon network as a graphene analog whose electronic band structures in proximity of the Fermi level are characterized by Dirac cones. We demonstrate that this unique carbon framework has topologically nontrivial electronic structures with the Z2 topological invariant of _v_ = 1 which is quite promising for hosting the quantum spin Hall effect (QSHE) in an experimentally accessible low temperature regime (<7 K). This provides a viable approach for searching for new TIs in 2D carbon allotropes.

[http://www.nature.com/srep/2013/131218/srep03532/full/srep03532.html](http://www.nature.com/srep/2013/131218/srep03532/full/srep03532.html)

### ssj3 — 2014-07-31T11:37:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-effects-of-a-dying-sea.11401/post-136290>

> [Jude said:](/goto/post?id=136278)
>
> Interesting re oxygen levels.............................

Oxygen levels in the sea support sea life.... from these studies:
\- falling oceanic oxygen concentrations may have severe and wide-ranging effects on marine species and ecosystems.
\- We found significant increasing trends in phosphate and nitrate, and a decreasing trend in apparent oxygen utilization (AOU).
\- Low-oxygen waters may have contributed to the mortality event by driving masses of krill to shallower-than-normal waters, where oxygen levels are higher.
\- krill excretion products... at levels expected in krill swarms, greatly stimulates bacteria resulting in an order-of-magnitude increase in growth and production... in the Southern Ocean.

[http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007/s11434-014-0113-0#page-1](http://link.springer.com/article/10.1007%2Fs11434-014-0113-0#page-1)
[http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0071766](http://www.plosone.org/article/info:doi/10.1371/journal.pone.0071766)
[http://oceanspaces.org/blog/sea-grant-studying-krill-die#sthash.zxnkrcyH.dpuf](http://oceanspaces.org/blog/sea-grant-studying-krill-die#sthash.zxnkrcyH.dpuf)

# Thread: Health Mayhem: Ashley's Optimal Journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/health-mayhem-ashleys-optimal-journal.11406/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 26
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### A.Diane — 2014-07-27T21:40:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/health-mayhem-ashleys-optimal-journal.11406/post-135729>

Hey All! Newbie here, and been viewing Dr. Jack's site for the past few weeks trying to put the pieces together and have to say it's a difficult task when you're sub-optimal!

After years of being passed around doctor to doctor being tested like a guinea pig and spewing out literally thousands of dollars for tests, appointments and treatments, I'm quite fed up and have been feeling hopeless in my road to recovery (whatever that entails). I'm hoping to get some feedback from you all and doctor Jack, as I've given up on most doctors and have been going the self-navigation route with little success. So below is a bit of information about me and health troubles/MAJOR eating isssues in my life up to this point to give a clear picture.

1989 - Born and eaten the typical SAD from toddler to June 2011. I've battled with depression on and off since I can remember.

Ages 9 to 15 - Started gaining weight and became overweight. I had an overeating issue.

Age 15 - I believe I started my first period at this age, before the anorexia. Got my first onset of anorexia, lasting about 6 months or so. Went from about 150lbs to 114lbs by eating a salad a day (no dressing or toppings, just plain lettuce) and loading up on coffee to get me through. The weekends my body would give out and I would binge on junk food. Eventually I gave in to eating normally and the disorder gradually went away as I gained weight back. Never lost my period during this timeframe.

Ages 16 to 18.5 - After my bout of anorexia I steadied my weight at 145lbs. During high school I didn't eat breakfast or lunch, and waited to come home to eat -- often times overeating and not realizing why (duh). I suppose you could say I still had a bit of the disordered thinking, but not near as intense. In fact it felt quite good and normal for me to behave this way and I didn't really have body image issues at this time.

Ages 17 to 18.5 - Graduated high school and started eating breakfast, lunch and dinner without really any snacking. My bodyweight stuck between 135 and 140lbs. This seemed to be my bodies "happy place" and I felt the best and most regulated during this time.

Age 19 - Started getting more depressed and started Paxil (WORST decision of my life). This drug made me even more crazy, as I started not caring about anything -- stopped going to class (college) and started drinking soda for the first time in my life, and eating loads of food. My weight shot up to 165lbs. I broke up with my 4 year boyfriend at the time too, which added to the craziness.

Age 19.5 21 - Got off Paxil, dropped the weight pretty quickly without even trying really. Started going back to class after a sad semester. Met a nice lad who I started dating (currently with him after almost 6 years), and started getting back into eating normally (i.e. breakfast, lunch and dinner). My body felt good again. From about 20-21 I dabbled in working out for the first time in my life, and it was very light, 3-4 times per week bodyweight workouts.

June 2011 to Dec 2012 (Age 21.5 22.5) - Just graduated and had an internship that offered me a full-time job right out of college for event coordination. My 2nd onset of anorexia came back HARD. I got this idea in my head that I would start eating 1200 calories a day and run 15 miles every week. I'd have days where I'd overeat and then I'd make up by eating on 100-300 calories the next few days (i.e. binging and purging on overexercise). I started adding in P90X and HIIT bodyweight workouts 4-5 days a week on top of the running. I became addicted. I went from 145lbs to 116lbs from June 2011 to December 2011 by eating starving, binging, overexercising, repeat. **I had severe fluctuation in water retention, very scarily severe the entire time I was going through anorexia. I lost my period two months after the anorexia started.I drove myself to the ER March 2012.** Symptoms: Body LITERALLY expanded, vomiting, confusion (yeah, I shouldn't have driven), shaking. Diagnosis: Nothing. They sent me home telling me to hydrate. Looking back I know exactly what it was -- hyponatremia. I ran 3 miles the night before and the morning it happened. I was drinking boat loads of water and I avoided sodium during this time, so I was literally dehydrating myself from running and sweating out sodium and loading water into my body, so my body pushed the water outside the cells. Quite disturbed that all that needed to be done was eating a high sodium meal. During this time I also had urinary incontinence. After the ER, I went back and forth between eating normal, binging, purging, overexercising, not exercising, restricting -- pretty much every ED possible. I tracked my calories obsessively, even during periods of binging. My bodyweight and water retention continued to fluctuate. I became intrigued by building muscle too, rather than just fat loss and attempted to do more powerlifting type exercises.
**January 2013 -** Got a personal trainer letting her know my "past". I wanted to train for a bikini competition. I was running (again) five days a week, 45 minutes at a time and weight training 5 times per week. I was, however, eating more than before. She even got me to 2,000 calories a day (but still in a high deficit from the exercising). I did gain some weight, muscle mostly, and got up to 138lbs. I ended my contract with her APril 2013.

May 2013 to Dec. 2013 (Age 24) - **My body shut down. I surrendered.** Quit my job. I felt like death. Since about 2012 I continued having SEVERE insomnia with the "wired and tired" feeling. I had been going to several doctors between the time my anorexia started and now. Gotten several tests done (blood tests, urine tests, MRIs) trying to find out why this was happening to my body. Quite sickens me to know how in denial I was, it was of course me doing this to my own body. **So here I am, almost bed ridden, no job (no income).** I found a holistic doctor. **Diagnosis: SEVERAL hormonal imbalances (big surprise). Severely low t3, body wasn't producing estrogen or progesterone, very low testosterone, severely low D3, anemia. DHEA was normal at this time surprisingly. I was put on bioidentical hormones (progesterone, t3 and t4), multi vitamin, fish oil, 10,000 I.U.s D3, high dose of injectible b12 with folic acid (I opted for sublingual after the first few shots).** I was told to go home, relax, meditate, EAT, and do what made me happy. This was hard for me -- before I was constantly on the go. I would wake up 530AM, get ready for work, go to work, go to the gym for almost 2.5 hours, come home, have dinner, then go to bed IF I could. **I stopped working out between May 2013 and October 2013, and just eating and stopped tracking. I did make sure I was getting plenty of protein, healthy fats and carbs. And of course I would have some junk food here and there. I got my body back to 145lbs. My period started again, after not having one since October 2011. I only got it for three months, and then it went away again. Sleeping was better, but still not well. I started feeling better, but still not WELL.** I actually took a personal trainer course online, because I wanted to understand the "healthy" way to go about being fit and eating well. I started eating steadily at what I found my maintenance calories to be and started ONLY weight lifting.

**January 2014 to now (Age 25) - Still no period. Although I feel immensely better than I did last year around my huge crash and quitting my job, I still feel like I can't do much of anything. Small everyday stressors take a lot out of me. However, I do still weight lift, no cardio though. I've grown to actually have a huge passion for weightlifting and don't want to give it up. I eat plentiful, and actually have occasions of binging now. I do still track my calories, but am more concerned about getting enough protein more than anything else. I'll have days where I'll eat 1500 calories, and when I do binge every now and then I'll get up to sometimes 3500 calories. My weight has steadied between 145 and 155lbs now. Insomnia is killing me. I can't fall asleep or stay asleep. I'm lucky to get 2-4 hours sleep. I still have a bit of a food "obsession", but not in a restrictive way, I just think about it a lot. I've been taking my hormones, and having to adjust the progesterone based on how I feel. Currently taking 25mcg t3, 50mcg t4, 75mg progesterone, and a very small dose of testosterone (testosterone is new since January of this year, and this has done wonders for my confidence). I isolate myself in my room much of the time because I'm afraid to have any energy taken away by interaction of any sort. I feel fatigued constantly. I know I've lost so many brain cells because of lack of sleep and feel like my IQ has dropped immensely, as I have a hard time remember things. I also within the past year developed a breathing issue -- oftentimes it feels I can't catch my breath. I'd love to be able to get to the point of eating intuitively, but still track my calories to ensure I'm getting plenty of protein and calories to support weight lifting.** Just purchased my blue blockers and started to wear them the past few nights. I'm trying to put Jack's blogs together to try to take the steps to get well, but I'm hoping you guys and Dr. Jack can give me some direction based on my story here. I'm so ready to just be normal, feel well, and be a NORMAL 25 year old. While my other "peers" are starting their careers, getting married, having children, and being 20-something-year-olds, I'm trying to just feel well. Any help is so welcomed and greatly appreciated. Looking forward to journaling my healing here moving forward.

### A.Diane — 2014-08-01T20:55:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/health-mayhem-ashleys-optimal-journal.11406/post-136390>

### ms.jammies — 2014-07-28T02:51:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/help-with-weight-gain-from-meds.11407/post-135769>

I'm looking for a little guidance here. I was successfully paleo (autoimmune protocol)for 3 years, lost all my excess weight, and kept it off without much effort.

I developed a severe, crushing depression and anxiety disorder (very long, crazy story on what caused it - took me over a year to figure out, but I'm great now). Anyway, the depression was not compatible with life, and after trying every natural treatment I could, my alternative doctor finally said we needed to go with prescription meds. I went on Zoloft. It worked well for the depression and the anxiety. But it really caused me to gain weight. I was still eating paleo throughout this time, but I did eat a LOT of rice. My depression caused me to be almost completely unable to eat - rice with butter and a bit of meat was about all I could choke down. I lost about 10 lbs during the depression before starting meds. I started gaining immediately, but it just didn't occur to me to worry about it at first because I was so desperate for any improvement. After I felt stable enough that it was safe to make changes, I switched over to wellbutrin. I stopped gaining weight, but could NOT lose. NO matter what I did, I could not even temporarily lose 1 lb.

Once I figured out the underlying cause I started working on it, I was also able to drop the Wellbutrin. Still no weight loss. None.

I have been 100% strict paleo, I've tried fasting, low carb, calorie counting. I have yet to lose a single lb. Not even for a day. I've been trying for a year.

I am so disgusted with my weight and it makes me miserable.

Has anyone heard of such a thing? Any ideas how it could happen? Could leptin reset fix whatever these meds have broken?

Thanks so much! I'd be thrilled with any advice

### NeilBB — 2014-07-28T22:13:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-135860>

I would really like to discuss to magnetism, so I'm gonna just start with this random thought I had...

MRI physics tells us that when you place protons in a magnetic field, they will "precess" or wobble at a particular frequency, the Larmour frequency, that corresponds to the strength of the magnetic field. Then, when an external RF pulse is administered that corresponds with the Larmour frequency, resonance occurs, which means protons will absorb and later re-emit this particular electromagnetic energy. This is basically how MRI works. The protons' response to this specific RF stimulus and relaxation from it must then be measured by receiving antennae in order to then acquire and generate the image.

So I was thinking. A 20-gauss magnetico pad would also have a specific Larmour frequency for protons. Turns out if you calculate it, it happens to be 85 kHz, which falls in the low frequency radio band. Interestingly, that RF frequency was approved last year for wireless charging stations for electric cars:

[http://www.sae.org/servlets/pressRoom?OBJECT\_TYPE=PressReleases&PAGE=showRelease&RELEASE\_ID=2296](http://www.sae.org/servlets/pressRoom?OBJECT_TYPE=PressReleases&PAGE=showRelease&RELEASE_ID=2296)

[http://www.greencarcongress.com/2014/02/20140213-tmcwireless.html](http://www.greencarcongress.com/2014/02/20140213-tmcwireless.html)

So if you sleep on a 20G Magnetico and get one of these babies in your driveway, I believe that you basically have created a very tiny MRI, sans the recording antennae! Whether this effect would detract from or enhance the baseline resonance benefit of the Magnetico, I don't know... Any thoughts?

### Sue-UK — 2014-07-30T16:23:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136088>

Ok I am way out of my depth but I'll have a go at showing how little I know ....  And on the basis that the only daft question is the one not asked ....

I understand why the magnetico is better than not having one in areas of bad NN EMF, so leaving aside the electron paramagnetic benefits, in terms of signalling generally, are there any implications of wobbling protons at one specific frequency for long periods of time?

### NeilBB — 2014-07-30T16:43:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136092>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=135979)
>
> ^^^^What he said.

It's pretty simple. NMR is all about the magnetic spin property of atomic nuclei. For MRI, the nucleus we are talking about is the hydrogen nucleus (aka proton) which is everywhere water is. NMR also affects other heavier nuclei like sodium, for example, but not all nuclei, only those that happen to have a magnetic dipole. The Larmour frequency is a function of both the strength of the magnetic field and the identity of the nucleus in question.

Electron paramagnetic resonance is a similar phenomenon but involves the magnetic spin of the electrons, not the heavier nuclei. This property is not used to aquire MR images, but like NMR can be used in spectroscopy, to identify the structure of chemicals.

### NeilBB — 2014-07-30T16:55:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136094>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=136088)
>
> Ok I am way out of my depth but I'll have a go at showing how little I know ....  And on the basis that the only daft question is the one not asked ....
>
> I understand why the magnetico is better than not having one in areas of bad NN EMF, so leaving aside the electron paramagnetic benefits, in terms of signalling generally, are there any implications of wobbling protons at one specific frequency for long periods of time?

Well, we evolved on earth in a fairly strong DC magnetic field. So the resonance effects of various nuclei and electrons in response to a field would be a normal and likely necessary phenomenon.

But the interesting thing is that when you vary the strength of the magnetic field, you change the specific radio frequencies at which energy can be exchanged for a given nucleus or particle. This is a quantized effect.

### NeilBB — 2014-07-30T19:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136113>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=136095)
>
> In reality, no one that knows enough to have a Magnetico would ever get an electric car, but I understand that this is a thought experiment.
> Thanks for bringing up this subject, Neil. I'm a bit intimidated by magnetism, but keenly interested. A bit of a quandary for me.
> Does electron paramagnetic resonance only affect the electrons (not the protons) because it is a weaker field?

EPR gets very complicated fast and is used primarily to study molecules with unpaired electrons. But I believe that all magnetic fields, regardless of strength would affect all dipoles and charged particles that are subject to it. What varies in the "resonance" phenomena is that the specific strength of the field defines a discrete quantized frequency of potential energy exchange for each magnetic particle. If the surrounding RF signal matches the resonance frequency of the particle in that field, energy transfer occurs.

I also got a history lesson about amps and how they started off using valves but then transistors came in. They were cheap and light so became very popular and the only companies that continued to make valve amps were Fender and Marshall. Then everyone realised the transistor amps were shite and now most amps are valves. The transistors you can get are at the very cheap end of the market and also for bass guitars.

> **Vacuum tube amplifiers**
>
> Vacuum tubes (valves) were by far the dominant active electronic components in most [instrument amplifier](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Instrument_amplifier) applications until the 1970s, when semiconductors (transistors) started taking over for performance and economic reasons, including heat and weight reduction, and improved reliability. High-end tube instrument amplifiers have survived as one of few exceptions, because of the sound quality. Typically, one or more dual [triodes](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Triode) are used in the preamplifier section in order to provide sufficient voltage gain to offset losses by tone controls and to drive the power amplifier section. While tube technology is in many ways outdated, they remain popular since many guitarists prefer the sound of tube amplifiers.[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier#cite_note-Gallagher-13)
>
>
> **Solid-state amplifiers**
> Most inexpensive guitar amplifiers are based on [semiconductor](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Semiconductor) (solid-state) circuits, and some designs incorporate tubes in the preamp stage for their subjectively warmer overdrive sound—see "Hybrid amplifiers", below. Solid-state amplifiers are much cheaper to produce and more reliable, and they are usually much lighter than tube amplifiers.[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier#cite_note-Gallagher-13)
>
> High-end solid-state amplifiers are less common, since many professional guitarists tend to favor vacuum tubes. Some jazz guitarists, however, tend to favor the "cleaner" sound of solid-state amplifiers; only a few solid-state amps have enduring attraction, such as the [Roland Jazz Chorus](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Roland_Jazz_Chorus).[\[13\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier#cite_note-Gallagher-13)[\[14\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier#cite_note-14)[\[15\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier#cite_note-15) Solid-state amplifiers vary in output power, functionality, size, price, and sound quality in a wide range, from practice amplifiers to professional models.
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar\_amplifier](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Guitar_amplifier)

### NeilBB — 2014-07-30T22:45:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136157>

[http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node4.html](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node4.html)

> But Bohmian mechanics is just such a deeper explanation (as is SL, of which, however, Feynman could not have been aware). It resolves the dilemma of the appearance, in one and the same phenomenon, of both particle and wave properties in a rather straightforward manner: Bohmian mechanics is a theory of motion describing a particle (or particles) guided by a wave. In Figure [1](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node4.html#tse) we have a family of Bohmian trajectories for the two-slit experiment. While each trajectory passes through but one of the slits, the wave passes through both; the interference profile that therefore develops in the wave generates a similar pattern in the trajectories guided by this wave.
>
> Is it not clear from the smallness of the scintillation on the screen that we have to do with a particle? And is it not clear, from the diffraction and interference patterns, that the motion of the particle is directed by a wave? De Broglie showed in detail how the motion of a particle, passing through just one of two holes in screen, could be influenced by waves propagating through both holes. And so influenced that the particle does not go where the waves cancel out, but is attracted to where they cooperate. This idea seems to me so natural and simple, to resolve the wave-particle dilemma in such a clear and ordinary way, that it is a great mystery to me that it was so generally ignored. (Bell 1986)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-30T22:48:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136158>

The pilot-wave approach to quantum theory was initiated, even before the discovery of quantum mechanics itself, by Einstein, who hoped that interference phenomena involving particle-like photons could be explained if the motion of the photons were somehow guided by the electromagnetic field--which would thus play the role of what he called a_Führungsfeld_or **_guiding field_** \[[26](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node7.html#Wigner), page 262,\]. While the notion of the electromagnetic field **as guiding field** turned out to be rather problematical, the possibility that for a system of electrons the wave function might play this role, of guiding field or pilot wave, was explored by Max Born in his early paper founding quantum scattering theory \[[27](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node7.html#born)\]--a suggestion to which Heisenberg was profoundly unsympathetic.

NOW RE READ LEVEE ONE IN THE QUILT

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-30T22:50:17+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136159>

By 1927, an equation of particle motion equivalent to ([3](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node4.html#dbe)) for a scalar wave function had been written down by Louis de Broglie \[[28](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node7.html#db), page 119,\], who explained at the 1927 Solvay Congress how this motion could account for quantum interference phenomena. However, de Broglie badly failed to respond adequately to an objection of Wolfgang Pauli \[[30](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node7.html#Pauli), pages 280-282,\] concerning inelastic scattering, no doubt making a rather poor impression on the illustrious audience gathered for the occasion (see Figure [7](http://www.math.rutgers.edu/~oldstein/papers/qts/node7.html#sol)).

Born and De Broglie very quickly abandoned the pilot-wave approach and became enthusiastic supporters of the rapidly developing consensus in favor of the Copenhagen interpretation.

This is where physics went off the rails.

**The most useful piece of learning for the uses of life is to unlearn what is untrue.** Many "educated people" spend their entire adult live filling their brains with millions of facts but remain very uneducated about life. To learn about life you need to remain curious. Question everything.

here is where we are now: [http://www.periodicplayground.com/periodic-feature/kane-supersymmetry](http://www.periodicplayground.com/periodic-feature/kane-supersymmetry)

here is the problem post Higgs: Theoretical physicist [Peter Woit](http://www.math.columbia.edu/~woit/wordpress/?p=4772) of Columbia University in New York wrote on his blog that the LHC's two main experiments are seeing the same signal as in December – hinting at a Higgs with an energy of 125 gigaelectronvolts – but this time with greater statistical significance. Woit declined to name his sources but assured the media they were "highly reliable". So what is the problem? TAU.

**Too many taus**
Still, for now, those rumours are just that. "It is still too premature to start going wild," says [Pauline Gagnon](http://www.indiana.edu/~iubphys/faculty/pgagnon.shtml) of CERN's ATLAS experiment.

In the meantime, a new analysis from the BaBar experiment, which ran at the SLAC National Accelerator Lab in California until 2008, suggests the standard model is not what it seems.

According to the model, a particle called the B meson, studied by BaBar, decays to produce particles including a W boson, which then decays further into a tau particle and a tau neutrino. Now [BaBar reports](http://arxiv.org/abs/arXiv:1205.5442) B mesons decaying into tau particles more often than the standard model predicts.

"**It looks like the standard model has something in it that we don't understand,**" says BaBar spokesperson [Michael Roney](https://particle.phys.uvic.ca/~roney/) at the University of Victoria in British Columbia, Canada.

Since 2009 Kepler telescope has really shown the problem in cosmology:
[http://www.sott.net/article/281284-Standard-model-for-planetary-system-formation-in-trouble](http://www.sott.net/article/281284-Standard-model-for-planetary-system-formation-in-trouble)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-30T23:27:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136171>

[http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/does-physics-have-a-problem/](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/does-physics-have-a-problem/)

Here is where we are now:
**Does Physics Have a Problem?**
Editor in chief Mariette DiChristina introduces the May 2014 issue of_Scientific American_
May 1, 2014 |By [Mariette DiChristina](http://www.scientificamerican.com/author/mariette-dichristina)

It was 2012, and physicists had just announced the big news: CERN's Large Hadron Collider had delivered what looked like ([and was later confirmed to be](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/higgs-cern-lhc-discovery/)) a Higgs particle, the capstone of a decades-long search to complete the so-called [Standard Model of particle physics](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/preons-the-particle-landscape/)—a discovery that would lead the following year to a [Nobel Prize](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/nobel-physics-prize-higgs-englert/). Naturally, many scientists immediately eyed the next piece of the puzzle that they anticipate the LHC will uncover. “I'm still hanging tough, hoping for supersymmetry,” said John Ellis of King's College London at the time, referring to the theory of matter that many physicists thought would supplant the Standard Model.

Since then, however, the search for “superpartner” particles that would help describe why particles have the masses they do and would solve the mystery of dark matter has led to disappointments. So far, as Joseph Lykken and Maria Spiropulu relate in their cover story, “[Supersymmetry and the Crisis in Physics](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/supersymmetry-and-the-crisis-in-physics/),” the “results from the first run of the LHC have ruled out almost all the best-studied versions” of the theory. A higher-power run is due in 2015, but there is no guarantee it will yield the answers. Then what?

While we wait for the foundational picture of how the universe works to take better shape, we can watch machines develop the ability to take different forms here on Earth. In “[Shape-Shifting Things to Come](http://www.scientificamerican.com/article/shape-shifting-things-to-come/)” Sridhar Kota chronicles efforts to employ elastic, or compliant, design in our man-made contraptions. Instead of using many rigid parts in complex and often inefficient systems, as is done today, such designs can distribute loads across flexible devices made of fewer parts. The materials could enable such applications as bendable aircraft wings and snake robots, and their use would improve efficiency and durability in our engineered creations. They may not solve some of our profound questions about the universe, but they could make things easier here in the meantime.

Now consider what George F. R. Ellis, the physicist-mathematician-cosmologist, an authority on the Big Bang and other cosmic mysteries says :
See his website, and his terrific 2011 critique of multiverse theories in Scientific American. Awesome sauce. [http://www.mth.uct.ac.za/~ellis/](http://www.mth.uct.ac.za/~ellis/)

He said, " Well this was just echoing what you have already said: many of the possible high-energy physics experiments and astronomy observations relevant to cosmology are now in essence nearly complete. Physics experiments are approaching the highest energies it will ever be possible to test by any collider experiment, both for financial and technical reasons. We can’t build a collider bigger than the surface of the Earth. Thus our ability to test high energy physics – and hence structures on the smallest physical scales – is approaching its limits. Astronomical observations at all wavelengths are now probing the most distant cosmological events that will ever be “seeable” by any kinds of radiation whatever, because of visual horizons for each form of radiation.

It’s rather like the situation as regards exploring the Earth: once upon a time we had only fragmentary knowledge of what is there. Then we obtained a global picture of the Earth’s surface, including detailed satellite images of the entire land mass. Once you have seen it all, you have seen it all; apart from finer and finer details, there is nothing more to find. You might respond, But we can’t see to the bottom of the oceans. However, we do indeed now have quite good maps of the ocean floor too, through various sounding techniques. This is similar to the way we have seen right back to the last scattering surface in the early universe at a redshift of 1200 (the analogue of seeing the entire surface of the Earth from space) with satellites such as COBE, WMAP, and Planck, and also (indirectly) to the time of emission of gravitational waves by Bicep2 (the analogue of seeing to the bottom of the ocean). We’ll sort out that controversy in the next couple of years.

So what we can see at the largest and smallest scales is approaching what will ever be possible, except for refining the details.

But I emphasize that this comment does not apply to complex systems. Complexity is almost unbounded – microbiology, biology, the brain will give us work to do for many centuries more, what we may find may be very unexpected. That might also apply to the foundations of quantum physics, and its relation to complexity. But – barring something very unforeseen – the possible tests of the very large and the very small are coming towards the limits of whatever will be possible.

Yes I know this kind of thing has been said before. That was before we had explored the entire visible universe at all possible wavelengths. I concede that observations relevant to structure formation in the universe – galaxies, stars, planets – have a good while to go, they are in essence verging to the side of studying complexity, and still have life in them yet, they are very interesting studies."

I'll decode that for you all........CERN data may show that supersymmetry is not a fundamental part of our universe. There are many signs that this is the case. This is why string theory and supersymmetry have evolved.......to fill the pot holes of the Standard model.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-01T13:53:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136356>

^^^^^ read it carefully.
"But for quantum particles, this is not necessarily true. Swap your pigeons for electrons, and send them three at a time into an interferometer. This device splits each electron into two, sends them along both paths simultaneously, then brings them together again.

Then "post-select" some of the electrons to influence their past state. You do this by measuring the electrons' states when they exit the interferometer, selecting electrons in a particular state that is significantly different to the one they had when they entered. That should create quantum links between those electrons, Tollaksen says [(see diagram)](http://www.newscientist.com/data/images/archive/2980/29802301.jpg).

To show the links are really there, you need to know what the electrons are doing inside the device. Another quantum effect, called superposition, means that each electron takes both paths at once. If any two of the three electrons share one arm, which must happen classically, their identical electrical charges will repel one another, deflecting their trajectories ever so slightly. That deflection should be detectable when the electrons exit the interferometer"

THIS IS WHAT A MITOCHONDRIA REALLY DOES TO FOOD ELECTRONS

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-03T20:26:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-136644>

Mass is just condensed energy. People forget it because they have an experience with matter and not so much with energy. Einstein's mass equivalence equation shows us this side of nature. So when an apple is up high it has energy in it called potential energy. When it drops from the heights to the ground, the action of falling ascribed to gravity is actually a thermodynamic phase change. As the apple falls potential energy is lost to kinetic energy due to the effect of gravity. Gravity is an emergent feature of condensed matter that exists to balance the energy transfers that happen between forms of condensed matter.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-14T06:37:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-137802>

Nature's innovations, rely on spare parts. (BG 2)
Evolution advances by taking available resources and cobbling them together to create new uses. (EPIGENETICS)
Evolution is about recycling. (OSF 7)
Billion years ago, if you were a carbon atom, there were only a few hundred molecular configurations you could stumble into. 600 million years ago carbon met oxygen and made a bunch more networks of chemicals were created. One, was DHA. It turns out DHA turns light into electricity, electricity to magnetism and magnetism to electricity or back to light. This ability allows us to control electrons to mix with other chemicals in us to make new things in us. Magnetism is a force that can sculpt matter but it must be controlled. Magnetism can be controlled by light to make matter do what it wants. Now consider that today that same carbon atom can help build a new human brain circuit, a new semiconductor like graphene, a new idea or innovation, a new virus like ebola or the H1N1 virus, along with every single object on the planet made of plastic.

Ideas are, almost inevitably, are networks of other ideas. We take the ideas we've inherited or stumbled across, and we put them together like liquor in a jigger and shake them together into some new shape. When an idea takes a new shape or size the idea’s thermodynamics change and from this action a new idea or concept is born. (OSF 3)

### nonchalant — 2014-08-14T16:15:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-137831>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=137802)
>
> Nature's innovations, rely on spare parts. (BG 2)
> Evolution advances by taking available resources and cobbling them together to create new uses. (EPIGENETICS)
> Evolution is about recycling. (OSF 7)
> Billion years ago, if you were a carbon atom, there were only a few hundred molecular configurations you could stumble into. 600 million years ago carbon met oxygen and made a bunch more networks of chemicals were created. One, was DHA. It turns out DHA turns light into electricity, electricity to magnetism and magnetism to electricity or back to light. This ability allows us to control electrons to mix with other chemicals in us to make new things in us. Magnetism is a force that can sculpt matter but it must be controlled. Magnetism can be controlled by light to make matter do what it wants. Now consider that today that same carbon atom can help build a new human brain circuit, a new semiconductor like graphene, a new idea or innovation, a new virus like ebola or the H1N1 virus, along with every single object on the planet made of plastic.
>
> Ideas are, almost inevitably, are networks of other ideas. We take the ideas we've inherited or stumbled across, and we put them together like liquor in a jigger and shake them together into some new shape. When an idea takes a new shape or size the idea’s thermodynamics change and from this action a new idea or concept is born. (OSF 3)

I love doing this!!
Out on our 'farm' we often have to innovate. We are far from a city, so every time we need something new, it would be an expensive (time and money) trip into town. So instead of wasting half a day, we innovate. We solve the problem in another way, or use parts already on hand, which usually are intended for a different purpose. Or we grab parts from some structure we have that is no longer in use, like the old cabin. Or we can borrow from a neighbor, and repay later with something else they would like...veggies from our garden, perhaps. It always makes us feel good, innovating. If we have to take a special trip to town, it often means that we have failed to solve the problem efficiently.

It helps to have room to store parts that are still usable, (or could be renovated) but are not currently needed.

Now I know why innovating makes us feel so good. We are doing what we were born to do.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-14T16:19:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-137834>

yep............but that thought is what OSF 3 is all about this. The quantum mechanism works like this. It uses what is known and then innovates from there to match the environment.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-14T16:19:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/magnetism.11412/post-137835>

In crawfish, blood cells are attracted to the niche, a nursery of baby neurons at the base of the brain. Each cell splits into two daughter cells, precursors to full neurons, both of which migrate out of the niche. Those head to two clumps of nerves in the brain called clusters 9 and 10.

[http://www.cell.com/developmental-cell/abstract/S1534-5807(14)00405-5](http://www.cell.com/developmental-cell/abstract/S1534-5807\(14\)00405-5)

One thing that I've noticed about magnetism is that at the new house our battery powered alarm clocks when placed on the floor slow down or stop all together. this effect is magnified when placed in proximity of the magneticos. At our old house we could put the alarm clocks on the floor or in direct proximity with the magneticos and we'd see no change.

There's something about our new place where the energy that's given off is positive. and it revitalizing. Even if it means we have to depend on our biological clocks to get up in the morning....

### Hope — 2014-07-29T00:37:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-printer.11414/post-135872>

Have any of you found any good wired printers? Or, is the only option now to do wireless.....and turn off that feature?

### prAna303 — 2014-07-29T08:38:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-printer.11414/post-135899>

There are tons of good ones on the second hand market. Place it in a different room than the one you sit and do your computing, especially if it is a laser based one.

### Inger — 2014-07-29T22:25:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-printer.11414/post-135945>

We got a new printer this winter and it had wireless built in but I shut that off and use only the wired option. It should not be hard to find one that have both options? Just be sure you turn off/deinstall the wireless option....

### LilyIncex — 2020-06-24T19:36:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-printer.11414/post-290499>

had the same problem a not long time ago. I think that wireless printers are so comfy. I use it every day for my work. But two months ago I wanted to buy a new one for my mum. Thanks to these guys [https://www.mrdepot.ca](https://www.mrdepot.ca/) I bought a quality printer from a famous brand. They provide a bunch of quality office products such as printers and toner cartridges. Moreover, they have free delivery, and that has saved my money and time. My mum was absolutely pleased with her gift from me! So if u still are looking for a good printer, try to check out this site for more info!

### Katie Durham — 2020-06-25T17:36:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/wired-printer.11414/post-290519>

I thought WiFi on my new printer was turned off but my SmartPhone says otherwise. Worth checking occasionally what WiFi signals you can pick up from inside your home.

# Thread: heart attack in crossfit class
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Lahelada — 2014-07-29T17:48:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135922>

[http://www.infobae.com/2014/07/29/1583870-murio-mientras-crossfit-un-gimnasio-recoleta](http://www.infobae.com/2014/07/29/1583870-murio-mientras-crossfit-un-gimnasio-recoleta).

26 year old habitual gym attendee passed out during warm up and subsequently died of cardio respiratory failure. He was well known at the gym as he trained 2 to 3 times a week.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-30T01:22:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135974>

we will see more and more of this

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-30T01:25:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135976>

electrons robbed from peter (heart) to pay paul (muscles)

### Lahelada — 2014-07-30T01:56:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135978>

Yup. It caused quite a stir here . Warnings are going out everywhere that Crossfit is not for the unfit,check with a doctor etc. Only problem is that the guy was a rugby player,weightljfter and not a novice in Crossfit. But that does not jive with the current thinking so it must be tbat he was unprepared. Conceding that excessive exercise in our conditions of existence is harmful would indeed upset the apple cart.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-30T02:12:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135981>

"fitness" is not really a thing, it is only a bunch of measurable variables at its core
just because fitness is better than none, does not mean that more must be better

### Jack Kruse — 2014-07-30T03:16:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-135995>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=135976)

This is what I think.........

### JoeBranca — 2014-07-31T20:58:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/heart-attack-in-crossfit-class.11417/post-136318>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=135981)
>
> "fitness" is not really a thing, it is only a bunch of measurable variables at its core
> just because fitness is better than none, does not mean that more must be better

I'll never forget attending a MovNat certification workshop held in an L.A. Crossfit... First and only time I've been in a crossfit gym. Kudos to the CF for hosting and being open to Movnat principles but the application of Movnat to these contexts will always be severely limited due to the incompatible philosophies at play. It was cognitive dissonance for me... the kicker I noticed was a large sign that said "DO SOMETHING YOU HATE TODAY". Of course, because movement is primarily about getting results and \*should\* be joyless and traumatic to the psyche. Sense of accomplishment comes later with measurable results, just another competitive rat race for so many... I truly hope more crossfitters find their primal way despite these misguided rally cries.

# Thread: tick

**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2014-07-29T18:01:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick.11418/post-135923>

Hi, I just removed a tick nymph from my child's foot. In my area apart of borrelia, tick born encephalitis is a risk. he is not vaccinated against encephalitis. Unfortunately the nymph was too small and after removal there was eventually nothing left to be sent to the laboratory for analysis. I am just wondering what to do having chronic lyme myself...

### Inger — 2014-07-29T22:04:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick.11418/post-135941>

I only know about the lyme issue, that you should see turn red in a circle around the bite if it is borrelia. Then take antibiotics if you want to. But the encephalitis I do not know much about.....
i do guess it is pretty rare? I would just follow my child extra closely next days and weeks to see if he changes in any way, and if, then take him to a good doc?
I bet if he eat good and is plenty outside in the sun and grounding in the nature his body could get rid of pretty much stuff just by itself

### kovita — 2014-07-30T14:43:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick.11418/post-136082>

Inger, he is now all days long out in the sun and in the nature with almost none emf. He has been eating consistently well for at least a year, has great vitD levels, so I hope even if there was something in the bloody tick, he would manage by himself. I started to feed him raw onions and garlic. He is a brave tiny thing and eats it without major objections. i was thinking in adding echinacea, ginseng, beta glucanes and maybe astragalus for at least a month now to bring up first line part of immune system. Good bee products would be great, but this year the bee keepers around struggle and it is very difficult to get it from the area, which i would prefare.

### Inger — 2014-07-30T14:57:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick.11418/post-136083>

Sounds great Kovita! I bet he will be just fine! Maybe do not give him too many "herbs" because i guess children are more sensitive, even if they are natural....

If you want great honey products, order by Orkos.com. I only buy my honey products there, they are totally natural and do not feed the bees sugar in winter at all. Pretty expensive but I have not been able to find anything as good as theirs. They are in Germany. Honey is usually from Italy or Spain. They have great honeycombs too, and bee pollen too. All raw and natural
just check out their website. That is where i buy my nuts too, all raw and organic. They have great dried and fresh seaweeds too. And their fruit is divine, used to buy it when I ate it but I do not anymore, I avoid fruit. I might order papaya tho soon. I love fresh papaya eaten with seeds and skin... very healing - not much sugar either - they have all kind of organic veggies too

### kovita — 2014-07-30T15:35:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tick.11418/post-136085>

thanks, Inger, their catalog looks interesting. I see they sell durian. Gosh, how do they send it! It brings me back to my previous life in Singapore, where carrying durian in any form or package was strictly prohibited in the public transport. There were even really funny signposts all around metro and bus stations. It was my favourite fruit. There was a restaurant specialized in durian feasts exclusively, always fully booked. I may try papaya myself. I love papaya but the same as you I stopped all fruit with exceptions of some wild berries picked in the woods.

### Ken23 — 2014-07-29T19:55:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-135932>

I start this journal after posting this 11 months ago on the forum:

[http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/26-with-bipolar-disorder.8088/](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/26-with-bipolar-disorder.8088/)

I wasn't sure how many people would respond or if any of this would work. At the time it felt a bit like it was a shot in the dark. But now, after 11 months, reading Dr. K's blogs and other members posts, I can say this has been, without a doubt, a success. And, I want to thank everyone who has responded to a question Iv had but especially want to thank Caroline, Cantweight, Gretchen and of course Dr. Kruse.

Without the initial responses from them I never would have given this a chance and never would have reached the level of health I am at now.

I recently became employed at Whole Foods, seemed like a fitting place to apply, and in time will be able to afford the webinars lol. I've used all of the resources here for free and want to show my appreciation.
