### Ken23 — 2014-08-03T04:06:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-136573>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=136133)
>
> How it fits is easy.......its the cysteine part. Re read EE12. Then read this paper:[http://www.davidwheldon.co.uk/NAC.html](http://www.davidwheldon.co.uk/NAC.html)

**When glutathione drops, it implies that you are using up your cysteine stores too quickly.** It turns out using up cysteine too fast makes you age faster than your biologic age and makes you ill on the way to accomplishing that task.

This quote from EE12 resonates with me. I believe in my case my bipolar disorder was ultimately caused by low blood sugar- insulin issues- cortisol- glutathione- inflammation. Is this some semblance of the right cause? I probably have them in the wrong order.

I have eaten ketogenic for 11 months( first time in my life), eaten massive amounts of seafood. Only recently have I added back incarbohydrates from fruits in season.... What I have noticed these last three weeks with adding back in fruits in season is that my brain ( mood, thinking, problem solving) has improved to a dramatic degree. To such an extent that I almost cannot articulate accurately the state of mind im in. A psychiatrist would label this MAnia. And it is but without the negative mania. I write this post after having plenty of carbs today( following 11 months of strict ketogenic epi paleo). The recent introduction of sulfur has TURNED everthing on for me. But it is the MSM supplement Iv been taking, MSM has no cysteine. Cysteine being a sulfur bearing amino acid, how does it factor in if the MSM has worked so well and raw eggs, pork, do not have the same affect? It seems I lose this natural high once my blood sugar drops back down...... But recapture back with a epipaleo meal with MSM

### Inger — 2014-08-03T09:55:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-136589>

Thought this was interesting but i am not educated enough to be able to judge the article much...
(anyways I love raw eggs... i just had raw chevice + raw hazelnuts for breakfast and then I still wanted something and went into the kitchen and made me egg yolk foam for dessert of 3 egg yolks and 1/4 teaspoon honey.. ohh yummy... and then I just drank the whites - they were yummy too, a bit salty!)

[http://www.immunehealthscience.com/cysteine.html](http://www.immunehealthscience.com/cysteine.html)

> Cysteine is present in all high-protein foods: all meats and poultry, dairy and eggs, quinoa, buckwheat.
>
> Small amounts of cysteine can be found in other plant sources: broccoli, Brussels sprouts, red and yellow bell peppers, onions, garlic.
>
> In food cysteine is bound into protein molecules by amide (peptide) bonds. **High temperatures during cooking break down these bonds and destroy bioactivity of cysteine.**
>
> **If plant sources of cysteine are consumed raw, then harsh stomach acids break down these bonds.** Free cysteine either gets snatched up by stomach and intestinal bacteria (they need it, too!), or if free cysteine survives the trip into the bloodstream, it cannot enter the cells.
>
> For cysteine to bypass the stomach acids, one will have to eat some 50 lbs. of raw veggies a day, or – 4-5 lbs. of raw meat, which is out of the question.
>
> Study “Immunoenhancing property of dietary whey protein in mice: role of glutathione” led by Dr. Bounous and Dr. Batist and published in _Clinical and Investigavive Medicine_ demonstrated that mice fed dietary (free) cysteine showed no positive immunological response.
>
>
>
> _”Cysteine has trouble surviving the trip from your mouth to your cells unless it’s part of a larger molecule or protein.”
> Dr. Jimmy Gutman “Glutathione. Your Key To Health”_
>
>
>
>
> And there is one food source out there with very interesting qualities where cysteine molecule remains intact during digestion.
>
> Raw cow’s milk (or rather, milk whey) contains three highly bioactive proteins: lactoferrin, serum albumin and alpha lactalbumin. **These proteins contain exceptional amounts of cysteine. And most importantly, in the form that can enter cells: each cysteine molecule is bonded with another cysteine molecule by a disulfide bond or bridge:**
>
>
>
> { "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }
>
>
>
>
> This paired unit is now called **cystine** (note the difference in spelling). It is pronounced /sis-‘ta-yn/. Cystine can easily enter the cell where it breaks down back into two cysteine molecules and participates in the formation of glutathione.
>
> **These disulfide bonds are very fragile and easily denatured by heat and mechanical stress.** Pasteurization of milk multiple times at high temperatures before it reaches your table, as well as mechanical stress during centrifuging, destroy these bonds.
>
> It makes cysteine from supermarket milk (and all mainstream dairy for that matter) absolutely useless for building glutathione. Humans outsmarted themselves by ruining the only viable food source of cysteine. For generations unprocessed milk, cheeses and yogurts used to be our ancestors’ sources of this critical glutathione precursor.
>
>
>
> _”So I have come down to rescue them from the hand of the Egyptians and to bring them up out of that land into a good and spacious land, a land flowing with **milk** and honey…”
> Exodus 3:8, NIV_

I sometimes buy me raw sheep or goat cheeses.... and i eat around 200 grams at once. I love it. I think dairy is not so good for me but maybe i sometimes need an extra boost of cysteine, who knows!

### nonchalant — 2014-08-04T21:17:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-136733>

I have a relative that cannot eat garlic, and has difficulty with many onions. Your investigations interest me. It is because of this relative that I pretty much stopped eating onion for a long period in my life. Just easier to stop cooking with it. After avoiding them for a decade or so, I found I no longer liked them, so it took DH a long time to get me to come around.

This relative cannot eat garlic in any form, and has problem with green onions, chives, shallots. His stomach really bothers him for a long time if the ingredients for something he normally eats has changed to include garlic, even if it is not enough to be in the ingredient list. He can eat sweet yellow & white onions raw, but in moderation, and not if they have been refrigerated after cutting them open. He can eat cooked yellow & white onions fairly well, even if they have been refrigerated or frozen.

### Alex97232 — 2014-08-05T19:42:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-136864>

> [Ken23 said:](/goto/post?id=136817)
>
> I'm thinking this might be something linked to gut bacteria/ candida.

Hi Ken,
Thinking you may be right. For years, based on an allergy sensitivity blood test, about 15 years ago, I avoided garlic. Allergy symptoms, including "allergy induced asthma" receded dramatically--got off of a lot of asthma drugs. I think I was just avoiding the herx reaction from the garlic. I now eat at least 2 cloves of crushed garlic with every meal. Oh, and at the same time I stopped the garlic and about 6 other reaction producing foods, I began to do yoga weekly, then, daily (learned how to breathe differently), stopped anything with sulfates added, including wine and beer and any other alcohol (a.m .headaches went away), quit going to pulmonalogist, stopped needing antibiotics for the quarterly sinus and chest infections), quit the steroid inhalers and the allergist M.D. who told me I would never be able to go without them, began regular weekly acupuncture, etc.
Bottom line: I was sick--didn't understand "leaky gut", but I knew there had to be a connection between my having become food sensitive to all of my favorite foods (eggs, garlic, tomatoes, bananas, kidney beans, spinach).
Without the garlic, candida took up a much greater residence in my gut.... Sorry so much about my own story--but, maybe some resonates for you and your friend--everybody's n=1 is different.
So, why would our gut bacteria be so freaky out of balance? Why do our bellies hurt? I believe Jack's got it nailed.

### Ken23 — 2014-08-05T22:24:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kens-journal.11419/post-136890>

Woke up today and thought about making salmon for breakfast but was feeling a little lazy. Decided to only have sulfur, a hand full of broccolli sprouts , Jarrows Broccomax supplement and a teaspoon of MSM. Was not sure how quickly I would get hunger cravings or how my body would react in general but it was by all accounts a successfull experiment. I went to the beach and had one of the clearest thinking days I can remember. Along with a great overall positive outlook. I was able to drink 3 liters of water without needing to use the bathroom! No solid food has been consumed in 16 hrs. Only running on good quality water and a ton of sulforaphane and organic sulfur from the MSM. Energy levels are perfectly steady and didn't burn from the sun at all. Thank God for sulfur

Australian "health" proponent Belle Gibson wrote "The Whole Pantry" app after being Dx with brain tumour
from watching her instagram feed last year it appears she spends every day indoors on the laptop making this app, travelling in the off times to a) other countries or b) other cities to appear on hyped up TV shows talking about "the next big game changer in health"

check out some of the "whole food" images she puts up

This just really hit home for me..... It is all about being curious. Dr. K. Has said that a million times! I have always gotten that ..... But now I get why people I know bore me to tears. Almost no one I know was curious to know why I had gone to Florida .... How could you not be curious about all the wonderful stuff I learned? They are just never really curious about anything.

I always get agitated and have to move on .... Don't have time to waste! I used to always feel bad about this - like it is my failing .....I am so grateful for everything I learn here.

I always think of all the old timers who wouldn't dream of having a microwave or a cell phone ..... They intuitively know something ....but are just thought to be old and crazy and stupid.

### lavendercat — 2014-07-30T14:17:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136077>

look. sorry. ive had enough of this. ive never seen such a load of self righteous nonsense. its not a competition to see who is the most right and the loser dies of cancer.

you will probably ban me for saying this. but im sick of the new age bullshit mixed in with good common sense and mind bending physics. anyone who "doesnt get it" obviously isnt eating enough fish.

sorry. i like some of the theories but you are all unbelievably pushy. i would rather eat a bit less fish, live less long, and be slightly less annoying.

### NeilBB — 2014-07-30T16:05:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136086>

> [lavendercat said:](/goto/post?id=136077)
>
> its not a competition to see who is the most right and the loser dies of cancer.

Are you sure about that? Look around.

Life is by definition a struggle for survival that involves both collaboration and competition. Always. You can choose with whom you wish to collaborate. But you can't always choose your enemy or the battlefield on which the struggle occurs.

Cancer is a common landmine on the battlefield of modern life. The people on this site are trying to avoid being blown up by that and similar modern landmines. This requires substantial investment of time and effort today. It also requires intellectual confidence. Optimal is not for everyone. Society creates dogma instead for the consumption of the masses.

### bionaut — 2014-07-30T16:23:13+0300

The information here is founded off of old and new physics. Nature is organized around these laws. Not obliging these is sort of like riding a bike with square wheels. You can go down the street but you won't get as far as someone who understands optimal principles based off of physics tied to natures laws.

This site is loaded with people that have had to disconnect themselves from their old lives and tried something long term for growth. Of course you are free to take it or leave it. If you are fed up then stick with the physics. The annoyingness is because their views are not congruent with yours. If you stick around long enough you might figure out why people think these things..

### Shijin13 — 2014-07-30T23:45:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136179>

> [cantweight said:](/goto/post?id=136111)

There are a lot of people here that just plain give a shit about others and are frustrated when they can't see what we've seen regardless of our own personal level of understanding. The heartbreaking desire to just shake some people and say wake up and look at this can come across as self righteous I guess.
>
> **This is precisely where those of us that give a shit need to be aware of when we shouldn't for our own self preservation.**

This is the hardest thing to do. we've got to let it go, and not give a shit about anyone but ourselves. Selfish isn't it. Though I think @[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) would agree with me, from the perspective of Ayn Rand - Selfishness is about striving for the greatness and the potential within you, no one else. Can't do it for them. you can only do it for you...

It sucks when you're faced with a situation that you don't know how to handle, or that you never expected. But you either give up, or pick yourself off and live life to its fullest to achieve optimal... some of us will get there, some of us will get 1/2 way, and others will turn back.

Look at @[MrPinkies](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5926/) Kate's effing rocking it at optimal...

so you either make the sacrifices needed to embrace change and survival... or you don't . _**Mediocrity has a gravitational effect to all but a few**_ \- Bergen. **Mediocrity advocates moderation. And in Nature's laws Moderation will kill you. Mother Nature isn't kind and gentle. She's cruel and ruthless. Eat or be eaten.** Kill or be Killed. Either ways She's coming for you, the question is will you be prepared, when she knocks on your door?

### MrPinkies — 2014-07-31T07:49:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136279>

"Now you know Audrey why I always say not everyone will make it. The attitude often predicts your aptitude in success."

I made it... and I'm maintaining it...

Attitude is EVERYTHING.. Whether you can or can't, you're right. Seriously, that is exactly how it is. If health is important to you, great, there's no problem... but if it truly is not a priority in your life, then, you will find some excuse to why you can't or won't.. My case of losing 165+ lbs now.. I couldn't have done it without changing the way I thought... I first had to stop myself from negative thoughts. If you think you CAN, you will do it.. I had a very hard time believing that I would be anywhere under 200 lbs again.. I am FAR from being 200 lbs now.. In fact.. I started out in a size 26 and now I am down into a size 4... This forum kept me going.. I am here to learn and grow.. and hopefully impact and inspire others.. I don't feel alone anymore.. When behavior started to change with Autism stuff (especially with my husband), I was/am unstoppable.

One more thing, Lavender.. I don't like fish... or seafood.. I puke every time I have it.. but, I think you've missed the point entirely.. Timing of meals, good water, clean eating, lots of sun, extra barefoot outside... Everything is in your control.. You control your food... you control your light bulb color/electric breaker.. you control when/etc you use computers and cellphones and other electrics.. and again.. if it's not important to you, you will find some excuse.. but... I have to say.. most of us feel so awesome that the thought of ever returning back to the old ways is damn near impossible..

For me, I LOVE having control.. I LOVE having big goals and dreams.. Life is short.. but has so many possibilities..

### Linz — 2014-07-31T12:29:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136292>

Perhaps you all should read what LC said in the feedback section just a few days ago.

LC I have huge respect for newbies trying to make sense of this enormous jigsaw puzzle where everything is so interconnected and a wrong piece can sabotage the rest, but it really does come back to being able to collect and keep energy by replenishing DHA and respecting nature's rhythms in a safe place (emf) - cold water and the sea and it's contents are a great shortcut. It takes time, up to two years I think, to adapt completely to being a good fat burner and you will have hiccups on the way. Wolfgang Lutz has simple but valuable advice about the transition in his book Life Without Bread which can be updated with the information here. Many of the problems you encounter on the way are actually a sign of improvement but sometimes you will get worse to get better, and that can be overwhelming when you start to realise how far out you are in taking control of your own health. Remember you may well need that antibiotic for a UTI but there are ways to offset the damage it can do here and most infections should become a thing of the past if you stay the course.

Looking back to see small but very real health changes (and asking here for reasons why or the best place to find the explanation) should help give you the reason to persevere. Look out for clearer head, skin improvements, finding you don't need to eat at a normal meal time or your muscles don't hurt after doing something more strenuous than usual, pain from an injury resolves quickly, or waking up and jumping out of bed first thing in the morning feeling refreshed - big changes when you find the explanation ( and all the pushy people here are waiting to point out the relevant blog because they really want you to catch up - the doctors and scientists have had to swallow their pride and relearn too).

Once you start to have a deeper understanding and feel some good changes yourself, it becomes harder and harder not to 'criticise' those with their own websites giving very narrow, half-informed but confident advice that doesn't encourage questions, even if those giving the advice are actually leading more and more to realise convention may be far off the mark.

Keep going. Finding the right place was the biggest step. You don't need to trust anyone, just listen for yourself ( expect a barrage of passionate advice if you ask for help) and take time to see changes.

### TimB — 2014-08-01T02:01:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136334>

> [Josh (Paleo Osteo) said:](/goto/post?id=135969)
>
> Australian "health" proponent Belle Gibson wrote "The Whole Pantry" app after being Dx with brain tumour
> from watching her instagram feed last year it appears she spends every day indoors on the laptop making this app, travelling in the off times to a) other countries or b) other cities to appear on hyped up TV shows talking about "the next big game changer in health"
>
>
>
> another case of a little bit of knowledge being a bad thing, meanwhile missing the massive EMF link and eating carbs all day
> but it doesnt matter because its "real food"
>
> the knock on effect of this kind of mass spread information is appauling
>
> im sorry for her health concerns but this is really what things like paleo have become in the mainstream

Cash flow 'needs' and identity desires have brought many individuals and companies down this road.
Companies like Whole Paycheck have misled many with their marketing programs of Whole this and Whole that.
Having come out of the 'natural' foods industry, I watched the gradual metamorphosis of the 'mission' statements from genuine concern and curiosity about physical health to a total focus on stockholder financial health.
It was rather disheartening, but also made it very clear that it's up to each person to decide what they want to do with their life.
And all we can do is offer up what we know to those who are interested and what sticks is going to stick or it's not.
It is pretty clear that most folks on this site held onto their curiosity in spite of the barrage of marketing hype, propaganda and B.S. that they had to wade through.

### Stella — 2014-08-01T04:20:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136345>

I haven't been on the forum for quite a while, mostly because I've been living the life that just a few months ago I didn't think was possible. Let me explain. I was one of those exercise, Crossfit and diet nuts who crashed hormonally and ended up with an autoimmune disorder.

What changed... I did. No one else has been able to scientifically explain why I ended up ruining my health... Down to the mitochondrial level. Except for Jack. When I found this website I was sick and on the verge of applying for disability at work. But I followed the protocols, even going to Orlando where I was able to get firsthand from Jack life changing information.

Today after after searching and having gone through 4 anti aging docs, following the protocols, eating more oysters, sardines and seafood than I care to admit... I am finally retaining electrons.

It took putting many things in my life on hold. To this day I still can't exercise regularly as I'm still not recovering well after exercise. But I'm improving month to month. Progress is slow but steady but I understand it took years for my body to get to rock bottom. I do less and my family has to pick up the slack. In return they are able to see a healthier wife and mother.

I am sleeping like a rock, enjoying life, my brain works and my sex life is great. I radiate health and vitality.

Many people that know me are surprised at my recovery and a few even have asked how I've managed to turn my health around. So few are curious???? How many have done what I've done? NO ONE!!!!

I continue to be a starfish. Fortunately I understand and can explain the science. But for most people no grains, no dairy, no nightshades, low lights, eating on a schedule seems to be too difficult. From my perspective, the price I've paid is so small compared to the life I'm now living.

### caroline — 2014-08-01T08:10:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136350>

I am so very proud of you Stella.....you took everything on board at jackkruse.com ........ and did what you needed to do.

You are a spectacular woman in every sense of the word.... And you are enjoying every single second of every single day!

I can only imagine what the future holds for you and your family.

Your sons now see what a beautiful, happy, healthy, energetic woman looks like...and you are even learning to hug more and better!

### Stella — 2014-08-01T22:38:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136402>

Thanks Hope. It's been a learning experience. Mostly learning to listen to my body.

The last one is a keeper. I'm on thyroid and BHRT. I would've kept on looking had this doctor not worked out. I was on BHRT from the first doc but not all BHRT is the same. And not all docs are willing to form a good patient doctor partnership. This last doctor has given me the responsibility of listening to my body and titrating my therapy. I have his email and we communicate frequently.

There are small pieces of the puzzle or the quilt but everything works together. Granted I'm still on a TNF blocker but that's not all I needed. I still didn't feel optimal. I knew what feeling well felt like and I wasn't about to explain it all away with aging. So I've changed my diet to accommodate an auto immune condition. I spend more time outdoors. I spent countless hours in a cold swimming pool, for s Florida that is. Getting lots of sun. Eating plenty of oysters and seafood. Cutting out lights after dark and lighting candles. Cut down on EMF. More oxy. All of these changes together made all the difference.

I can honestly say that I've turned the clock back at least 20 years. In a competitive profession where younger people are looking to get your job, I can safely say that in my present state of mind, experience trumps youth. Especially if those younger colleagues are not optimal.

Only thing I can say is, if you are still not optimal keep working at it and never stop searching and improving. The hard work pays off.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-02T17:35:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-136516>

> [View attachment 821](https://forum.jackkruse.com/attachments/821/)

People will say we're being crude or arrogant or unsympathetic pointing this out...........but guess how humans learn? From errors.
Don’t regret your errors or errors of others. . A subtle thought that is in error may yet give rise to fruitful inquiry that can establish truths of great value to your future.

The right thing isn't always real obvious to the rest of the world. Sometimes the right thing for one person is the wrong thing for someone else. And everything, in retrospect, is obvious to those who use forethought. How you think makes you great. 99.9% of humans use hindsight to dictate their actions. Some of us train to see the unseen.

The key is realizing what holds your thoughts captive to keep you blind.

i am currently writing a blog on this whole topic. yes i may be cast as a vilain with no heart, but it in a time when anyone can post anything from anywhere on any topic, we have too many health and wellness "experts"
some of these people have hundreds and thousands of followers who cast them in the same light as an angel, following their every word
if someone is touting a vegetarian diet for brain cancer, maybe these followers will adopt said diet for "prevention" all the while checking for updates every 5 minutes on their timeline....
us professionals have to be registered under governing bodies to regulate our actions to cause no harm to patients, yet this is alowed to hapen daily
the point is people need to be better informed before they start pushing their ideas to the unsuspecting masses, or the masses need to wake up and do some of their own work

Audrey the new age stuff does nothing for me as well. Ive been very fortunate financially and when you do mega deals (over 50m in my mind) then you learn to be hard because the other side is as well. Im generally a dont give a shit for bullshit person. Talking about love for the universe and jumping unicorns. Not interested.

But what I have learned is a lot and applied it personally. I dont care for jacks facebook updates generally with the flowery stuff and he doesnt give a shit about my opinion. Does he care. No. Do I care. No.

But that doesnt mean you cant learn. If you dont like the new age flowery stuff like me then let it wash over you. Dont stress about it. Move on. A gas deal I was doing feel through. Would have upgraded the ferrari. Do I give a shit. No. Move on. Next deal.

Do the same on here. Analyse. Ingest. Decipher. And throw out the shit you dont like. You might learn something new.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2015-12-19T07:14:57+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-paleo-style-whole-food-proponent-with-cancer.11420/post-184494>

**Belle Gibson’s mum slams troubled childhood claims as ‘a lot of rubbish’**
May 18, 20152:24pm

Disgraced wellness blogger Belle Gibson blamed a troubled childhood but her mother says it is just more lies.

STAFF WRITERS[news.com.au](http://www.news.com.au/)

-   Email a friend

**“I’VE never seen her cry in her life ... I can’t tell you how embarrassed we are about what she has done”**

That’s the brutal assessment of disgraced wellness blogger Belle Gibson’s mother, who has spoken for the first time about the storm engulfing the 23-year-old after she admitted lying about having terminal brain cancer to launch a cookbook and health app business.

Natalie Dal-Bello [told the _Australian Women’s Weekly_ she was “embarrassed” by Gibson, who she said had dragged her family through the mud in her attempt to blame her cancer lies on a troubled childhood.](http://www.aww.com.au/latest-news/in-the-mag/belle-gibsons-mother-speaks-to-the-australian-womens-weekly-20596)

Gibson said as a five-year-old girl she had been forced to care for Mrs Dal-Bello — who has multiple sclerosis — and run the household, while also looking after her autistic brother.

“What a lot of rubbish,” Mrs Dal-Bello (formerly Gibson) told the magazine, saying the only truth to the story was her MS.

“Her brother is not autistic and she’s barely done a minute’s housework in her life,” she said.

“I’ve practically worked myself into an early grave to give that girl everything she wanted in life.”

Mrs Dal-Bello said she had not been in touch with her estranged daughter for four years, and had no idea of the success she had found as a wellness entrepreneur until it came publicly crashing down.

“I can’t tell you how embarrassed we are about what she has done,” she said.

“She just plucked bits and pieces of other people’s medical problems and assumed them as her own She had a heart problem growing up, but that was it.

“She doesn’t seem to be sorry. There doesn’t appear to be any remorse. I’ve never seen her cry in her life.”

Belle Gibson has kept her head down since her story unravelled. Pic: Rob Leeson_Source:_News Corp Australia

Mrs Dal-Bello said her daughter “always had ideas above her station”.

She described Belle having expensive tastes and living beyond her means, and being “addicted to her computer”.

“She used to fall asleep with it. Always on Facebook, always online,” she said.

“But that world is not real, it’s not healthy.”

Gibson’s brother Nick, who does not have autism, has also spoken out telling _AWW_ he is “really upset” by his sister’s stories.

“I’m disgusted with Belle and what she’s done,” he said.

“It’s about attention. She’s always been like this.”

Gibson, who admitted she didn’t have cancer in an interview with the same magazine last month, blamed a difficult upbringing for her lifelong struggle with the truth.

“I think my life has just got so many complexities around it and within it, that it’s just easier to assume \[I’m lying\]” [Gibson told _AWW_.](http://www.aww.com.au/latest-news/in-the-mag/belle-gibson-teaser-20269)

“I am still jumping between what I think I know and what is reality. I have lived it and I’m not really there yet.

“If I don’t have an answer, then I will sort of theorise it myself and come up with one. I think that’s an easy thing to often revert to if you don’t know what the answer is.”

Gibson also said she was unsure if her mother was alive or dead. Mrs Dal-Bello said her daughter needed to apologise and dedicate years of her life to repaying the debt she owed, including volunteering for cancer charities.

While she denied much of her daughter’s story, it is clear Gibson did have a somewhat complicated childhood. Both Gibson and her mother agreed on the story of her leaving home at age 12 when her mum and brother said she struck up a friendship and moved in with a man in his 60s.

On Gibson’s absent father, Mrs Dal-Bello described him only as “a sperm donor”.

Despite their anger, Gibson’s family say they would like to reconnect with her.

“I would tell her I love her, that she needs to take care of her son, but at the end of the day, she need to take responsibility for her actions.”

Brother Nick said he would like to see Belle again, but doesn’t think he would be able to ever forgive her.

“The things I’d like to ask to her face now are, what did I do to deserve this? Please explain, why did you do this to our family?” he said.

Gibson was eventually [dumped by her publisher and Apple](http://www.news.com.au/lifestyle/health/belle-gibson-cancer-confession-sparks-fury/story-fneuzlbd-1227316424925) after turning her terminal cancer tall tale into a popular cookbook and wellness app, The Whole Pantry.

She even managed to fool the tech giant into signing her up for the launch of the Apple Watch, where The Whole Pantry was to feature.

But it all came crashing down after charities she claimed to have donated money to through the sale of the app confirmed they hadn’t received a cent from her.

Gibson went into hiding, her business collapsed and she reportedly had to put her Melbourne house on the market.

# Thread: The color .....red....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 18
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### caroline — 2014-07-30T09:45:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136048>

What does the color red ....or any color mean to you?

Is it a power color? A confidence color? How do you feel when you wear it?

Notice Dr. K's new FB picture .... Clearly he feels powerful/confident - or at the very least - in a very good place!
And he very likely has his red shorts on.

So when we start our day ....do we reach for a particular color because of how we feel?

I had forgotten that I have owned a couple of red outfits years ago...

One night we were in our favorite restaurant in Nassau - we had been going there for years - we knew all the staff and they had always treated us with extreme generosity and care.

This night I had a lovely red outfit ....and red shoes...... And they completely forgot about us! I kept thinking how could they overlook us when I am dressed from head to toe in red???

I think this says way more about me ...and much less about them ......

Did I just fade into the woodwork? even tho I was wearing red? I had no presence regardless of what I was wearing?

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-07-30T10:27:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136051>

red popped into my head yesterday regarding sunburn

do we go reflect red because we have obtained enough UV (blue) - is it therefore a signal that the cache is full?
much like how the brain appears pink because it functions on absorbing blue light?

### Inger — 2014-07-30T10:51:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136054>

Haha.... that is weird Audrey! Hmmm... I seriously have no clue... but i do think no color or clothes can mask our energy.........

I do like colors, and I love red too, I like different colors, like some days I want just white and another day red.... i have so many different colored dresses
i like pastel colors too, like rose....
but i think colors fit differently depending on your skin / hair tone and appearance... it is a kind of art to get good at using colors that fits you

I have discovered gray fits me very well.... what does that tell?

Maybe the red color did not fit you too well... maybe it was too strong and took over? So one could see only the red, not you? I think you are blond like me and if one were black it would be a different thing, I love when black people wear strong colors, it fits them so well!

### Sue-UK — 2014-07-30T11:10:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136057>

Caroline, I have a friend who helps people find what colours suit them, and how to emphasise good points etc ...she told me that if people tell me a dress looks fabulous, it's possibly the wrong dress , even if the colour choice is right ...If people notice me in the dress (think I look great, really well, healthy, glowing etc), its probably the right dress ....... when trying on clothes now I ask myself am I wearing the dress, or is the dress wearing me .....

### caroline — 2014-07-30T12:38:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136063>

Sue ..... I totally get that! I really notice now if I am trying things on.....sometimes it is overpowering - not that it doesn't look good - but it overtakes me and I get lost and I don't want to be lost anymore!

I would never have noticed that before for some reason...maybe I chose to hide inside my clothes? Maybe that was the preferred state of being that I wanted ...I didn't know who I was..... especially with all the crazy weight gains. I was lost.

I have a lot of black clothes - from before my renovation \[as Jack would say\] ....and I cannot wear them - I put them on and take them right back off again!

They seem to drag me down to a place I absolutely won't go anymore. They actually give me the creeps - like a death sign or symbol or an unease that isn't acceptable to me any longer.

And yet ....what could be nicer than a little black dress? Maybe I am not where I need to go yet? I need to wear the clothes - not them me. Maybe one of these days I will put on a little black dress and feel awesome ...I will really know who I am. Of course - it is going to need to be short and low cut.......

What could be better than glowing with health and vitality.....but then - does wearing something red support that?

I definitely think ...YES

### caroline — 2014-07-30T12:53:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136064>

There is another component to this ..... with all the C/T and sunshine and seafood etc - my skin tone has changed.

People now tell me I am so lucky because I have an olive skin tone and don't burn. This has never, ever, ever been the case!
I used to be so envious of a person with an olive skin tone.

So I have just circled back to being healthy and glowing and being aware of who I am .... more and more every day  and, of course, I want to wear red now!

Maybe we could all change our avatars .... and wear red!

and .... of course, every guy needs some red speedos ......

### Sue-UK — 2014-07-30T13:47:42+0300

### nicld — 2014-07-30T17:22:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-color-red.11422/post-136097>

I am wearing red today  Now that I am getting more sun I seem to be a deeper shade of pink. I saw my mother yesterday and she commented on how I must have really had a sunburn on my chest since it appeared to be very red, I was not burned. I was wearing pink so the pink just made it look red.

I hardly every wear just black, it has to have some other color with it. I look better in warmer hues of colors and my favorite color is salmon or coral. I look really really good in that color.

### caroline — 2014-07-31T01:10:36+0300

> -   Why are so many of the transport items and people in the photographs in Section 3.6 red in colour?
>
>

# Thread: Oral Temperatures During CT
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oral-temperatures-during-ct.11426/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Valerie — 2014-08-01T03:25:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oral-temperatures-during-ct.11426/post-136342>

So I measured my (oral) temperature with mercury thermometer during CT for first time (after 5 months CT).
Within about 5 minutes of getting into a 12 deg C bath my temperature went from 98 deg F to 99.2 deg F
Body temperature was steady for about 30 minute. For the next 15 minute (through 45 minutes total) my temperature dropped to 98.8 deg F as the bath temperature cooled to 9 deg C.

I think this is a good sign that I have some BAT. I do feel warmer throughout the day. Just want to wear sleeveless tops. Also notice I don't shiver quite as much after CT, but that could be because it is summer.

Does anyone else have temperature values to compare with mine?

### nonchalant — 2014-08-01T05:30:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oral-temperatures-during-ct.11426/post-136348>

I did take some oral temps long ago. My temp also rose a little while after getting in the water. I think it also went back to normal after a while. Sorry I don't have anything specific!

### QuantumRadical — 2014-08-07T20:57:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/oral-temperatures-during-ct.11426/post-137182>

Stay in long enough and you will get hypothermic no matter what. Even the Iceman and Jack have limits for tolerating the cold. I notice a distinct spike in generated heat coming off the top of my head within seconds of hitting the cold water. I may have to start using my nice IR thermometer and measure that phenomena.

With a bath temp starting at around 45-47 degrees, I can usually stay in the water fully submerged up to my chin for around 30-35 minutes without starting to shiver. However, I have gone as long as 50 minutes without starting to shiver (I was in the zone), and after a bit of mild shivering I usually get out. Again, the point is not to hurt yourself, but to kick off the bio-quantum-chemical reactions to illicit the right response for optimality. I have tried even colder water, in fact right at 32 degrees, and don't think after those couple of sessions that it has any more value than the 45-56 degree water temperature. Just that you get out faster.

# Thread: Biohacking eyesight
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 73
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### NeilBB — 2014-08-01T19:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136383>

Does anyone have any experience or thoughts on naturally biohacking eyesight for better visual acuity? Aside from DHA, nutrition and avoiding corrective lenses, sunglasses, too much computer time, etc. Has anyone used the "[Bates Method](http://www.seeing.org/)" or similar resources or had significant success in this area either for nearsightedness or presbyopia? I am very interested in this personally.

### SeaHorse — 2014-08-01T21:05:55+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136391>

Talk about synchronicity…I was just floating around in the pond, with my glasses off, thinking that I would like to get back to reading the Bates stuff and pay attention to my eyes again and here is your post…but the short answer to your question is that I have noticed changes in my vision….but I haven't started any exercises, so it's due to other changes.

I think Nonchalant mentioned her vision improving too…..

I'll report back in a few weeks and let you know how the Bates approach is working is me...

### cantweight — 2014-08-01T22:33:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136401>

I think its fairly obvious at this point that I am not going to come at you with anything scientific at all, but I used to wear glasses for reading, and for driving. My vision was not severely impaired 20/30 and 20/40. It is now 20/20 has been for a few years.

I also used to be very photo sensitive to florescent lighting and sunlight, spent a lot of time in sunglasses attempting to ward of migraines......if I don a pair of sunglasses now its just to look cool

Shocked my eye doctor.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-02T00:47:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136418>

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bates\_method](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bates_method)

### ssj3 — 2014-08-02T11:19:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136477>

Maybe not exactly what you are after, but I was taking a supplement containing cyanidin 3-glucoside (C3G) (for muscle nutrient uptake), but it did have some studies regarding improved eyesight.

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19267871](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/19267871)
[http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2010...erry-Extract-For-Improved-Night-Vision\_01.htm](http://www.lef.org/magazine/mag2010/ss2010_Cutting-Edge-Berry-Extract-For-Improved-Night-Vision_01.htm)

### NeilBB — 2014-08-02T16:36:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136504>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=136418)
>
> [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bates\_method](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bates_method)

It doesn't seem that Bates was completely accurate in all that he said, but I wonder if he was directionally correct? There is a lot of interest in this area nowadays, and it seems at least somewhat plausible to me. Also, I wonder how much the nervous system can potentially compensate for subpar optics in visual processing...

I forced myself to go without either glasses or contacts for a whole day yesterday and although there was a lot of blurriness, I was able to function and my eyes felt a hell of a lot better than they usually do throughout the day.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-02T16:37:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136505>

If it opens your eyes to a new way is that a bad thing?

### NeilBB — 2014-08-02T16:55:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136509>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=136505)
>
> If it opens your eyes to a new way is that a bad thing?

Of course not. And I would love to be restored to somewhere near 20/20 without correction. Have worn glasses or contacts for myopia since 10 y/o. And now to make it worse, at age 46 I'm getting presbyopia on top of that which really really blows. If there's a way, I'm gonna find it...

### Sue-UK — 2014-08-03T12:26:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136604>

[http://www.jacobliberman.org/](http://www.jacobliberman.org/) might be worth a look at. I read "Take of Your Glasses and See" and still have "Light: Medicine of the Future."

### ssj3 — 2014-08-03T13:03:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136607>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=136604)
>
> [http://www.jacobliberman.org/](http://www.jacobliberman.org/) might be worth a look at. I read "Take of Your Glasses and See" and still have "Light: Medicine of the Future."

Might be similar... z-health has something called the 'vision gym'.

### NeilBB — 2014-08-03T15:20:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136612>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=136604)
>
> [http://www.jacobliberman.org/](http://www.jacobliberman.org/) might be worth a look at. I read "Take of Your Glasses and See" and still have "Light: Medicine of the Future."

Thanks. I'm actually looking at those now. Many interesting ideas. Well worth reading.

While he believes that both can improve, he distinguishes between central vision in dim light (doctors test) versus dynamic functional vision out in the world. I can already think of several mechanisms by which "functional vision" might potentially compensate for poor central vision.

### nicld — 2014-08-03T15:26:53+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136614>

I swear mine has been improving.

### SeaHorse — 2014-08-03T16:11:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136617>

> [Sue-UK said:](/goto/post?id=136604)
>
> [http://www.jacobliberman.org/](http://www.jacobliberman.org/) might be worth a look at. I read "Take of Your Glasses and See" and still have "Light: Medicine of the Future."

I just got shivers up my spine when I went on Jacob Liberman's site and found a testimonial quote in the consulting section from my old eye doctor Stanley Applebaum. I saw him briefly about forty years ago in my early teens. That was my first alternative exploration of working on my vision. Through exercises he helped get both my eyes working and focusing together . This helped a lot in school with things like spelling…I was always borderline dyslexic and still have to think about left and right…it doesn't come instantly. Clearly the connections between vision and bilateral brain function are ripe territory.

The Bates stuff has been reinterpreted a number of times, so the Wiki article is not reflective of what you would get from somebody today who teaches "Bates" techniques.

Very interesting stuff from Jacob Liberman….thanks Sue, you're my new north star for interesting books and people! Still waiting for my Staples book to arrive.

### Lahelada — 2014-08-03T16:26:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136619>

Neil, I also feel better with my glasses off. I do have a sense of feeling better with contacts but not quite as much as without glasses. Feeling better could also have to do with not making distances longer and objects smaller.

### Clayton — 2014-08-03T17:44:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-136626>

Last year when I went to the eye doc, I was astonished when he said my prescription has not changed. But to me my vision was a lot better. Perhaps it is better in sunlight, not in a dimly lighted room.
But this year it has gotten to the point where I don't like wearing my glasses at all. They change colors, somehow. Everything looks so "small" with them on, and I can feel my eyes strain to compensate. Perhaps they are over correcting.

This makes me think of the racketball class I had in college. I kept getting hit in the head, and my glasses would go flying. So I stopped wearing them. That made me even more uncoordinated, but I began to compensate after a while. I got better, and stopped taking hits, but I was reluctant to use my glasses again, because they would have thrown off my coordination again.

### WereBear — 2014-08-09T17:39:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-137382>

I didn't realize I needed reading glasses for a year... and got terrible eyestrain because of it. I used the Bates Method of complete darkness and "thinking about black" to relieve it quickly and completely -- my eyes looked like they'd been drenched in beet juice. And I got readers.

But I swing between .75 and 1.25 depending on what I'm doing, and only for reading print. I've not worn sunglasses in years, since reading about natural light and the eyes.

I would say the Bates Method is worth looking into based on my experience. Even now, it's the best way of resting my eyes I've ever run across, and I was an early adopter of ergonomic IT practice... monitor arms length or greater from my face, frequent breaks to look up at clouds and distant trees, remembering to blink.

### NeilBB — 2014-08-09T18:34:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-137386>

I have been varying my lens prescriptions and filters for several days now and experimenting with different tasks in different environments. Assimilating info and thinking about this a lot. I think Bates and Lieberman are partially but not completely correct about a lot of things. Bates knowledge of what the eye muscles do was not completely accurate. Lieberman is interesting, but prefers to leap into mystical preaching at times rather than scientifically exploring QED. I know a number of conventionally trained ophthalmologists and I think they are largely clueless about this whole thing.

The real issue involves optics, neurology, and QED among other things. Very few can integrate all that. Human vision is also a much more dynamic process than is generally recognized. A huge portion of the brain is involved in visual processing. I am very interested in particular in the difference between gazing at a _luminous_ object versus an _illuminated_ object. I think it's an enormous issue today. Hint: unless you printed this out on a piece of paper, you are looking directly into a luminous object right now, as we all now do much of the time, in fact. However, throughout history we evolved looking mostly at illuminated objects. It is not the same as, but is closely related to, the distinction between natural and artificial light. I think it's huge...

### NeilBB — 2014-08-09T19:23:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-137390>

I would say that artificial light has at least 3 distinct classes of problems I can think of right now (aside from other non-visual EMF considerations):

1\. Temporally inappropriate. Use at night (after sun is down). Circadian disrupter, of course.

2\. Frequency/Intensity inappropriate. Unnatural thinband artifical frequency spectrum, which is very different from natural sources. Newer "energy saving" bulbs even worse, and higher in bright high-intensity blue light, of course. This is biologically novel and likely damaging to body's protective processes because its light-sensing mechanisms are likely "confused" as to how much "light" is really there.

3\. Luminous vs Illuminated. In many cases the optical target is the source of the light itself. All monitors, phones, TVs, etc. But also much of what you see in a city at night. Illuminated signs. Street lights, etc. This is very different from the condition in which the sun saturates the environment with full-spectrum light and we look at purely reflected light between different objects to discern distinctions. With luminous objects, beams of limited frequency light are projected directly onto the retina, without environmental "tempering," so to speak. The contrast between the brightness of the screen or light and background environment is often dramatic and unnatural as well. Not too different from just shining a low-intensity blurry laser in the eye all the time. Who would shine a laser in their eye knowingly? Is what we are doing now qualitatively different or only quantitatively?

I think the eyes and brain evolved to sense background ambient light conditions and adjust focus, rod/cone activation, neural processing accordingly to discern objects bathed in light in the environment. This adaptation process does not occur instantaneously but is designed to occur gradually, as the sun sets and rises gradually. It seems to me that the above mechanisms would clearly over time be capable of disrupting those systems quite significantly and that this may contribute to all forms of eye disease including myopia, presbyopia, as well as cataracts, glaucoma, and retinal problems.

And when people look into computers, phones and TV's at night in dark ambient surroundings, all 3 of these mechanisms would be in full force simultaneously.

### NeilBB — 2014-08-09T20:18:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-137391>

As far as practical implementation, I am experimenting and working on that. It would seem obvious that replacing DHA and avoiding computer screens, especially at night and in dark interior environments should play a huge role. As much natural sunlight as possible should help.

I think there is a place for certain eye exercises-working on that. I think it makes sense to reduce corrective prescriptions when and if it is possible to do safely for the task at hand. I'm experimenting heavily with that now...

I think that in refractive problems, the brain may be "confused" as to how far away the target object actually is. If so, this may be able to be improved through training, at least in some cases. Corrective lenses would likely exacerbate, rather than help this problem.

I think background lighting when working on computers could be a big deal. I think there may be a role for protective eyewear when looking at screens, especially in low-lit ambient conditions. Paradoxically, I think maybe increasing ambient full spectrum incandescent lighting in the room when on TV or computer may actually be helpful. So as to reduce the potential contrast, which I think may be quite harmful. Looking at screens outside whenever possible should theoretically be best however. Will update more later when I have more results...

### thomasdoud@gmail.com — 2014-09-23T03:45:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-141663>

I am a developmental optometrist who treats patients with in office vision therapy. This is much more intensive than the Bates model and would glad to give you some input. For nearsightedness (myopia) you can reduce your prescription some if you are willing to put in a lot of work. Much of what you gain by doing this type of work is not a reduction in refractive error (eyeglass power) but and improved ability to interpret blur. All myopes have some extra focusing they are not relaxing and are in a sense wearing too strong of a prescription. This is only about 10% of most prescriptions. It goes away typically in late 40's or early 50's. For someone who is 60 you will not change your prescription but might get better at blur interpretation. My personal opinion and that of the patients I have personally worked with who tried these methods is that they are not worth the time invested. If you have a child with a new myopia diagnosis find a doc that does Ortho-K and stop it in its tracks. As far as presbyopia that is going to happen with all of us. It occurs because the lens gets too big and the zonular system no longer works. We used to think it was hardening of lens or weakness of muscle but neither of those are the major issue. All near point focusing is stressful to humans. Reducing that stress with lenses is very helpful. Quit reading or get the proper lenses if you want to be optimal. If you want to find a functional optometrist search at oepf.org or covd.org. As far as improving visual function there are nutritional issues and following all Dr. K's advice is critical but one proven method is to increase the lutein and zeaxanthine in your macula. Find someone who does MPOD (macular pigment optical density) testing to see if you are low and need fortified supplementation.

### nonchalant — 2016-05-06T19:40:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-194298>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=138459)
>
> I went to the eye doc today, and he was very surprised at my positive vision changes. In the past two years (I skipped going last year) my vision has changed from -3.50 (R) & -4.25 (L) to a current -3.25 (R) & -3.50 (L). But he was more surprised by my focusing ability. He said my range is very large now. He had me put on my old glasses and put on additional lenses. He said everything should be a blur, but I was able to focus through them. And I can focus very close to my eyes.
>
> Wow, all this, and without even exercising! I told him it's all the seafood and hydration. He said he has been increasing his fish consumption lately also.

More vision improvements: Yesterday my trip to the optometrist yielded a new prescription, -2.75 (R) and - 3.00 (L). Half a diopter improvement in both eyes in the past year and a half. He said with my focusing ability, I can actually wear a range of prescriptions, but I'd probably need something closer to this current prescription to see most comfortably at night. As if I drive at night, heh. It's interesting that my weaker eye is so close to my stronger eye. Maybe I'm using it more.

He wasn't very interested in my talk about sunshine and seafood though.

I am very interested in this personally.

One thing I hear from the Iris computer screen software developer is that using _**Arial**_, a smooth font was better to use for online reading... I've switched over to that default but then he also said to change to _**Times New Roman**_ for print reading---- I knew that from years ago but had forgotten it... I post and author on Arial but then save and print in Times New Roman.

**I am working on my vision too .**
I've been practicing _Palming_ when I'm in my **complete darkness time** before bed- that is when I listen to disks of Mp3 lessons.
I first heard about _Palming_ from **Meir Schneider** [https://self-healing.org/](https://self-healing.org/) and others.
I don't know the history of Palming ...perhaps from the Bates Method.

### Richard Watson — 2021-02-19T06:54:50+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-295675>

Reversing myopia has been my success story, a year ago I was using prescription glasses for short shortsightedness, at the time did not knew that myopia could be reversed till I started reading about active focusing by Glen Jeffery, a professor of neuroscience at University College London's Institute of Ophthalmology. He wrote about using a 670nm light a few minutes per day, I had nothing to loose so I bought a light [https://lifegivingstore.com/deep-red-670nm-handheld-device-lgs7/](https://lifegivingstore.com/deep-red-670nm-handheld-device-lgs7/) I use it every day, eyes closed. The results today are amazing, to be able to see clear again is a incredible feeling. I can still get a small amount of blur in the mid day sun, but if I concentrate, blink a few times i can refocus. Morning and evening is always 100% clear eyesight. I cant say if its the light or the active focusing that has done it, either way I will keep both up till they close the lid on me.
I know that I will have to keep exercising the focusing muscles for the rest of my life, that's cool, after all the same applies for the rest of the body too.
[https://endmyopia.org/how-to-finding-active-focus/](https://endmyopia.org/how-to-finding-active-focus/)

### TaylorF — 2022-07-21T20:51:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-314536>

Does anyone know Jack's current recommended blue light glasses brand? I have Ra Optics, UVEX, and EMR-tek, and I love the latter the most but the lenses are thin and let light in around the sides.

My mom is struggling with her eyesight and I want to get her the best-made brand for both day and night.

### Laudy Cincotta — 2022-07-22T03:49:21+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/biohacking-eyesight.11430/post-314538>

> [Taylor Freeman said:](/goto/post?id=314536)
>
> Does anyone know Jack's current recommended blue light glasses brand? I have Ra Optics, UVEX, and EMR-tek, and I love the latter the most but the lenses are thin and let light in around the sides.
>
> My mom is struggling with her eyesight and I want to get her the best-made brand for both day and night.

Hi [@Taylor Freeman](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/23982/) i don't know Jack's current recommendation.

At the start of my journey i bought Ra Optics (cost a bomb) and the quality was terrible so i turfed them, they were flimsy and broke (hope they have fixed some of these issues).

I used Australian brand BluBlox (now changed their name) ok but very pricey, wouldn't necessarily use them again if i found a better brand but i found them better than Ra.

I just purchased and love the ethics and quality of Viva Rays ([https://vivarays.com/](https://vivarays.com/)).

Owner Roudy has an engineering background and came to light via issues with his health ie ADD, ADHD, crap sleep etc and figured it out so he is on the same page as all mitochondriacs on this journey.

His product from an environmental perspective is paramount for me, the frames are made from sustainably harvested wood, they are beautiful (not plastic) and look great.

They don't wrap around but i love the thought behind them.

I have the 3-in-1 style. They have magnetic dot points across the top (I know) and you only ever need to carry one pair which is the lighter yellow base for day wear, you then clip on the darker yellow in the afternoon and remove them to clip on the red in the evening.

Very user friendly, my daughter who was hit and miss with the glasses uses them religiously, she is 29 and conscious of the look but swears by and explains her glasses to people who comment and she switches the lenses over at different parts of the day, its a win with her.

I don't have any vested interest but i really believe they are thoughtful and functional in many more ways than everyone who has jumped on board this ship with $$$$ as their driver rather than health.

Good luck with finding the right glasses you need, hope my 5 cents worth helps.

# Thread: Kids and EMF
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-emf.11431/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 31

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-01T20:05:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/kids-and-emf.11431/post-136384>

[http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2213879X14000583](http://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S2213879X14000583)

**Abstract**
Computer simulation using MRI scans of children is the only possible way to determine the microwave radiation (MWR) absorbed in specific tissues in children. Children absorb more MWR than adults because their brain tissues are more absorbent, their skulls are thinner and their relative size is smaller. MWR from wireless devices has been declared a possible human carcinogen. Children are at greater risk than adults when exposed to any carcinogen. Because the average latency time between first exposure and diagnosis of a tumor can be decades, tumors induced in children may not be diagnosed until well into adulthood. The fetus is particularly vulnerable to MWR. MWR exposure can result in degeneration of the protective myelin sheath that surrounds brain neurons. MWR-emitting toys are being sold for use by young infants and toddlers. Digital dementia has been reported in school age children. A case study has shown when cellphones are placed in teenage girls’ bras multiple primary breast cancer develop beneath where the phones are placed. MWR exposure limits have remained unchanged for 19 years. All manufacturers of smartphones have warnings which describe the minimum distance at which phone must be kept away from users in order to not exceed the present legal limits for exposure to MWR. The exposure limit for laptop computers and tablets is set when devices are tested 20 cm away from the body. Belgium, France, India and other technologically sophisticated governments are passing laws and/or issuing warnings about children's use of wireless devices.

**Abbreviations**

-   MRI, magnetic resonance imaging;
-   MWR, microwave radiation;
-   CNS, central nervous system;
-   FDTD,finite-difference, time-domain;
-   GBM, glioblastoma multiforme (also called glioblastoma);
-   cm, centimeter

### AJT — 2014-11-24T17:31:23+0200

A few years back my son sustained a pretty bad concussion while playing football. The "specialists" told me there was nothing to do nutritionally, and to take a medication that was off labeled for concussions. I decided to really dig in to brain nutrition and therapies. Outside of the mechanical aspect of the equipment etc, I started to wonder if this is due to low DHA, and phospholipids.

After reading this thread it also seems like EMF part of the concussion epidemic in sports, what do you folks think?

I don't think a man made device will duplicate the dynamics of Earth's water cycle. When it rains, I capture water in buckets to give to my pets and houseplants. It makes a difference for them. I would filter it for myself, if I didn't have access to well water and RO water.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-07T17:41:37+0300

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-08T05:10:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/are-healthy-carbs-really-that-bad.11434/post-137248>

> [nonchalant said:](/goto/post?id=137147)
>
> The energy of water does matter to us. People in the 'donut hole' have water with less energy.
> Structured water is not the only thing we get from water.
>
> I don't think a man made device will duplicate the dynamics of Earth's water cycle. When it rains, I capture water in buckets to give to my pets and houseplants. It makes a difference for them. I would filter it for myself, if I didn't have access to well water and RO water.

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=137155)
>
> the hydrogen bonding network determines how much or little energy water can carry

And Jack has said that the H-O-H bond angle is altered (presumably irreversibly) due to strong solar radiation. The angle is made smaller, resulting in a more voluminous water molecule. This reduces the strength of the hydrogen bond network as a whole; the strongest bonds are found in a tightly packed, ordered structure (an example being solid ice vs liquid water).

Do note that it is only one factor that influences the overall strength of the hydrogen bonding network, and I don't know how significant this factor is given that water is a relatively loosely-packed structure (even in ice form -- [http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/ice1h.html](http://www1.lsbu.ac.uk/water/ice1h.html))
