# Thread: Anxiety and Choice
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-and-choice.11439/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-08-02T18:27:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/anxiety-and-choice.11439/post-136517>

Note the fMRI study methodology....

> It may not surprise you to learn that healthy, well-fed people in affluent countries are often unhappy and anxious. But it did startle Zbigniew Lipowski when he came to a full realization of this fact. He had emigrated to North America from Dublin, in 1955, and, in the mid-nineteen-sixties, was put in charge of the psychiatry practices at two Montreal hospitals, Royal Victoria and Montreal Neurological. Why, he thought, as he worked there, would so many people living in such good conditions have so much anxiety?
>
> Lipowski was born in Poland and, in 1944, took part in the Warsaw Uprising, a mass revolt against the German Army that left more than two hundred thousand civilians dead. Lipowski, masquerading as a French refugee returning to France, was one of the fortunate few who escaped. “Those two months were the most significant experience of my life,” he would later recall. “The odor of burning flesh was with us day and night. We were bombed and shelled daily, food was very scarce, and water had to be obtained at night from a well some distance away. I was so hungry as to almost hallucinate food.”
>
> North America, however, greeted him with constant abundance and leisure. As he pondered the contrast, Lipowski thought of Buridan’s ass: an apocryphal donkey that finds itself standing between two equally appealing stacks of hay. Unable to decide which to consume, it starves to death. The donkey got its name from Jean Buridan, the nominalist fourteenth-century philosopher and Catholic priest who wrote extensively about free will. Buridan posited that free will could sometimes lead to inaction: an inability to choose due to excess uncertainty and, potentially, excess choice. Buridan’s ass, in turn, became the mascot for that general principle (though no equines of any kind actually appear in Buridan’s writing). For Lipowski, this scenario helped to explain the type of anxiety that he was witnessing around him. He called it an approach-approach conflict: faced with enticing options, you find yourself unable to commit to any of them quickly. And even when you do choose, you remain anxious about the opportunities that you may have lost: maybe that other stack of hay tasted sweeter.
>
> Lipowski summarized his theory in a [paper](http://ajp.psychiatryonline.org/article.aspx?articleID=151991) published in The American Journal of Psychiatry, in 1970. “I maintain that it is specifically the overabundance of attractive alternatives, aided and abetted by an affluent and increasingly complex society,” he wrote, “that leads to conflict, frustration, unrelieved appetitive tension, more approach tendencies and more conflict—a veritable vicious cycle.” That cycle, in turn, likely had “far-reaching and probably harmful effects on the mental and physical health of affected individuals.” Lipowski concluded that the overabundance of good scenarios was the main source of the anxiety around him. It was here, in the land of plenty, he wrote, “that the fate of Buridan’s ass haunts us.”
>
> While Lipowski’s work received some immediate wide attention, it soon fell into relative obscurity. Thirty years later, the Columbia University psychologist Sheena Iyengar [revived](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/11138768) the idea of conflict created by an overabundance of choice—a concept that the Swarthmore University psychologist Barry Schwartz would then popularize and rename as the [paradox of choice](http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2004/03/01/select-all)—though, unlike Lipowski, she focussed largely on the concept of cognitive demands. When shoppers had to choose between jams or chocolates, she found, they were more likely to make a selection when faced with six choices than when presented with twenty-four or thirty. They were also more satisfied with their ultimate selection. Too much choice, Iyengar concluded, would reduce motivation. Why exactly that would be the case, however, remained unexplored. One possibility, she hypothesized in her conclusion, was that an abundance of options may “simultaneously attract and repel choice-makers,” an emotion-based explanation not unlike the one that Lipowski had considered. Still, the potential of an underlying emotional tug-of-war remained more speculation than fact.
>
> Around the time that Iyengar and Schwartz were writing, Amitai Shenhav, now a psychologist at Princeton University, began to examine the literature on anxiety—one of the core emotions that people feel when they’re pulled in two directions. As he read, he realized that the nature of the experiences being studied was generally negative by design. “When people study anxious emotional states, they usually use negative stimuli,” he told me. “Like electric shocks: something that makes you anxious because you want to avoid it.” When experimenters looked at choice, on the other hand, they focussed more on the cognitive demands and the results of decisions, not the experience of anxiety as the choice was being made. So, inspired in part by his reading of Lipowski, Shenhav decided to investigate whether making choices from among appealing options could, in itself, provoke anxiety—and whether that anxiety was caused by the sort of emotional back-and-forth that Lipowski had speculated about. “If you think about it, that’s a much more prevalent scenario, when we have to choose between things we experience simultaneously,” he said. “I’ve had intuitions about this my entire life—from decisions like where to go to grad school to where we’re going to go for dinner tonight.”
>
> In a [series of imaging studies](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/25024178), Shenhav and Randy Buckner, a cognitive neuroscientist at Harvard, observed students making various choices. One to three days before the actual study began, Shenhav and Buckner had all of the participants evaluate more than three hundred different products, ranging from iPods and digital cameras to water bottles and T-shirts. When the experiment started, participants were put inside an fMRI scanner, shown pictures of the objects, and then asked to indicate which ones they preferred: Would they, for example, like to choose a digital camera or a camcorder? (The participants were told that, at the end of the study, they would randomly receive their object of choice from one of the trials.) Each choice was between either two similarly ranked items—both relatively low-value or both relatively high-value—or two items that were on opposite ends of the spectrum. After they had finished making all of their choices, the participants were asked to rate each decision on a series of five-point scales: How emotionally positive did it make them feel? How anxious did it make them feel? And how certain were they that they had chosen the way they had wanted to? The participants were also offered the surprise opportunity to change their choice: If they had it to do over, would they go the other way?
>
> Unsurprisingly, when people were asked to decide between something like an iPod and a bag of pretzels, they didn’t feel particularly anxious: the choice was clear and life was good. When both choices were low in value, the emotions were similarly clear-cut. No one was particularly happy, but neither were they anxious. But when multiple highly positive options were available—a digital camera and a camcorder, say—anxiety skyrocketed, just as Lipowski had predicted. The choices between those objects that they valued most highly were both the most positive and the most anxiety-filled. The more choices they had—the study was repeated with up to six items per choice—the more anxious they felt. “When you have more good choices, you don’t feel better,” Shenhav says. “You just feel more anxious.”
>
> **_The neural data that Shenhav and Buckner collected told the same thing. There was a very clear dissociation between the positivity-related and the anxiety-related regions of the brain involved in each choice. In other words, one distinct activation pattern tracked closely with positivity—the value of the good options themselves—while another tracked with the anxiety of making each choice. The activity in the anxiety-related regions, in fact, predicted how likely it was that a person would want to reverse her initial choice. The more difficult the choice was to begin with, the higher the chance of reversal. “It might actually be tracking the conflict after the choice is made,” Shenhav speculates._**
>
> Perhaps, then, what we’re really seeing is how the old fear of missing out plays out in the brain. We’re surrounded by great choices to make, great places to be, great things to do—and that’s wonderful. But when we’re made to commit to one, just think of everything that gets away. Shenhav himself refers to it as the “neural correlates of First World problems.” We know that someone else is eating that delicious ice cream that we passed up—or filling that job that we turned down.
>
> Lipowski himself, however, didn’t feel as if the relatively minor scale of the problems that wealthy people face in any way cheapened the emotions felt by his patients. **_What changes as we move from the scarcity of wartime Warsaw to the abundance of the First World isn’t the nature of the anxiety, it’s just the nature and significance of the choice itself. In one case, it seems heart-wrenching; in the other, trivial. Our brains, though, don’t make those kinds of value judgments: to them, a difficult choice is a difficult choice. And difficult choices mean anxiety._**

[http://www.newyorker.com/science/maria-konnikova/bad-good-choices?src=mp](http://www.newyorker.com/science/maria-konnikova/bad-good-choices?src=mp)

> [Josh said:](/goto/post?id=136517)
>

>
>
>
> [http://www.newyorker.com/science/maria-konnikova/bad-good-choices?src=mp](http://www.newyorker.com/science/maria-konnikova/bad-good-choices?src=mp)

The anxiety is caused by the inability to make a good, fast decision. That donkey needed more energy to easily make a good decision. Like you said, dopamine and cortisol. ### ssj3 — 2014-08-03T11:37:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mylapka-personal-environment-monitoring.11440/post-136599>

This device looks very promising... it has sensors which plug into your smartphone and allow you to measure EMF, radiation, humidity and nitrates in raw food. Its only available on iPhone, but android version is under development.

[https://mylapka.com/pem](https://mylapka.com/pem)

If someone is able to validate its accuracy and effectiveness is looks like a cheap way to test EMF ($79 for this component). If enough people become interested we could try to negotiate a group discount.

Sensor Details:

EMF:
HF: Coil antenna used to capture frequency range from 500 kHz to 3000 MHz.
LF: Magnetic antenna used to capture frequency range from 50 Hz to 500 kHz

Radiation:
Geiger Muller counter (Hard Beta and Gamma GM Detector) CJ32

Humidity:
1pc Honeywell HIH-4000-001 Relative Humidity Sensor

Nitrates:
Conductivity sensor. Custom made stainless steel probe .

# Thread: What is your price??

**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 17
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ssj3 — 2014-08-03T13:01:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136605>

I came across this article which shows just how much some people are willing to sacrifice.

h[ttp://articles.elitefts.com/training-articles/price-of-the-platform/](http://articles.elitefts.com/training-articles/price-of-the-platform/)

Sure it is the extreme of the extreme in a single sport but I think it is important thing to understand… sure it might be easy just to lay down the optimal law, but unless you are able to grasp the other person’s world and find ways to illicit change, it will be difficult to overcome decades of dogma as well as their mind.

Just saying… after burning through a few too many electrons...

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-08-04T23:36:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136745>

A colleague had a guy see him for torn shoulders. He was concerned that without training he was "losing size"
(Big guy)
Colleague almost burst out laughing, replying "you look in the mirror and see some kind of work of art, when i look at you all i see is a bloody disgrace."

### ssj3 — 2014-08-04T23:41:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136746>

> (Big guy)
> Colleague almost burst out laughing, replying "you look in the mirror and see some kind of work of art, when i look at you all i see is a bloody disgrace."

And what did it achieve... I'm sure the guy went back to doing what he was doing.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-05T01:55:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136766>

Funny..........Sometimes when you sacrifice something precious to your health, you're not really losing it. You're just passing the illness on to someone else. Wisdom is knowing what not to think about any longer. Ironically, you can't lose what you've never had but few people have that kind of insight about their own delusions. I guess that’s just part of caring for people: You have to give things up sometimes, and sometimes you even have to give them up. Sounds harsh, but we can not fundamentally make some else's thought bloom even when we share knowledge with them..........knowledge is often not enough for a broken mind.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-08-05T02:13:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136778>

> [ssj3 said:](/goto/post?id=136746)
>
> And what did it achieve... I'm sure the guy went back to doing what he was doing.

Obviously it wasnt all that was said. It planted a seed for change.

### caroline — 2014-08-05T02:15:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136780>

I seem to keep learning this lesson over and over ...for a variety of reasons.....

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-05T02:16:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136781>

What has life fundamentally taught me? For me, it was what I can do to my life. Do I accept life as what is? Or do I reject my current life and recreate what can be? This year taught me circumstances may be beyond my control, but how I respond to them is a recreative force in me. The situation is not my boss, I am. If I want something new, I had to stop doing something old. Just changing your ideas about what is, is how creative destruction begins to transform what you will become. Change is tied to DHA.

### ssj3 — 2014-08-05T02:17:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136782>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=136766)
>
> Funny..........Sometimes when you sacrifice something precious to your health, you're not really losing it. You're just passing the illness on to someone else. Wisdom is knowing what not to think about any longer. Ironically, you can't lose what you've never had but few people have that kind of insight about their own delusions. I guess that’s just part of caring for people: You have to give things up sometimes, and sometimes you even have to give them up. Sounds harsh, but we can not fundamentally make some else's thought bloom even when we share knowledge with them..........knowledge is often not enough for a broken mind.

For me, if I helped someone get 10%, then 50% and maybe finally 80% of the way there, I would be content if I did everything I could to get them there. I think this is more effective than approaching with my 100% bias and then giving up when it doesn’t eventuate… it was unrealistic expectation in the first place.

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-08-05T02:37:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136785>

when one is training poorly for "size" and creating shoulder issues via this training method, a seed must be planted that a change needs to occur.
maybe you need to see the guy to get the idea.
so he wants to be able to come and see someone who make his symptoms go away so he can go back to the activity that brought him here in the first place
all we can sell is the idea that there are better ways
the rest is left to the individual. we are too busy at the clinic to worry too much about those who dont want to help themselves because of the pursuit of body image

### Josh — 2014-08-05T06:35:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-136820>

Slow dogs and weak chimps......

### ssj3 — 2014-08-07T11:29:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-is-your-price.11441/post-137100>

**SSJ and the Bean Stork**

SSJ suffering from a shoulder issues arisen from years of physical activity visits a practitioner.

The practitioner, from a completely different background, knows what’s best. The practitioner criticises SSJ, unable to relate or understand SSJ’s pursuits or mindset and tells SSJ to cease all training immediately or face an list of endless health problems.

A visibly annoyed and confused SSJ leaves vowing never to listen to some thoughtless authority again. He continues on his destructive path, suffering multiple injuries, surgeries and health issues. His descendant SSJ2 learns from SSJ and follows the same destructive behaviour and spirals down the same path.

SSJ3, the youngest descendant, starts experiencing the same health issues but known to be a stubborn and rebellious individual decides visits a practitioner for help.

The practitioner immediately recognises factors contributing to the myriad of health issues experienced by a young SSJ3. However, the witty practitioner digs deeper to understand the current situation and gain a detailed perspective before outlaying the path back to health.

SSJ3 explains that lifting has been a big part of his life and probably wouldn’t be around or would be locked up somewhere without it.

The practitioner knowing that a recommendation for a complete cease from lifting would not be followed, sets out the recovery path. They discuss some alternative training methods that are structured, smarter and deliver proven results displayed by some well respected authorities without the constant toll on SSJ3’s body.

A few months later a recovered SSJ3 revisits the practitioner. He tells the practitioner that he has had great success with the new training methods... he doesn’t need to train as much and feels better. He also says that many of the provided resources discussed nutrition and asks advice on changes made from this new found knowledge due to the large amount of conflicting advice. A now receptive SSJ3, learns about EpiPaleo.

SSJ3 makes noticeable improvements following EpiPaleo. At the next visit, SSJ3 reveals his sleep is not the best, while discussing some blood markers which are still not optimal. They discuss water, WiFi and EMF exposure and SSJ3 leaves with some actionable tips.

An ecstatic SSJ3 rings the practitioner two weeks later to tell how he has never slept better and feels even better. His mental clarity has improved as has his wellbeing.

While SSJ3 and the practitioner continue down the optimal path, some of SSJ3’s crew notice the improvements and want in. They also begin down their own path to optimal.

Another practitioner affiliated with SSJ3’s crew, observes these improvements and becomes keenly interested. He contacts the original practitioner so he can learn and starts implementing with his own patients.

And so it continues....

# Thread: Rewild Yourself with Daniel Vitalis and Dave Asprey
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewild-yourself-with-daniel-vitalis-and-dave-asprey.11442/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-08-03T14:11:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewild-yourself-with-daniel-vitalis-and-dave-asprey.11442/post-136610>

Just listened to the latest Dave Asprey Podcast and found it very interesting, entertaining and thought provoking and certainly will resonate with everyone here. One of the best podcasts I've listened to..

[http://www.bulletproofexec.com/rewild-yourself-with-daniel-vitalis-podcast-141/](http://www.bulletproofexec.com/rewild-yourself-with-daniel-vitalis-podcast-141/)

> Daniel Vitalis Bio section said:
>
> I believe that in your heart resides the vigorous spirit of something wilder, something stronger, and something more brilliant than you have ever imagined. At the heart of each of your cells resides the ancient record of your DNA. It reaches back through the ages connecting you to each and every one of your ancestors, and to the wildness that existed in our species before the degenerative processes of domestication took hold.
>
> Awakening that wildness is what fuels my passion for the work that I do! It’s why I have created this website and why I’m encouraging you to “ReWild Yourself!”
>
> I’ve come to believe that all strength, all vigor, and all vitality emanates from the wildness of a thing. The further down the path of domestication we travel the more mal-adapted we become. Our health degenerates as does our will and our sense of purpose.
>
> Perhaps it’s presumptuous, but I believe you would like to be healthier, more self-sufficient, more confident and more in control. I would like to help you achieve that. I teach an approach to lifestyle that can put us in touch with our instincts and our incredible powers of adaptability. I’ll never tell you what to do, but I will always remind you of what you already know, that a wild intelligence resides in your DNA.
>
> My philosophy has helped thousands of people to rediscover their inner physician, their inner teacher, and that part of themselves that is free and independent to think, to feel, to perceive and to lead. It has worked for them and I believe it can work for you too.

### Clayton — 2014-08-03T17:29:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/rewild-yourself-with-daniel-vitalis-and-dave-asprey.11442/post-136625>

Sounds badass. Im gonna have a listen

### prAna303 — 2014-08-03T20:09:47+0300

# Thread: Minoxidil and hair loss
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minoxidil-and-hair-loss.11443/>
**Subforum:** Beginners Area
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben1983 — 2014-08-03T18:01:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/minoxidil-and-hair-loss.11443/post-136627>

What damage can the use the hair loss product "minoxidil" do to our health? I used this product for three years, combined with laser treatments to scalp (initially) and had good results as far as some hair growing back. I stopped using this product about a year ago as I just became too lazy, but then I noticed my hairline gradually start to recede again (hair loss completed halted when I was on the product). I was going to start using the product again but a naturopathic friend of mine suggested not to as it could damage my health in one way or another...I should have asked for details but I did not hence why I'm here now. The strength of the solution I was using was 3%. Also, on that note, does anyone know of any solutions that work to stop hair loss and possibly even grow back some hair? My hair is very thick and is only receding at the front...there's no hair loss at the back of my head.

### SeaHorse — 2014-08-03T18:46:39+0300

# Thread: Digestive enzymes...strange reaction
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/digestive-enzymes-strange-reaction.11444/>
**Subforum:** Adrenal Rx and Leaky Gut Rx
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Ben1983 — 2014-08-03T18:26:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/digestive-enzymes-strange-reaction.11444/post-136628>

I have had some issues with my gut over the past year. I get minor cramps after eating or drinking things such as coffee, dairy products and eggs. I had a food intolerance test done which showed quite a number of foods on the list. I decided to try digestive enzymes for the first time as I have read that that can help. I ordered a product called "Beauty Enzymes" by a company called "Sunfood". The product was far from cheap at nearly $70 for one month supply. It is supposed to be the most potent plant-based digestive enzyme product available (apparently 7 times more potent than most enzyme products and twice as potent as the next-best formula). Anyway, the first day I tried it, I took it with my first meal of the day, along with my other supplements, and didn't really notice much other than strting to feeling very slightly agitated, but also a little more alert and aware for the whole day. I also took the product again at my second and final meal of the day. I felt like the agitation increased somewhat just prior to going to sleep, too.
The next day was somewhat more interesting. I once again took the product in the morning along with my other supplements, however, this time I felt a huge rush feeling go straight to my brain probably five minutes after I took it. I was quite drowsy when I took it (as I am most morning when I wake up), but when the rush hit my brain (and it kind of scared me but only a little) I went from that drowsy state to feeling very alert and focused....probably unlike I can recall. I also felt like things that I had been worrying about previously were now just a piece of cake....it felt awesome. However, that state of mind only lasted fro 30-45 minutes and then I started to feel agitated again as I had the day previously.....but this time I felt agitated to a greater degree. I was at work thinking about going home because I just felt so strange and I just wanted to get away from people incase something weird came out of my mouth....that's how strange I felt. Anyway, the agitation died down after several hours, but since then I haven't been game enough to try it again. It's probably hard to tell what would have caused that reaction without any labs to go by, but I'm just wondering what some possibilities may be? Oh, and if I could always have that state of mind I had for 3o minutes, my God could I achieve much more in life!

### SeaHorse — 2014-08-03T18:35:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/digestive-enzymes-strange-reaction.11444/post-136630>

Sounds like some excitatory neurotransmitter activity was triggered in your gut…..have you read the Brain Gut series of blogs? Sorry I can't be more helpful...

### Ben1983 — 2014-08-03T18:55:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/digestive-enzymes-strange-reaction.11444/post-136633>

This is a link to the product description:[http://www.amazon.com/Sunfood-Enzymes-Indigestion-Vegetarian-Capsules/dp/B0009612IE](http://www.amazon.com/Sunfood-Enzymes-Indigestion-Vegetarian-Capsules/dp/B0009612IE)

### Ben1983 — 2014-08-03T18:56:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/digestive-enzymes-strange-reaction.11444/post-136634>

I love the British style...."particularly unfortunate" ...LOL...NYC="F\*\*\*\*NG Tragedy"....J. Conrad..."the horror, the horror".....English was his 4th language.....

> RCT’s unreliablity is particularly unfortunate for patients on statins since waiting in the wings is a new generation of super statins that lower cholesterol even more powerfully and are expected to cost around 20.000 dollars a year – [Beware new cholesterol drugs coming](http://healthinsightuk.org/2013/11/06/beware-new-cholesterol-lowering-drugs-coming/). How are we going to decide if they are worth it?
>
> Meanwhile we may be on the verge of having a firm decision about one of statins’ long terms effects – type 2 diabetes. Nearly a thousand women have so far begun legal proceedings against the pharmaceutical giant Pfizer, claiming that their best-selling cholesterol lowering statin Lipitor caused them to develop diabetes – [Thousand woman lipitor lawsuit](http://www.thelipitorlawsuit.com/thousands-women-lipitor-lawsuit-claims-will-soon-join-federal-litigation/)
>
> If they win, and there is considerable evidence to suggest there is a link, then NICE’s decision to recommend the drugs for another four million people or so, along with Sir Rory’s iron clad confidence that there are no side-effects worth worrying about, will look careless, arrogant and dangerous.

### nonchalant — 2014-08-04T23:44:34+0300

> Meanwhile we may be on the verge of having a firm decision about one of statins’ long terms effects – type 2 diabetes. Nearly a thousand women have so far begun legal proceedings against the pharmaceutical giant Pfizer, claiming that their best-selling cholesterol lowering statin Lipitor caused them to develop diabetes

I don't know why these women are unhappy about T2D, it's a whole lot better than getting cancer from artificially low LDL cholesterol levels.

### WereBear — 2014-08-08T02:59:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-statin-debate-is-about-over.11446/post-137226>

Statins have horrified me for a decade, when we convinced my husband's aunt, who was being treated for cancer, to stop the Lipitor that was costing her $800 a month. We were utterly astonished that her doctor could be so irresponsible as to treat a dying woman, under great financial pressure, for a possible heart attack at such a high cost! We were unaware of the statin dangers at the time.

She regained her ability to walk and think, and had a relatively good summer with her friends and relatives before passing away. What I've discovered since then is horrifying.

### ATL_Paleo — 2014-08-08T22:29:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-statin-debate-is-about-over.11446/post-137317>

The ultimate death of statins will be largely driven by ..... gasp  ..... attorneys. That trend appears to be gaining traction.

[http://blogs.wsj.com/pharmalot/2014/08/08/pfizer-faces-numerous-lawsuits-over-lipitor-side-effects/?](http://blogs.wsj.com/pharmalot/2014/08/08/pfizer-faces-numerous-lawsuits-over-lipitor-side-effects/?)

A fast-growing number of lawsuits have been filed by women who allege the drug maker knew about the possibility of such serious side effects associated with its blockbuster cholesterol-lowering pill, but never properly warned the public, according to [Reuters](http://www.reuters.com/article/2014/08/08/us-pfizer-lipitor-lawsuits-insight-idUSKBN0G80E520140808).

In the past five months, the news service reviewed federal court filings that show lawsuits by women in the U.S., who say that Lipitor caused them to develop type-2 diabetes, jumped from 56 to almost 1,000. One attorney says the actual number of women represented in these lawsuits numbers approximately 4,000.

The filings began picking up two years ago, after the FDA warned that Lipitor and other statins were linked to incidents of memory loss and a “small increased risk” of diabetes. According to lawyers representing plaintiffs, women face a higher risk than men of developing diabetes from using Lipitor, and gain fewer benefits, Reuters writes.

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-statin-debate-is-about-over.11446/post-137500>

I see the statin lawsuits gaining traction, but those legal mills will grind too slowly to really have an impact. However, it can shift the pharmaceutical industry away from a drug that is no longer so profitable. My prediction is the next wave of sleep drugs: the specialist I saw (who was no help at all) was really eager to give me Ambien. Maybe that's the hot new intervention!

But we're already seeing the slow slide away as the lipid hypothesis starts its long-overdue erosion process. Statins are pegged directly to that theoretical structure. What brings one down will undermine the other.

# Thread: cinnamon's journal
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 158
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### cinnamon — 2014-08-04T02:33:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-136676>

Hi! I’ve been offline for the past two weeks. Did you all miss me? I sure was thinking about you guys a lot of the time (maybe there is something to this quantum entanglement thing?) \*grin\*

I don’t know when I’ll ever catch up as I was already so far behind. I do hope everyone is doing well!

The reason I’ve been away is because of this:

{ "lightbox\_close": "Close", "lightbox\_next": "Next", "lightbox\_previous": "Previous", "lightbox\_error": "The requested content cannot be loaded. Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

The journey started with me panting in pain on the bathroom floor at 4 am on Saturday two weeks ago. I took an ambulance to the hospital. The picture is the aftermath of emergency surgery to remove a perforated appendix together with a section of bowel that had also perforated. The surgeon described it as “quite a mess”.

I’ve been around at jackkruse.com for more than 2 years and some of you will have seen my occasional posts on these forums. I was doing very well until this recent setback. And I credit the JK protocols with putting me into an excellent state to withstand the path I’ve found myself on. I feel maybe I have a story to tell now – about this recent “dip in the road” – and as in some ways, on this side of it, I’m starting over, it seems a good time to finally begin in journal land.

There were many dark moments along the way, but also many discoveries, too. And while I crossed paths with many different people, I definitely felt that they crossed paths with me too. I’ll write some about my interactions and observations. Maybe it will help someone else that finds themselves in similar circumstances in future.

### cinnamon — 2014-08-04T02:38:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-136677>

This post is a bit of background info - the context that we always talk about.

I’m female and recently turned 52. People seem to have a hard time believing my age.

I live in a big city in a small downtown house. Neighbours are close, but I do have a small backyard where I can get my feet onto the earth.

Here’s a picture I took a couple of months ago:

I never had any children. My partner and his 22 year old daughter live with me.

When I first came to jackkruse.com more than 2 years ago, I was suffering badly from insomnia and poor energy levels. My neck was always sore and I had pains in my upper back. I since started minding circadian rhythms, eating a ketogenic epi-paleo diet, getting lots of good water, grounding, getting sunshine and regularly using showers for CT. Pretty much everything I tried worked well for me.

I was not diagnosed with any particular illness. As the years went on, I found it difficult to cope with a regular work schedule (I understand now that the office towers I worked in, full of fluorescent lights and electronic equipment, were sapping my energy). The work I did was corporate financial accounting type of assignments – with computers since the late 1980s. The work often involved deadlines and so some very long hours. I have taken a lot of breaks from the work world over my life. I’m very frugal and while I don’t have all the choices money would have given me, I’ve often felt that my time was worth more than they could ever pay me.

Looking back, I can see I did myself no favours by adopting an almost vegan diet from age 34 to 47.

I also lived on the 33rd floor of a downtown condo tower from age 28 to 34. Fortunately, non-native EMF was not nearly so prevalent in the early 1990s, but I’m sure I was ungrounded much of that time.

As a child, I developed pollen allergies at about age 5. I now feel that it was a sign that something was off, but all I got were antihistamines. My father also had allergies, so I was told I must have “inherited” it. The allergies have increased at times, especially when under a lot of stress. They have also all but disappeared a couple of times – first when I started the vegan diet and then again when I started the JK protocols. They are currently not an issue for me.

I’m sure I’ve forgotten something here, so I may add a bit more later.

### cinnamon — 2014-08-06T22:34:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-136996>

Well thank you all for your good wishes. I just got back from a return visit of a couple of nights at the hospital. They warned me when I left the first time that I was at high risk for an abscess because of the original perforation. Well, yes, I had an abscess. They put a drain in and I get another 10 days of antibiotics.

Because of the location of the abscess they had to insert the drain in from the back. It's a couple of inches to the left of my tailbone. I was anesthetized and the drain was inserted by a radiologist using CT scans for guidance. I've been irradiated more than ever in my life with now having had at least 4 CT scans in the past few weeks.

Though the drain itself is uncomfortable, the pain I had in my gut has nearly dissipated entirely. They told me there was about 200ml of pus and debris that was released as soon as the put in the drain. What has flowed since then looks pretty good. And my white blood cell count has already dropped to near normal levels.

I'm home now with a prescription for 10 more days of antibiotics. A homecare nurse is supposed to come by daily to flush the drain for the next week or so. I don't think I'll be able to wear my bikini for a while yet. \*sigh\*

So, just another little dip in the road... Oh and Audrey, I hope you will forgive me that I don't think I will put up pictures of the drain that is now in my backside!  (Everybody knows Audrey will always ask for pictures...)

### cinnamon — 2014-08-20T03:32:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-138348>

I'm back!

I didn't mean to make you all worry. Living with the drain really wrecked my sleep so I've been dealing with low energy levels. I've come on here several times for a short time, reading and gaining a little inspiration from the wonderful community here.

I had one visit back to the ER since the drain was inserted. My incision had opened. The gap was only about 1.5 cm but the homecare nurse advised that we should go to the hospital, so we did. They told me to just keep covering it with gauze and not to worry unless it starts to look infected or inflamed. The gap is now about 1 cm and it looks a bit like I have two belly buttons!  (Should look extra special in my bikini! )

The drain finally came out last Thursday. It sure came out a lot more easily than it went in. I am feeling a bit more like myself, which is a very good thing. I lost about 15 lbs though and need to put some of that back. I have returned to eating the high fat epi paleo way I did before everything happened - and I have been eating a lot of sauerkraut, salami, parmagiana reggiano, kefir, high fat yogurt and goat cheese together with some local fruit. I wouldn't normally eat much fruit (or dairy, for that matter) but as peaches, plums and strawberries are in season here now and I am trying to put on some weight, I've decided it's okay. The antibiotics are finally done this past Saturday, so I'm hoping to be able to make some headway.

Has anyone tried those commercial probiotic products like Bio-K? Are they any good?

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-138353>

Welcome back!

### cinnamon — 2015-02-03T03:24:12+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-154571>

So, I had to scroll a few pages back in the journals section to get to mine. And I know I promised to tell some stories about my experiences with my emergency appendectomy last summer. I still will, so I hope you all can hang in there waiting!  By way of update, I can report that I had my six month follow-up with the surgeon in charge of my case last week. He looked at me and said, “You look great! No need to come back to see me!” YAY!

I’m still working on my gut healing after all the antibiotics, the NG tube and my poor gut microbiome being exposed to light during the surgery - eek!  The brain-gut connection seems very tangible to me as my energy levels and brain power have not recovered completely. My brain was starting to heal from all the seafood I have added to my diet in the past couple of years, but I did have a setback after the surgery. I’ve been watching you guys on the forum make some pretty amazing breakthroughs and clearly your brains are healing well! I would love to be able to participate at that level. I’ve been somewhat hopeful as recently my dreams are getting even more vivid, I’ve been remembering all kinds of miscellaneous details of long past people and events, and my sense of humour is returning.

In particular, I’ve been finding my ability to recognize and come up with puns is coming back! Punning is kind of a family trait. And at family events, I noticed for several years that my father and brothers (and uncles, too) could get into these wonderful contests where they would try to “outpun” each other, escalating until we were all laughing too hard to speak. The female members of the family didn’t seem to be able to keep up - this despite the fact that we are all just as intelligent by the usual measures. I was thinking recently that this might be because as women we are more susceptible to the environment and our systems may have been depleted of electrons, relatively speaking. The ability to see odd connections between things is necessary for the successful punster, and maybe our brains needed more energy to do that?

Tonight, in light of all the recent forum discussion of quantum entanglement, I decided to add to my journal to get myself into your thoughts. You see, my big brother (who is one of the very best at puns!) is having surgery tomorrow. He has been diagnosed with prostate cancer and is having a “radical prostatectomy”. He is a really, really, good guy. So please, if you could, focus a little bit of your attention on him sending thoughts for a positive outcome? I know I would definitely appreciate it.

Love to you all!

cinnamon

### cinnamon — 2015-02-03T18:10:42+0200

I just got word that the surgery went very well and my brother is now in recovery. No enlarged lymph nodes, so that is a very good thing. Apparently, he said he felt very supported by all the people that were thinking about him.

I could definitely feel the love too. It made me feel stronger and calmer somehow? Thank you all so much for your kind thoughts. When I visit him later today, I will let him know that there are people sending positive thoughts from all over the world!

My brother made it home from the hospital yesterday. He has some healing to do, but is in good spirits. When I visited him in the hospital I told him about all you folks from all around the world. He just nodded and said that he had definitely felt supported while he was en route to the hospital on surgery day. As I said earlier, I felt it too. Again, thank you all so much for your good wishes and thoughts!

love,
cinnamon

### cinnamon — 2015-03-17T01:02:58+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-158709>

Yesterday I woke up thinking about prisms. Must have been the effect of reading Ubi7!

I was thinking that if I set my computer screen so that it was "white" and put a prism in front of it to recreate the Pink Floyd album cover, the rainbow would be only Red Green and Blue. And I guess one third would be blue? Maybe someone with a prism can try this with one of those crazy "flashlight" apps on their smartphone...

The other thing I've been thinking about lately is cytochromes. Such an odd word - cytochrome. The cyto part means cellular - that's cool. But the chrome part - doesn't that refer to colour? Do the cytochromes at Complex I in the Electron Transport Chain have a **different colour** to the ones at Complex II? And since different colours have different frequencies, is that how it comes to be that supposedly carbs go through Complex I and fats through Complex II? I mean are they matching up frequencies?

### cinnamon — 2015-05-13T02:52:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-164028>

Okay, so in the spirit of n=1 and biohacking and all that, I've been thinking about niacin for a while. I bought some of the "non-flushing" variety over a year ago but then wasn't sure about adding another supplement so there it sat in my kitchen cupboard, hiding behind the magnesium, the d-ribose powder and the ubiquinol. After WereBear's recent posts on her success with niacin, I decided to open the bottle.

I took it a few times last week. I wasn't sure it had much effect, but I thought my dreams seemed a little more vivid. Then I was reading a bit about whether the non-flushing variety works the same way as regular niacin. It would seem that it does not follow the same pathway. So, I wondered about regular niacin and yesterday I found some while shopping and it leaped into my basket and made it all the way to the checkout.

This morning after breakfast, I took one 500mg regular niacin tablet. I wondered if I would get the "flush" and how long I would have to wait to find out. Then I went outside to sit in the morning sun. It was a gorgeous morning with clear blue skies and sunshine following thunderstorms of the night before. I sat in my chair with my bare feet on the damp patio stones, grounding in the sunshine and started reading. (@[Josh](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/8791/), you might appreciate that I was reading Osho's Tao the pathless path. I came across it quite serendipitously a few days ago and remembering the parables you have sometimes brought to us on the forum, I decided to take a closer look).

A few minutes later, I had my answer. Yes, I got the flush! And wow, I have been tanning so easily and well the last couple of years that I had forgotten what sunburn feels like!  As I was sitting in the sun, I think that may have exacerbated things a wee bit so I closed the book and went inside. The skin on my knees and lower arms was bright red and blotchy. My face felt like it was on fire! I grabbed some ice water and started drinking. That helped some, but it was all a bit scary. I mean this was just one tablet from a very ordinary brand of vitamins available right off the shelf! I suspect that if niacin was patentable, it would be sold by big pharma under a trademark name (maybe WonderNi? ha ha ha) and it would have big warnings on the label. People would likely be convinced that it was strong and effective since they can feel it working!  LOL

It took about an hour before the flushing quieted down. Then I got some chills. They reminded me a lot of what it felt like when I used to get sunburn. And my digestive system was a bit upset by it all. I am feeling okay now and suspect that I'm not any the worse for having tried it. Now the question is, do I do it again?

I suppose I am crazy, but I'm thinking of waiting a day and then trying a half tablet dose...

### caroline — 2015-05-13T03:36:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-164036>

Good morning from Oz Michelle!

### cinnamon — 2015-05-17T02:05:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-164482>

Time for a little update on my "n=1 acin" hack!

I did NOT take any niacin on day 2 after I experienced the big flush with 500 mg on day 1.

On day 3, I split a tablet and took a one-quarter dose. This didn't seem to have any noticeable side-effects.

On day 4, I took a one-quarter dose. Again, it didn't seem to produce any particular effects.

This morning was day 5. I thought about taking a one-half dose. However, I have company visiting so decided not to be adventurous and just stick with the one-quarter dose. Well, within just a few minutes I got a flush reaction that was comparable to day 1!  I think it may have lasted a few minutes less - maybe about 45 minutes instead of an hour or so. But it was pretty severe. My face and neck were deep red and on fire! My thighs were all blotchy and radiating heat as if sun-burned! Wow! Also, about an hour and half later, I had diarrhea again. I don't think I freaked out my company too much, but I did make excuses to go "get dressed" and so on in order to leave the scene! LOL

So, I have decided that I definitely will NOT take any niacin on day 6. I'm not sure about day 7 though...

I wonder how it would be to take some niacin, get the flush and then do CT?

### nonchalant — 2015-05-17T02:20:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-164483>

Hi Cinnamon. You might want to try niacin at bedtime. Won't stop the flushing, but hopefully you won't be awake to notice.

My DH ended up with hives from niacin after a couple weeks of a tiny 50 mg daily dose. We backed off for a while, and then he started back at one per week, now he's at four times/week.

Niacinamide causes him zero problems, and he takes that the other days of the week.

### Haley — 2015-05-24T02:38:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-165153>

I also had an experience where I learned just how effective niacin can be. I took 2 tabs (don't remember the dose), got in my car, 10 minutes later BAM. I began to flush, swell and black out while driving. I barely made it into a gas station where i passed out on the floor waiting for the bathroom. I woke up and went into the bathroom, made myself throw up a little and 10 minutes later i was completely normal. Crzy stuff!

### cinnamon — 2015-07-13T20:28:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-171849>

I was just reading in @[Grmoline](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1289/) 's journal about how she thinks [she maybe an "Inuit"](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/ridin-the-jk-express-to-optimum.9441/page-12#post-171833). Over the past few days I was thinking about the sun and skin colour thing too!

The tan I got on the cruise was the darkest I have _ever_ been. In the past, with that much sun I would surely have turned bright red and it all would have peeled off! I did burn a little with the midday sun exposure at Roatan, but still it was amazing considering how much time I was out in full sun.

I guess the deep tan I got was a protective mechanism because the Caribbean sun was so very powerful - much more powerful than I am used to. I have been in the sun a lot since I got back to Toronto and now my tan has faded. Could it be that my solar panels are trying to scoop up all the photons/UV rays they can from the way less powerful sun here?

### Grmoline — 2015-07-13T21:04:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-171852>

Another way to look at it is that the tan allows you to stay in the sun longer, giving you protection whilst allowing maximum exposure to UV in those northern latitudes....

### cinnamon — 2015-07-27T02:30:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-173166>

Hi Inger! I hope you are doing well.

So, I guess it's time for a little update. I've been a bit distracted since the cruise - a little bit in firefighting mode, dealing with some stuff at home.

It is also the anniversary of the week I spent in hospital last year (the emergency appendectomy).  Maybe being more tied into the ebb and flow of the seasons brings back memories of similar times of year? I don't know, but there have been a few flashback type memories. And while I don't remember being _that_ anxious or emotional about it while it was all happening, I suppose a year of distance might be allowing me to process some of the emotions that probably belong with the event. It has been more stressful than I like and I want to regain the level of bliss and joy you can see in all our faces when you look at the pictures from the cruise! I'm hopeful that I will get there.

I am trying to spend as much time as possible in the AM sunlight (with my palms toward the incoming sunbeams!) I am way behind on the forums, not to mention all the webinars and blogs I need to catch up on. Even though I am behind, I feel connections happening all over the place - so that is good!  And the connections aren't always the physics stuff. Sometimes just things that seem a bit random. I mean, I honestly didn't know how special the magnolia tree at Jack's house was when I scooped up a couple of leaves and brought them home with me!

I hear @[RobH](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1033/) has a plan to get to 10,000 oysters. My thought was "wow!" followed by "well, if I keep going the way I am now, I should pass 1,000 cans of sardines in less than three years!" LOL A sense of humour is a fine thing! Yup, I do believe this is so.

### cinnamon — 2015-08-02T02:30:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-173754>

So, two things to report today.

For the past two days, while out in the AM sunlight with my hands up, I have felt a vibration in my palms. Maybe it is just my pulse, but I have never felt it in my palms before. It's a nice feeling.

This morning, I had my eyes closed and the sun was shining on my face. Through my closed lids, I watched what might have been a floater in my right eye. It moved just like the second hand on a clock - going backwards for 3 or 4 seconds. How cool was that? I thought "Hey, maybe I'm unwinding the arrow of time!"

I see we have some new emoticons.

### cinnamon — 2015-08-07T23:05:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-174210>

So, I just got off the phone with my dad. He had a scary story to tell.

Early last week, he had a colonscopy as a follow-up to an occult blood test that was part of his annual physical. During the colonscopy they removed 4 polyps. And then, a couple of days later, things went wrong. He started bleeding big time and went to the ER at the hospital. They put the scope back in to see what the problem was, but were unable to see because there was too much blood. They tried again the next day and still too much blood. And by this time, his hemoglobin had dropped and they needed to bring it back up. So they gave him blood and let him rest overnight. And then they had him take some kind of laxative an hour before they tried again with the scope. Two docs then worked him over to massage the blood out of his colon while a couple of nurses held him down. He says he thinks he was screaming and things were changing colour in his vision. I know, it's an ugly image! They were able to get the scope in and they found the spot that was bleeding and cauterized the wound. He then spent a couple more days in hospital, eating jello and being told not to move. They let him go home a few days ago and he says he is feeling a bit better today.

By the way, he is 83. He has a bit of a laundry list of ailments, but he usually copes very well in spite of those. Just for reference, he has DISH, interstitial cystis (IC), hypothyroidism and GERD. And he has had cataract surgery and hernia surgery. So, I guess you can see that he has collagen issues and gut issues. Mentally, he is still 100%.

He told me he has to get his hemoglobin up (due to all the blood he lost) and he cannot take any kind of iron supplement. He is doing some dietary support (like eating eggs and peppers and blackstrap molasses). But I wonder if there is anything else in the world of water/light/magnetism that might be helpful? I suggested he spend time in AM sunlight (with his palms exposed). Any comments would be much appreciated!

thanks,
cinnamon

### fitness@home — 2015-08-08T00:25:37+0300

Michelle, wishing your Dad a speedy recovery. What happened to him sounds very scary.

### cinnamon — 2015-08-08T19:33:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-174261>

Thank you for your kind thoughts and good wishes, Dawn, Audrey and Inger.

I think his environment is pretty good. At least now that he is out of the hospital. He and my mom live on Vancouver Island (on the Pacific coast of Canada). I always feel great after spending a few days visiting their house. It is in a low density suburban area with tons of conifers and trails through the forest everywhere. When I was there last year, still only 2 wifi signals that I could see from there. He will get plenty of love and attention from my mom, too. They have a very positive and supportive relationship which is kind of awesome to see. They celebrated their 6oth wedding anniversary in June. He already does a lot of things right - like going to bed early, not eating late, keeping the house cool, getting outside often, etc. So, I think he will be okay.

I just want to help if I can! He is open to suggestions and said he would definitely try the AM sunlight. I think he liked it when I started telling him about porphyrin rings and red blood cells! LOL

Are we speaking of:

-   direct exposure to red light, such as it being radiated upon our skin, or
-   indirect exposure to red light, such as changing out our light bulbs in a room to say, red LEDs or red incandescents, where the photons aren't directly radiating our skin, but we can see it, and thus the photons must be impacting us in some way (being sensed by photoretinal ganglionic cells, and them then sending signals to other neural tissues, etc)?

If we are speaking of (1) how long would we need to be irradiated?

If we are speaking of (2) how powerful/bright/weak/dim would the light need to be? Any specific distance from our body?

Lastly, are we speaking about using the light sources I indicated above, or something else, such as the UFO light set up, using it in short bursts throughout the day/night?

Thanks in advance.

### cinnamon — 2015-09-18T03:45:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177308>

I had forgotten that onions make me tear up. And it isn't that I haven't been eating onions!

No, all this talk about uv light and myopia. I have to admit that I _knew_ it was coming. So today I went the whole day without my contacts. And when I went to chop a red onion for my salmon salad early this evening, I had the tear thing happen. And I remembered a time, oh so long ago, when that happened in the past!

Yes, I'm one of those folks with myopia.  For me, it started at age 9. For sure something strange happened during that year, though I am not sure just what. I had some kind of sharp pain like a stitch when walking home from school one day. It lasted for an hour or so and would only ease if I slowed to an absolute crawl. It reappeared a couple of times and then I went to the doctor. I don't think they ever came to any conclusion about it, but it disappeared. I missed a bit of school over the whole episode and when I got back there, I had trouble seeing the blackboard. So, I got my first pair of glasses together with instructions that I was only to use them for looking at the blackboard or watching tv. I suppose that was really good advice, considering what we are learning now about the need for UV light in the eye.

I wore my glasses only when essential up until my second year of university. Then I got contact lenses. I loved my contacts. I could converse with people and actually see the details of their expressions. I remember sitting at the back of a city bus and being amazed that I could see the expression on the driver's face as reflected in the rear-view mirror!

The thing about contacts though, was that once they were in, they were in. So, they were in all day. And even worse, by the end of my last year at university, the latest and greatest thing was the "extended wear" lenses. I got those before taking off for a trip to Europe that summer. I don't think I ever went the whole 30 days they were supposed to be good for without taking them out, but probably close to that.

I regularly wore lenses for a full week at a time without taking them out for probably the next 10 years or so. And then I started taking them out every day, even though they were still supposed to be extended wear. I don't really remember why I started doing that, but probably because it was much more comfortable. Still, I really never left home without putting in my contacts, so I doubt I was exposed to much UV light.

In the last couple of years, I have been spending a little bit of the morning without the contacts. It let me go out and see the sun in the AM.

When we were on the cruise, each morning I got up from our pitch black interior cabin, with no watch or phone and not sure what time it was. I grabbed my key card and stumbled out into the garishly lit hallway without glasses or contacts and made my way to the public balcony at the front of our deck. There I spent a few minutes looking at the sun. I was actually pretty surprised that my sense of it being morning was not that far off. Just one day I was a little early as it was still quite dark out, but roosters were crowing!

For the last few days, I have been spending an extended time in the AM without putting in my contacts. Strangely, in some ways, I feel I can see. My near vision has always been remarkably good, so I can read, for example, while sitting out on my back deck (did my first pass through on Ubi 25 this afternoon). And I swear I can see more clearly in the distance than before as well. (Maybe that's due, in part, to all the DHA I am taking in over the last couple of years?) Hey, if the astronauts can have myopia develop in only a few weeks, maybe improvement can happen quickly too?

Anyway, today I decided to just leave my contacts out and enjoy the PM sun too. I also spent about an hour working in the garden, blind as a bat! LOL So long as I'm in a reasonably familiar location, I realize that I'm okay.  Well, except I need to be prepared for onions!

Oh, and just for the record, my last prescription was -7.5 and -6.0. So yeah, by all accounts, I really cannot see!

### nicld — 2015-09-18T16:22:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177319>

Keep up the great work. I swear that my vision improved by NOT wearing my glasses all the time.

### Lahelada — 2015-09-23T17:15:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177654>

> [cinnamon said:](/goto/post?id=177308)
>
> For the last few days, I have been spending an extended time in the AM without putting in my contacts. Strangely, in some ways, I feel I can see. My near vision has always been remarkably good, so I can read, for example, while sitting out on my back deck (did my first pass through on Ubi 25 this afternoon). Hey, if the astronauts can have myopia develop in only a few weeks, maybe improvement can happen quickly too?
>
> , blind as a bat! LOL So long as I'm in a reasonably familiar location, I realize that I'm okay.  Well, except I need to be prepared for onions!
>
> Oh, and just for the record, my last prescription was -7.5 and -6.0. So yeah, by all accounts, I really cannot see!

I had a similar experience with my vision,on the bus as it happens. I always take my glasses off as a kind of game whose nose I can make out and how far back they sit.I see more noses and glasses and they sit a row further back than before. To the normal sighted without my glasses I would like to explain that people at a distNce have the face of faceless aliens from seventies sci fi series.

A stranger thing happened though. Last week on two different occasions the bus went along the route lined by tall buildings. The sun was up,it had been up for maybe an hour or so and if you can imagine there were flashes of sunlight when the bus passed the empty spaces between buildings. Those rays illuminated the faces of my fellow passengers in the back rows and for a split second I could see them clearly...glasses noses,lipstick,man or woman. It was easier and more pronounced with people of lighter skin or who were wearing a prominent colour. I have a prescription of 6.5 in both eyes,too....another blind bat!

### cinnamon — 2015-09-23T19:25:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177665>

Isn't that interesting, Lahelada! I mean the part about how the sunlight "lit up" their faces. I have noticed for sure that my vision in full spectrum sunlight is way sharper than it is in artifical lighting - contacts, glasses, or naturally. I don't think this used to be the case - just in the last couple of years since I upped my DHA and got really particular about exposing myself to light after dark.

My night vision has changed as well. When I first stumbled upon the forum and started minding circadian rhythms, I would get "stars in my eyes" at night when I was in a completely pitch black room. It freaked me out a bit when it happened! I still get this effect sometimes, but I notice that the stars are now appearing in a complete ring. I don't know, but I'm guessing the addition of DHA has caused some level of repair?  I'm hoping it's a good sign!  I have also noticed that I can pull the covers over my head in bed in a completely dark room, and I can still kind of see my hand in front of my face! I suppose my skin must be glowing? I hope I'm not leaking too much light!

I've been thinking a lot that parents need to make sure their kids get enough AM UV light so that they can see! I used to think that my bad eyesight was a genetic thing, but I don't think so anymore. And certainly my ancestors would never have survived to end up creating me if their eyesight was that bad!

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-24T18:00:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177751>

Light matters even in the vastness of space. Sunlight is a cathode ray and comets and planets are anodes. As light hits an anode water is released because of the charge effect of a moving target. The same thing hold true when you are holding your cell phone or laptop. You become dehydrated like a comet or like California is now. Think about physics.......it is tied to life too. [http://www.cbsnews.com/news/water-blasts-on-comets-tied-to-sunlight-cycle/](http://www.cbsnews.com/news/water-blasts-on-comets-tied-to-sunlight-cycle/)

### cinnamon — 2015-09-25T01:11:31+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177826>

Well, I got me some black light bulbs yesterday!
They are not reptile ones, but they do give a nice purple glow. This is the kind I bought: _[link](http://www.canadiantire.ca/en/pdp/blue-planet-13w-mini-cfl-bulb-black-0525146p.html#.VgRysSsVc70)_
(Thanks to @[alyson thiessen](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/13352/) for suggesting Canadian Tire as a place to look! )
I bought 2 and put them up in the ceiling light fixture in the room where my computer lives. I didn't put the glass part of the fixture back up, so they are exposed. (Oh and let me tell you, the look isn't going to win any interior design awards! LOL )

I had them on for maybe 2 or 3 hours late yesterday. I wasn't too sure about leaving them on after sunset, so I turned them off then and just went with my red led lamp, my f.lux and my orange glasses.

Now here is the weird part. This morning, when I was outside in the AM sun, it was like I could sense the UV part of the spectrum. I mean, anything around that was white, looked kind of purple-tinged - just like it does under black lights!  Isn't that interesting? The effect lasted for the first 15 minutes or so that I was outside, and it seemed more intense with things that were not directly in the sunlight. I wonder if it will happen again tomorrow!

### cinnamon — 2015-09-25T03:30:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177836>

Yes, I felt that way too @[nonchalant](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/191/)! I had developed a great dislike and disregard for fluorescent lighting. But I think Jack said his bulbs were "mercury vapor" bulbs, and well these fluorescents have got mercury!

To be honest, I am a bit anxious to be playing with light as I believe it's very powerful. And for sure the wrong frequencies at the wrong times could be very bad news. I am not afraid to use the sun for some real UV, but it's now officially fall and there might not be that much available.

So, I'm venturing forth with some artificial stuff and giving it a try! If nothing else, it should be an interesting experiment.

### cinnamon — 2015-09-25T17:28:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177863>

hmmm.... @[av8r](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/5713/) , you have given me an idea. Maybe I need to put some black lights up in my laundry room where I have an unfinished concrete floor and can do a little grounding when the world is frozen outside! I did not notice much of a "sensing of UV light" effect today, but the sky was overcast. Yesterday, I noticed the effect the most when the sun was actually shining on my face and I was looking at white objects that were not in direct sunlight. I'm hoping for some bright sunshine tomorrow!

I love this! I did the same thing this morning. ### cinnamon — 2015-09-25T22:51:12+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177886>

Okay, another new and odd thing to report!

The skies cleared and the sun came out. So, I went outside.  I was lying on my stomach, with my head turned to the right. I closed my left eye for a second. Then I opened it and closed my right eye. Whoa! The intensity of the colours I saw was very, very different!  The right eye saw things kind of washed out and with purple and green hues. The left eye saw things with deep colour intensity and very vividly. It was so weird that I tried it a few times, winking back and forth.

I had never seen this before and wondered what was happening. Then I tried lying with my head turned to the left. And the same thing happened - _but with the right eye now having vivid colour vision and the left with the faded purply version!_

It was pretty close to solar noon, so the sun was as high in the sky as it gets this time of year. And I was lying in the sunlight such that I wouldn't think there would be much difference in the angles reaching my right or left side but the sun was slightly behind me. Each time, it was the eye closest to the sun that had the faded purply vision.

Oh, and when I sat up and looked at the world, everything was normal, with equal colour vision in both eyes.

So strange!

### cinnamon — 2015-09-27T02:33:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-177953>

Today was a clear and sunny day. I can report that the effect I first noticed a couple of days ago recurred. When the morning sun was shining directly on my face, white things in indirect light were glowing purple!

I was out running around at noon today, so didn't get a chance to see if the different colour perception between eyes like yesterday could be repeated. Maybe tomorrow!

I am in the same boat.  Glasses since 1st grade, contact lenses for 37 years. Never could wear the overnights, though due to dry eyes. Decided after reading UBI 25 to ditch the contacts. I have been doing the same as you, not wearing glasses in the AM sun (every day at least 1-2 hrs.). Haven't braved garden work yet, but read on the deck or crochet. My current vision which has been stable since about high school or college is -6.5 both eyes. I got glasses with -6.5 in the left lens and -5.0 in the right as opposed to getting bifocals. I can see near without correction, but with correction the near was getting blurry. Before I ditched the contacts, I got some nice blue-blockers to wear at night over contacts. Can't use those now, so got some clip-ons for glasses. Since you have been doing the AM sun for a couple years, I am going to follow your thread as I am interested in finding out if your vision improves. I have only been doing the AM sun for about 2 months, but curious to see if my vision improves over time. Is there anything else you have been doing for vision? Thanks, Lori

### Jack Kruse — 2015-09-30T20:50:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-178308>

you can use saline but dry eye is from lack of neural input (DC current) via lacrimal nerve and nasocillary nerve due to a lack of UV to the RPE.

### cinnamon — 2015-10-01T01:11:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-178322>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=178297)

> smart move on contacts.......When you look in the direction of the sun blink a lot.......FYI

Hi Dr K - I saw you posted this advice earlier today, so I tried it!
I've been feeling more energized this afternoon and I seem to be seeing with more clarity. Might just be that I've spent quite a bit of time with my black lights on over my head when I'm inside for this past week. But maybe the furious blinking might have triggered something? I dunno, but I like it and I hope it continues tomorrow and the tomorrows after that!

### cinnamon — 2015-10-01T01:43:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-178323>

@[LBK](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1236/)
Hi Lori!

I've been doing the AM "look at the sun" thing for just a couple of minutes for most of the last 3 years. It's only been in the past three months that I've intentionally spent extended time outdoors in the mornings. And after UBI 24, that's when I went for longer times with no glasses or contacts.

Other things that I'm doing that I hope will help improve my vision:

-   Eating lots of seafood.

-   Sleeping in complete darkness.
-   Getting that circadian rhythm thing down so I activate melatonin while sleeping to allow my body to repair.
-   Avoiding artificial light as much as possible, EXCEPT for red lights and black lights.
-   Eating quite a lot of turmeric. It contains a carotenoid called xanthophyll. [Read this thread](https://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/carotenoids-they-are-hugely-important-in-quantum-biology.16441/) to understand how it might help your eyes. I put about a half to a full teaspoon in a cup of green tea (with butter and coconut oil) each morning. I add a little black pepper into the mix too as it is supposed to really enhance the effectiveness of the turmeric.

And now I see that I need to add "fast and furious blinking" while looking toward the sun to this list!

### Optimalbound — 2015-10-07T13:35:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-178802>

Hi Cinnamon,

I'm also myopic and have been using contact lenses. So I had a look at contact lens specifications, and found interesting differences between different types. [Here's an example.](http://www.optix-now.com/ciba-vision-lenses/dailies-aquacomfort-plus/511-comparing-trueye-to-dailies-aquacomfort.html)

I've been using the Dailies AquaComfort Plus, which are listed as having high UV transmissibility, while some other types block over 95%. On the other hand the AquaComfort has much lower oxygen transmissibility than some other types. While I will definitely use glasses instead of contacts much more than before, I'll keep looking for contacts that have high transmissibility of both UV and O2. I'll post it here if I find anything.

### cinnamon — 2015-10-07T21:39:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-178837>

Thanks for this, @[Optimalbound](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1385/) !

The lenses I use are Bausch & Lomb PureVision. So far as I can tell, they do not intentionally block UV. I'm sure they'd be selling that as a feature if they did!

I did find an abstract of an article from 2006 with a comparison of the UV blocking abilities of several different lenses. [See this link](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16697696). It suggests that PureVision is only moderately UV blocking, unlike several kinds that are specifically marketed to block UV. Now 2006 is a while ago, so it is possible there could be some change to the lens specs since then; however, there is nothing mentioned at all about UV in the current Bausch & Lomb literature about these lenses.

And I'm with you on choosing bare eyes or glasses over contact lenses a whole lot more often than before! I would think that even _some_ blocking (of both oxygen and UV light) might be getting in the way of reaching optimal!

>
> I'm also myopic and have been using contact lenses. So I had a look at contact lens specifications, and found interesting differences between different types. [Here's an example.](http://www.optix-now.com/ciba-vision-lenses/dailies-aquacomfort-plus/511-comparing-trueye-to-dailies-aquacomfort.html)
>
> I've been using the Dailies AquaComfort Plus, which are listed as having high UV transmissibility, while some other types block over 95%. On the other hand the AquaComfort has much lower oxygen transmissibility than some other types. While I will definitely use glasses instead of contacts much more than before, I'll keep looking for contacts that have high transmissibility of both UV and O2. I'll post it here if I find anything.

I would like to know what you find! For now, I'm going with glasses.

### LBK — 2015-10-12T00:29:38+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/cinnamons-journal.11447/post-179139>

> [cinnamon said:](/goto/post?id=178837)
>
> Thanks for this, @[Optimalbound](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1385/) !
>
> The lenses I use are Bausch & Lomb PureVision. So far as I can tell, they do not intentionally block UV. I'm sure they'd be selling that as a feature if they did!
>
> I did find an abstract of an article from 2006 with a comparison of the UV blocking abilities of several different lenses. [See this link](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/16697696). It suggests that PureVision is only moderately UV blocking, unlike several kinds that are specifically marketed to block UV. Now 2006 is a while ago, so it is possible there could be some change to the lens specs since then; however, there is nothing mentioned at all about UV in the current Bausch & Lomb literature about these lenses.
>
> And I'm with you on choosing bare eyes or glasses over contact lenses a whole lot more often than before! I would think that even _some_ blocking (of both oxygen and UV light) might be getting in the way of reaching optimal!

I wore the Acuvue Oasys for years! Thought I was doing something good by blocking UV. Now I know better, of course!
