### JoeBranca — 2014-09-25T08:01:30+0300

>
> The Mojave on I-40 is beautiful...coming down from BArstow is a little ugly...then Big Bear and such. Plenty to enjoy.

Big Bear is a worthy destination. I did a couple of rock climbing / natural movement excursions up there this year inspired by the Jeremy blog. Felt very connected.

And a desert gem: there's those awesome undeground lava tubes at Pisgah crater, off the I-40:

[https://www.google.com/search?q=pis...&ei=WKEjVNLaMILRoATh34CoDA&sqi=2&ved=0CDAQsAQ](https://www.google.com/search?q=pisgah+lava+tubes&espv=2&biw=1366&bih=643&tbm=isch&tbo=u&source=univ&sa=X&ei=WKEjVNLaMILRoATh34CoDA&sqi=2&ved=0CDAQsAQ)

# Thread: Feedback about labs???
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-about-labs.11542/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 3
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tms — 2014-08-20T18:28:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-about-labs.11542/post-138395>

Hi there, I would really appreciate some feedback about my labs.
I don't have much money for good labs and have a super high deductible. Been working with a doctor on my plan for about 8 months. He dx with hypothyroid and low testosterone. He put me on bio identical testosterone but it did nothing so I stopped it. I am still on 130 grains of nature thyroid but don't see much change there. I've been looking over my labs and the numbers seem strange to me. Maybe there are fine but I just want to be sure I'm getting the right treatment. I tried iodine supplementation but has a terrible detox reaction so stopped it. I would try again though with the right supplementation support... Which I did not do thoroughly enough. I am supplementing with selenium now. Hoping to gather some money together good labs soon! There are 3 sets of labs spanning over 6 months.

Symptoms:
Up early.. Cortisol issues
Mood dysregulation
Anxiety
Digestive/ leaky gut stuff
Chronic fatigue
No sex drive
Hard to lose weight although at an average weight (5'6, 145)
Awful pms lasting 2-3 weeks. Cycle has gone from 30 days to 46 days over 8 month span

Labs 12/11/13:
Ferritin 13
Testosterone 37
DHEA 316.4
Free t3 2.52
Free t4 0.62
Tsh 1.77

Labs: 3/5/14:
No DHEA numbers
No ferritin numbers
Testosterone 35
T3 2.44
T4 0.72
Tsh 0.72

Labs 6/8/14
DHEA 205.1
Testosterone 23
T3 3.04
T4 1.14
TSH 0.19

I really appreciate any help!
Thanks
Tara

### nonchalant — 2014-08-21T02:59:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-about-labs.11542/post-138420>

Hi Tara. Not sure about your labs, but I also had problems with iodine. I finally put 1 drop of 2% in a water bottle, and took only a tablespoon each day, and also drank about a half gallon of water within a couple hours of the iodine. Finally I could tolerate it. A test showed that I was extremely deficient in iodine.

Eventually, over a year or two, I was able to take a couple drops/day. But now I don't supplement any more. I just eat seafood daily.

### JanSz — 2014-08-23T18:51:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/feedback-about-labs.11542/post-138715>

Until you figure out how to get better labs, work on
selenium supplementation and Lugol's solution as form of iodine+potassium iodide supplementation.

If you are sure that you had iodine detox problem that likely indicate that you are very high on the other halides.

You must stop taking in new and detoxify those that you already have.

But possibly you were too low on selenium.
2/day-->Super Selenium Complex, 200 mcg 100 capsules (lef.org)

Take selenium for a month, try Lugol's again.

If it happens again, detoxify.

I have noted that there is Detoxamin back available for purchase.
[http://www.detoxamin.ca/index.php?option=com\_content&task=view&id=1&Itemid=2](http://www.detoxamin.ca/index.php?option=com_content&task=view&id=1&Itemid=2)

Few years back I used it myself, highly efficient.
You may want yo get 2-3 jars at once (this may be short supply).

Other way of getting it is thru doctor and compounding pharmacy.

After a month on Detoxamin suppositories, try Lugol's again.

Repeat another 2-3 times if need.

Good iodine/iodide, actually super excess of iodine/iodide is basic need.

In the meant time work also on:
Circadian Rhythm
and
Vit D levels (you want to be at 100 or more)

//

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/august-webinar-will-go-out-tonight.11549/post-138460>

**August 2014 Webinar
_"Where Kruses' Law Comes From"_**
The topic of this month's webinar was created based on what has been recently talked about on the forum - "Kruses' Theorem". Credit is given to Neil "Neil BB" in posing the question in person to Jack and on the forums for the community. Neil wanted to understand what the driving meme of [JackKruse.com](http://JackKruse.com/) is? His thoughts are that there is a general axiom on the site that utilizes Jack's approach, that it would say "any significant technological advancement that man can design or invent has already been incorporated into living systems in some evolutionary way - the key factor is that you just have to look for it." This created the question for Jack, does [JackKruse.com](http://JackKruse.com/) really have any laws? Jack has a different take than Neils' on what the meme of [JackKruse.com](http://JackKruse.com/) is and that is what he is going to be sharing with you on the August 2014 Webinar - "Where Kruses' Law Comes From".

### Hope — 2014-08-22T04:09:48+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/darkening-windows-and-pretty.11550/post-138493>

Hi all,

do any of you have suggestions for how to darken windows to make them not let light through, and in a pretty way? I have to still be able to open blinds/curtains so am limited. I used to have liners before curtains but had to put towels on the top.......to keep light from sneaking through there. But I'm kinda sick of that look.
SO I thought I'd ask how anyone here does theirs.....oh and I'd Love to see a pic. But even a quick description would be so appreciated.

My brain feels tired.. ....from all the organizing, cleaning, unpacking, figuring out what to put where, LOL

### Lahelada — 2014-08-22T04:20:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/darkening-windows-and-pretty.11550/post-138494>

Black out roller blinds and curtains ?
If the blind top offends you, you could fashion it a disguise . A box, a swag or a wooden pelmet,whatever fits with the rest of the decor.

### Hope — 2014-08-22T14:26:51+0300

This was using regular curtains with a blackout liner underneath. no issue with the blind top. I have thought about replacing the current super light blinds with room darkening ones - but they let a little light through at the sides. Unless I put thick curtains over it; I have thought about that.

Oh how I miss the ROLLADEN in Germany.....then, there would be no issue!!! how i wish they had them here, but no

Thanks for responding!!

Hoping the next house I can have a bedroom in a basement.

BUT ..... if a person does CT indoors in a tub w/ flouridated water, what is the potential trade-off between the value of CT, and harm of flouride and chlorine absorbed through the skin??

Just some thoughts to consider .... like Jack says "question everything".

> this is what i was hinting at in my August QandA question re: homeopathy

And I answered it pretty solidly. Hydrogen is a very important element to master. Soon I will get to it.......on the blog but I have mentioned it a lot here.
Water memory is tied 100% to the hydrogen bonding network it finds itself in. The length of hydrogen bonds depends on bond strength, temperature, and pressure. The bond strength itself is dependent on temperature, pressure, bond angle, and environment. This is usually characterized by _local_ [dielectric](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dielectric) constant of the environment the water is within. Water has a very high dielectric constant normally but it has a massive range which is why it is a chameleon for life. This is why life is metastable. Water is a big reason.

Hydrogen is a chemists conundrum, a biologists enigma, and physicists dream. Is it a donor or a collector of electrons? Is it a metal or a gas? Science depends on compelling narratives and few people seem to the know the story the real story behind hydrogen. Hydrogen makes life a cooperative quantum dance. It also holds the key that maybe the Standard Model is inaccurate.

### BTA — 2014-09-09T08:15:56+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/water-memory.11552/post-140366>

Hydrogen is the only element that forms compounds in which the valence electrons are in the _n_ = 1 shell. As a result, hydrogen can have three oxidation states, corresponding to the H+ion, a neutral H atom, and the H- ion.

Hydrogen is both metal & gas at the same time -
[http://www.popsci.com/science/article/2011-11/researchers-turn-hydrogen-gas-metal](http://www.popsci.com/science/article/2011-11/researchers-turn-hydrogen-gas-metal)

# Thread: Duke University Bioelectricity: A Quantitative Approach free course: Aug 25th
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/duke-university-bioelectricity-a-quantitative-approach-free-course-aug-25th.11553/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 12
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Penny — 2014-08-22T06:19:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/duke-university-bioelectricity-a-quantitative-approach-free-course-aug-25th.11553/post-138515>

Uh, I about fell over dead when I saw Duke University offering a free course in \*bioelectricity\* through Coursera... Wow, I sure know how to have fun....

In this class you will learn how to think about electrically active tissue in terms of individual mechanisms, and you will learn to analyze the mechanisms quantitatively as well as describe them qualitatively. The course uses many of the same examples used by Hodgkin and Huxley, who won the Nobel Prize for their experimental unraveling of the mechanisms of the nerve axon of the giant squid, and their creation of a mathematical model of membranes and propagation to understand its function. That work has been the foundational element of most subsequent understandings of electrically active tissue, whether in nerves, the brain or in muscle, including the heart.

In this course, topics include:

1.  The electrical charging of active membranes from the creation and use of differences in ionic concentrations across the membrane.
2.  The stimulation of the membrane, both naturally and from engineered sources.
3.  The creation of action potentials by the membrane in response to stimulation.
4.  The chain reaction of membrane responses, with each small region or cell initiating an action potential in adjacent ones.
5.  The observation of associated electrical currents in terms of extracellular wave voltages they create, the basis of clinical measurements such as the electrocardiogram.

[https://www.coursera.org/course/bioelectricity](https://www.coursera.org/course/bioelectricity)

### nonchalant — 2014-08-22T19:11:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/duke-university-bioelectricity-a-quantitative-approach-free-course-aug-25th.11553/post-138577>

Aw, thanks Penny. I was in a quantum physics class, and chatting with one guy, who was excited that he thought eyesight had quantum elements, if he could just figure out the machine involved. I mentioned water, and he seemed to go into shock, as far as I could tell from a distance. I later gave him some links to Becker and Kruse (this was before Jack's vision blog- he had just gotten to quantum bone and such), and my friend said he had to think things over, and that's the last I heard from him.

That physics class was very time consuming, and I soon fell behind and stopped participating. Actually, the first lecture or two were the best, in my opinion, because they expressed the broad ideas. But then they made certain assumptions about what was worth pursuing, or they explained away certain aspects and made them "non issues". Later classes went deep into the details of the aspects that were 'important', and I lost interest (and I fell hopelessly behind.)

I wanted to spend my time on Jack's website instead. But I kept participating on the Coursera forum. The physics students were more open/inquisitive than I experienced with the biology or genetics classes I took. It was fun, discussing things with them, because they knew far more than I did about physics, but I had some fascinating knowledge that they hadn't encountered before. I'd say something offhand, and someone would want more info, and I had to scramble to find a cite or two to back it up.  I felt like Dr. K, offering one-liners here and there. Some were ignored, some not.

I'd like to do another course, some day.

# Thread: Another Newbie
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-newbie.11557/>
**Subforum:** Meet and Greet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ElCubano — 2014-08-22T16:42:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/another-newbie.11557/post-138550>

Good Friday morning!

Stumbled onto this site when looking for more info on the Leptin Reset. I started it a few weeks ago unfortunately at the time I also started a job slinging boxes in the stockroom of a major department store.
Little bit of hx: 42yo, US Navy vet, Hisp. male, overweight...Masters in Divinity, married, no kids but we're trying to adopt
Little bit of future: getting older, still Hispanic, losing fat, finishing prereqs to apply to medical school

The LR has been really good. I started it to help my wife with her leptin so I get up and cook a B.O.B. every morning and hear her complain every morning. Yet she's not hungry till dinner and while not losing weight her clothes are fitting looser. I'm down a few pounds. So in order to make certain I was doing it right I decided to read more about it and found this endless pit of information here. Feeling more than a little overwhelmed. What I've done so far is to add oysters to my breakfast and got rid of any dairy...well except butter. I've found that throwing 9 eggs, 4 tbsp grassfed butter, 4tbsp coconut oil, & ~5oz raw oysters in a blender and just drinking it works. I only started this "shake" the past two days but I like it...the taste is what it is.

Three weeks with a horrible overnight schedule (1030pm-7am) yet I'm feeling much better than ever. I finally was selected for a "real" job so I will be able to work with my circadian system. I quit my overnight job yesterday and start my new job Monday. Been spending at least 30 minutes in the sun walking barefoot on the ground behind our condo. Working on the EMF stuff which is going to be difficult because of my spouse and her love for wifi and bright lights in the house but persistency will prevail.

Looking forward to becoming optimal as I learn more here and study my way into medical school. I've realized with friends who are physicians that the science that Dr. Kruse acts upon is contrary to the "dogma" of medical school. I'm prepared for that. Heck in Bio101 when pointing out how much ATP we get from glucose vs fats it became clear that fats are the way to go. Yet in the same breath the professor said that fats are horrible for the body and a horrible energy source. So from day one we indoctrinate kids into not thinking about things which doesn't seem to change into graduate/doctoral studies unfortunately.

Well anyway I think I've rambled on enough. Suffice it to say that I'm glad I found this place and looking forward to any and all recommendations. Trying to now get my brain firing on all 12 cylinders as I take these last remaining prereqs and the MCAT...and eventually using my n=1 plus this site's info to treat my future patients.

### Grmoline — 2014-08-26T22:23:29+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/%E2%80%98bad-fat%E2%80%99-may-be-good-for-cancer-patients.11558/post-138966>

I don't understand the link to cancer. The Warburg Effect kicks in with cancer cells due to the disruption in mitochondrial function that blocks the cell's ability to derive energy from the ETC. It can only use glucose as fuel, which is presumably why going ketogenic helps. Fat burning liberates ketones, which healthy cells can use as fuel, but not cancer cells.

### Me2 — 2014-08-26T05:57:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-fest-help.11560/post-138920>

Okay, this EMF info sounds promising. I will go back and read Jacks EMF information. I'm already wondering why it effects sme more than others, though I'm sure he talks about it in his posts. I do work in education where we have wifi, and I have wifi at home as well. Obviously I have no control over work. Can you point me to a post with tips for dealing with such environments?

@[Inger](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1057/) by grounding are you talking about earthing? I know what you mean about sun! I find about an hour a day including my stomach and I'm optimal. Sad thing is I live in Colorado and most of the time that's not possible. I did buy a uvb lamp, not sure what peoples thoughts are on that. And thank you for the positive affirmation that I can heal, I've been thinking about it and reminding myself.

@[cantweight](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/1283/) thank you for your story, how do you test for emf?

Random thought: what about fluorescent lights?

Okay here's my story. I will try and keep it short.

**Childhood**:I was raised a vegetarian. We did eat occasional meat, never seafood, some eggs, and no dairy. This still makes me cringe to think of my body trying to go through puberty on such a diet but my mom was a hippy in boulder and that was the major dogma at that time. Chronic constipation since birth.
**Young adult**: During that time I even went on a fat free diet! Aagh. Within a couple of years of the beginning of my period I developed severe dysmenorrhea wherein my period was so painful I would quite literally blackout, vomit, and miss a day of school or work almost every month. I'm pretty sure I'm estrogen dominant. I was also a big weekend drinker. By 18 I was a size 10, which I've pretty much maintained ever since, sometimes going to 8.
**18-20s**: I began to get recurring UTIs that no doctor would do anything about but give me antibiotics. This went on through to 2012 when I had my last one. I've done so many antibiotics it's gross. Still a drinker. Went gf in 2006 and lost a ton of inflammation all over and some weight. I suffered from deep depression and impulsivity starting @14.
**29-now**: I was a teacher at a high poverty school. I completely burned out my adrenals and developed hashimotos thyrodytis, could barely even get out of bed to eat on the weekends. I couldn't quit as my husband and I need the money. I had a third life crisis and started meditating and cut drinking, dropped all my friends. In 2010 I found marks daily apple and went primal. In 2011 I started gaps to address constipation. It also addressed my depression and impulsivity. In 2012 I did the lepton reset while on gaps, then got a uti and candida symptoms began and insidiously spread until they came to a head in March. And here I am. Fighting for my health for 4 years and in some ways worse than before.

Need to know anything else?

I knew if anyone could help, it would be the Kruse crew.

Now it sounds like I have a lot of reading to do.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-10-30T00:46:39+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/candida-fest-help.11560/post-145587>

Your issue is tied to Tensegrity 7: An important feature of cholesterol sulfate is that it is amphiphilic, due to its LARGE NEGATIVE charge, and thus it can travel freely in the bloodstream. If you happen to be real anemic or has a real low redox chronically this could set the tone for many brain gut diseases to develop. I spoke about it in the King of Pop blog. Why?

The electrostatic charge in the gut is the short answer.

So what could screw with your diseases or redox in this case? Anything with a MASSIVE POSITIVE CHARGE.

Let us consider candida or yeast, since you brought it up. Actually any bacteria in the gut or blood stream can allow for the development of a positive charge really. So let's consider candida: It makes a chemical called acetaldehyde. It happens to be a highly electrophilic **_atomically small_** radius molecule. Any acetaldehyde emitted by yeast metabolism in the intestinal villi will easily pass through the intestinal wall and have unrestricted access to the components circulating in the bloodstream and the GALT (RBCs, albumin, cholesterol sulfate, etc.) If acetaldehyde binds irreversibly to cholesterol sulfate in the bloodstream or the GALT, attracted to its negative charge, this would disable all of the downstream functions of this cholesterol sulfate molecule. This will destroy cell membrane chemistry and alter DHA in the CM. This means your circadian biology gets destroyed. This is how candida hurts you. The body then attempts to compensate by increasing the amount of un-sulfated cholesterol as raw material for the sulfating mechanisms in those with yeast. This is how you as a yeast infected person travels thru the world with a nitrogen based O2 and glucose mechanism. This would demolish the mitochondria as cells divide and disease results.

The cure?

How do you alter a positive charge?

With a negative one.

What captures electrons best in all eukaryotes?

DHA.

GAME SET MATCH.

# Thread: Chinese Healthcare Woes...Will They Become Ours?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-healthcare-woes-will-they-become-ours.11566/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-08-23T21:03:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/chinese-healthcare-woes-will-they-become-ours.11566/post-138721>

> Violence against doctors in China has become a familiar occurrence. In September, 2011, a calligrapher in Beijing, dissatisfied with his throat-cancer treatment, stabbed a doctor seventeen times. In May, 2012, a woman attacked a young nurse in Nanjing with a knife because of complications from an operation performed sixteen years earlier. In a two-week period this February, angry patients paralyzed a nurse in Nanjing, cut the throat of a doctor in Hebei, and beat a Heilongjiang doctor to death with a lead pipe. A survey by the China Hospital Management Association found that violence against medical personnel rose an average of twenty-three per cent each year between 2002 and 2012. By then, Chinese hospitals were reporting an average of twenty-seven attacks a year, per hospital.

Here is where it gets interesting....

> If the official channels yield nothing, patients will often stage protests. In 2013, a thirty-four-year-old mother alleging malpractice after a Cesarean section rallied a dozen friends and relatives to storm Beijing’s No. 6 Hospital. She ended up winning about ten thousand dollars in compensation. In May this year, in Kunming, a man whose wife and baby had died during a Cesarean section climbed onto the roof of the hospital carrying his other child in his arms. He threatened to jump while a crowd of relatives below brought traffic to a standstill. When a patient dies and family members blame the doctors, they sometimes lay the corpse in front of the hospital entrance until they are paid off.
>
> In Beijing, I met Benjamin Liebman, a professor at Columbia Law School, who has published a study on “malpractice mobs” in China. He told me that protests consistently extract more money from hospitals than legal proceedings do. Family members can even hire professional protesters. One report in Shenzhen mentioned an average price of fifty yuan a day for the service of a protester. The radiologist in Shanghai told me, “If your mother dies in the hospital, there will be an agency that comes to you and says, ‘We can help you. We can have twenty guys who can come to the hospital, blackmail them, and share fifty per cent of the profits.’ They’re very professional.”
>
> Surprisingly, in a country where even a small public gathering can spark a government response, malpractice mobs often proceed without intervention. It’s possible that the authorities see them as justified or simply as an efficient method for negotiating compensation. Some scholars even argue that the Chinese government tacitly welcomes such protests, considering them to be a relatively harmless outlet for discontent and an early-warning signal of more serious social unrest—a phenomenon that the Berkeley political scientist Peter Lorentzen, in a study of authoritarian regimes, has called “regularizing rioting.” Liebman told me, “Protest becomes the mechanism for providing social security and for distinguishing people who need help.” The problem is, he said, “everyone knows the government has funds to pay people who petition or protest, and that incentivizes more protest.”

[http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2014/08/25/under-the-knife?src=mp](http://www.newyorker.com/magazine/2014/08/25/under-the-knife?src=mp)


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════

### Aerose91 — 2014-08-24T06:15:51+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-important-is-elevation-to-disease-recovery.11569/post-138747>

Under the obvious pretense that there is less oxygen the higher you go, where does this lie in order of importance for Neolithic disease reversal? Would 4,000-6,000 feet hinder any potential progress?

I know athletes can greatly increase performance with altitude training but in the case of mitochondrial damage and hypoperfusion would the opposite happen?

Thanks for any help

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-24T18:53:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-important-is-elevation-to-disease-recovery.11569/post-138773>

Let's just say the effect is huge. My current year long biohack is on this very topic.

O2 is special quantum gas magnetically.

The oxygen molecule has 2 electrons in unpaired orbits in it making it a paramagnetic gas. This means it is susceptible to attraction by a magnetic field. Many people do not realize that most other gases are weakly diamagnetic and repell magnetic fields. This is a fundamental quantum effect of oxygen that gives each individual mitochondria its own magnetic field.

After Tensegrity 4 you might see how this matters a big deal.

**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-25T04:41:30+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/massive-paper-importance-the-gut-link-polyp-bhb.11574/post-138803>

[http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00260/full](http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/fphys.2014.00260/full)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-25T04:43:14+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/massive-paper-importance-the-gut-link-polyp-bhb.11574/post-138804>

When do you think people are going to make the link yet? inorganic phosphate is bound up by our mitochondria to stop calcium efflux? It always comes back to calcium. People need to look at the ion frequency resonance of calcium and they will be stunned at what they find. Y'all already heard that story.

"These exciting findings indicate that polyP has a dual effect on mPTP activity—promoting the transient opening of Ca2+-induced mPTP opening which can prevent mitochondria from Ca2+ overload. On the other hand, polyP was required for protection against oxidative stress-induced mPTP opening and cell death."

PolyP = ketosis and a huge benefit to mammals.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-25T04:44:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/massive-paper-importance-the-gut-link-polyp-bhb.11574/post-138805>

It is always found near electron dense clusters. Surprise!

PolyP, a negatively charged polyanion, may bind with extracellular Ca2+, inhibit LPS-induced Ca2+ influx, and result in a decreased expression of iNOS mRNA (Harada et al., 2013). How does it get its negative charge? ELECTRONS

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-25T04:45:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/massive-paper-importance-the-gut-link-polyp-bhb.11574/post-138806>

Using polyP of various chain lengths (14, 60, and 130 Pi residues) it was demonstrated that polyP with longer chains (130 Pi residues) was more potent than those with shorter chains in suppressing LPS-induced iNOS expression. Once again showing size and shape determine thermodynamics which impacts physiologic ability. Compliant design 101 from OSF 3.

have a careful look at the gut microbe section...........the paleo purveyors will never believe this story because it is a franchise breaker.

### nonchalant — 2014-08-25T04:55:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/massive-paper-importance-the-gut-link-polyp-bhb.11574/post-138807>

# Thread: I Bet This is EMF/Protein Folding Dependent -Spinal Morphology Stuff
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-bet-this-is-emf-protein-folding-dependent-spinal-morphology-stuff.11580/>
**Subforum:** Educating Doctors
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-08-25T20:27:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/i-bet-this-is-emf-protein-folding-dependent-spinal-morphology-stuff.11580/post-138862>

> Effects of N-cadherin disruption on spine morphological dynamics
>
> Structural changes at synapses are thought to be a key mechanism for the encoding of memories in the brain. Recent studies have shown that changes in the dynamic behavior of dendritic spines accompany bidirectional changes in synaptic plasticity, and that the disruption of structural constraints at synapses may play a mechanistic role in spine plasticity. While the prolonged disruption of N-cadherin, a key synaptic adhesion molecule, has been shown to alter spine morphology, little is known about the short-term regulation of spine morphological dynamics by N-cadherin. With time-lapse, confocal imaging in cultured hippocampal neurons, we examined the progression of structural changes in spines following an acute treatment with AHAVD, a peptide known to interfere with the function of N-cadherin. We characterized fast and slow timescale spine dynamics (minutes and hours, respectively) in the same population of
> spines. We show that N-cadherin disruption leads to enhanced spine motility and reduced length, followed by spine loss. The structural effects are accompanied by a loss of functional connectivity. Further, we demonstrate that early structural changes induced by AHAVD treatment, namely enhanced motility and reduced length, are indicators for later spine fate, i.e., spines with the former changes are more
> likely to be subsequently lost. Our results thus reveal the short-term regulation of synaptic structure by N-cadherin and suggest that some forms of morphological dynamics may be potential readouts for subsequent, stimulus-induced rewiring in neuronal networks.

[http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/neuro.03.001.2007/abstract](http://journal.frontiersin.org/Journal/10.3389/neuro.03.001.2007/abstract)

# Thread: Is deionized water as good, worse or better than RO?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-deionized-water-as-good-worse-or-better-than-ro.11581/>
**Subforum:** Biohacking 101
**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kyrakitty — 2014-08-25T23:04:10+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-deionized-water-as-good-worse-or-better-than-ro.11581/post-138871>

So I thought the water delivered to my home was reverse osmosis but it turns out it is actually deionized water. Does anyone have any idea if deionized water is the same, better or worse than RO? I can't tell by what I read on line :/

Thanks ya'll

### nonchalant — 2014-08-26T01:16:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-deionized-water-as-good-worse-or-better-than-ro.11581/post-138879>

After reading the wikipedia entry for purified water, I'd say no, Kyra.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-26T01:28:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-deionized-water-as-good-worse-or-better-than-ro.11581/post-138884>

What does ionized mean? It means electron affinity or electron loving or electron dense.

What does deionized mean? It means lack of electron affinity, electron loving, or electron density.

Ionization energies measure the tendency of a neutral atom to resist the loss of electrons. It takes a considerable amount of energy, for example, to remove an electron from a neutral fluorine atom to form a _positively charged ion_.

What do you think now of deionized water for life based upon what you have been learning here?

### kyrakitty — 2014-08-26T03:54:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-deionized-water-as-good-worse-or-better-than-ro.11581/post-138894>

### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-08-26T02:54:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/limitation-to-scientific-enquiry.11583/post-138890>

as we all agree, there is a vast limitation to RCT in that it seeks to take a given cohort, take 68% of a certain finding and pass it off as new found knowledge that applies to all

i was listening to this interview with deepak chopra on my way to work this morning, where towards the end he talks of Quallia

Deepak is talking about the human condition by which we give an arising a name, and then it becomes reality.
he is particularly talking about the electromagnetic field which is interesting.

i particularly like the challenge he throws to doctors:

do you believe that life is biology? YES
and that biology is biochemistry? YES
and that biochemistry is chemistry? YES
and that chemistry is physics? YES
and that physics is quantum physics? YES
and that quantum physics is mathematics? YES

and where or what is mathematics? ............

he is eluding to the fact that these are all quallia in themselves, and that the true reality or consciousness is that which is truly perceiving, and if one looks for empirical evidence of what this IS, it cannot be found anywhere but in our own experience. this is essentially non-duality...

> and that biology is biochemistry? YES
> and that biochemistry is chemistry? YES
> and that chemistry is physics? YES
> and that physics is quantum physics? YES
> and that quantum physics is mathematics? YES
>

This is the main reason I love this blog.
I'm finally getting the answers I sought 25 years ago.

Marie

PS I switched from Physics because there were more cute guys in the Biochem program. Ah... to be young and hormonal.

### Greenisland — 2014-09-10T17:57:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/why-evidence-based-medicine-has-no-evidence.11586/post-140544>

I have been seeing this "low protein" promotion for pregnant women and I am wondering where in the hell they get this from?? Most of us home birth midwives were trained in the Brewer diet, which recommends 80-120 grams of protein a day minimum, 2 eggs a day, whole milk products, butter, green veggies. During the 1960s and 1970s Dr. Tom Brewer was able to reduce his pre-eclampsia/toxemia/pregnancy induced hypertension/preterm labor complications in his practice to less than 1%, using only this diet. None of his OB compatriots would listen, but he kept getting calls from midwives. At first he blew them off but soon realized they were the only ones interested in his research. So he took midwives under his wing and his legacy lives on. The Brewer diet is pretty standard in the natural childbirth world although there has been some pushback in the last few years..
[www.blueribbonbaby.org](http://www.blueribbonbaby.org)

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-26T15:33:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-reversing-aging-by-restoring-nad-levels.11588/post-138943>

When you eat a ketogenic diet guess what else happens? It is believed we bypass complex one at the inner mitochondrial membrane and enter FADH2. I used to believe this but I no longer do because of technology light. You heard about this in the Quantum electron post.........but what did not I tell you? Why does a ketogenic diet work in T1D, T2D, and to lose weight? When you lose water conduction at the inner mitochondrial membrane you lose the ability of proper nanoscopic protein folding of cytochrome 1. This throws off the nanoscopic precision required for quantum tunneling and this cause increased ROS and metabolic syndrome........This is also why niacin can work in these diseases because it is a ketone mimic drug (sans blue light). Why? Niacin also known as vitamin B3, nicotinic acid and vitamin PP, is an organic compound with the formula C6H5NO2 and one of the 40 to 80 essential human nutrients. In fact, when niacin deficiency is present it is one of 5 vitamins that causes a pandemic disease condition called pellagra. But it can be used to bypass a "broken" cytochrome 1 when misfolding (due to a chronic ATP deficiency think EMF 7). But why ? Why am I tormenting you with this science,.......because you need to think about things........and you will see where QED takes us..........Vitamin B3 can help activate PPP when we are broken for some reason when we are ketotic from a dietary standpoint using vast amounts of coherent water as I laid out in the quantum electron blog post. ( ) Yes, kathylu, niacin is a precursor to NAD+/NADH (cytochrome 1) and NADP+/NADPH (the magic of the PPP), which play essential metabolic roles in all living cells as I showed in EMF 4. Niacin cannot be directly converted to nicotinamide, but both compounds could be converted to NAD+ and NADP+ in vivo and this information is very valuable when you have liver leptin resistance, metabolic syndrome, or epilepsy. When cytochrome 1 has misfolded electron transport proteins (quinolone issue of CoEnQ10) you need to bypass it 100% of the time by eating fats predominately because if you don't, your mitochondria begin to make massive ROS that overwhelms the cell lowers vitamin D and all hormones, causes pregnenolone steal syndrome, and eventually shortens its telomeres to cause cellular signaling problems that lead to either senescence or cancer. The reason why? NAD+ builds up in relation to NADH levels on a per unit basis. There is a special case for blue light where a ketogenic diet can fatten you if you are chronically destroying cytochrome 1. Then a fat-laden diet during spring and summer can make you very obese. Increasing the NAD+ in comparison to NADH changes a most of the mechanistic downstream signaling in protein kinase B pathways of cells. Why is that big? The AKT pathway is also known as protein kinase B (PKB). It is a serine/threonine-specific protein kinase that plays a key role in multiple cellular processes such as **glucose metabolism, apoptosis, cell proliferation, transcription and cell migration.** Akt1 is involved in cellular survival pathways, by inhibiting apoptotic processes. Akt1 is also able to induce protein synthesis pathways (urea cycle), and is, therefore, a key signaling protein in the cellular pathways that lead to skeletal muscle hypertrophy, and general tissue growth. Since it can block apoptosis (cancer risk), and thereby promote cell survival, Akt1 has been implicated as a major factor in many types of cancer. Akt (now also called Akt1) was originally identified as the oncogene in the transforming retrovirus, AKT8. UVA light can help restore apoptosis. In a mouse (nocturnal) which is null for Akt1 but normal for Akt2, glucose homeostasis is unperturbed, but the animals are smaller, consistent with a role for Akt1 in growth. In contrast, mice which do not have Akt2, but have normal Akt1, have mild growth deficiency and display a diabetic phenotype (insulin resistance/ T2D), again consistent with the idea that Akt2 is more specific for the insulin receptor signaling pathway. The role of Akt3 is less clear, though it appears to be predominantly expressed in the brain. It has been reported that mice lacking Akt3 have small brains. This is what causes brain shrinkage in all the neurodegenerative disorders that are linked to cytochrome 1 issues. PCOS is tied to this early one before the more chronic diseases come to the forefront.
Akt2 is an important signaling molecule in the Insulin signaling pathway. It is required to induce glucose transport.

The follow through? AKT1 is involved in the PI3K/AKT/mTOR pathway and other signaling pathways. These all cause massive changes in cell cycle signaling and in cellular communication. mTOR is huge in longevity and in cancer progression via the dis-regulation of the p 53 gene. If the high oxygen pathways are defective for any reason cancer is likely. (TCA/urea cycle)

Now you can see why when this pathway is activated many bad things can happen that are common to T1D, T2D, most cases of leptin resistance with elevations in HS CRP. These steps are stops on the way to either final pathway and we call them neolithic diseases. Yes.......you got an insight into Quantum Metabolic syndrome early.

We are not making it complicated......it is. This response is the proof of just how complicated it is.......and you need to have a lot of science background to understand this complexity......believe it or not I leave a lot of it out on purpose.......but not in this response.

### Penny — 2014-08-26T21:19:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/question-reversing-aging-by-restoring-nad-levels.11588/post-138961>

So, are we talking orthomolecular doses of niacin/niacinimide/inositol hexacinimate of 3 grams per day?

And I also read that tryptophan converts into niacin at a rate of 1.5% - it also relieves stress although I also read beta carotene will itself become oxidative once it donates its electrons, thus retinyl palmitate might be a better antioxidant - and it also looks like adding pectin will improve its antioxidant ability - although Jack probably wouldn't approve of that apple a day thing...

I believe he is quoting Hoffer here:

[http://www.doctoryourself.com/hoffer\_niacin.html](http://www.doctoryourself.com/hoffer_niacin.html)

"The catecholamines, of which adrenalin is the best known example, and the aminochromes, of which adrenochrome is the best known example, are intimately involved in stress reactions. Therefore to moderate the influence of stress or to negate it, one must use compounds which prevent these substances from damaging the body. Vitamin B-3 is a specific antidote to adrenalin, and the antioxidants such as vitamin C, Vitamin E, beta carotene, selenium and others protect the body against the effect of the free radicals by removing them more rapidly from the body. Any disease or condition which is stress related ought therefore to respond to the combined use of vitamin B-3 and these antioxidants provided they are all given in optimum doses, whether small or large as in orthomolecular therapy"

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23776395](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23776395)

**Anti-oxidative activity of pectin and its stabilizing effect on retinyl palmitate.**
[Ro J](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Ro%20J%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395)1, [Kim Y](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Kim%20Y%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Kim H](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Kim%20H%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Jang SB](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Jang%20SB%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Lee HJ](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Lee%20HJ%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Chakma S](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Chakma%20S%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Jeong JH](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Jeong%20JH%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395), [Lee J](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed?term=Lee%20J%5BAuthor%5D&cauthor=true&cauthor_uid=23776395).
**[Author information](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/23776395#)**

**Abstract**
The purpose of this study was to examine the anti-oxidative activity of pectin and other polysaccharides in order to develop a cosmeceutical base having anti-oxidative effects towards retinyl palmitate (RP). The anti-oxidative stabilizing effects of pectin and other polysaccharides on RP were evaluated by DPPH assay and then the stabilizing effect of pectin on RP was examined as a function of time. Among the polysaccharides we examined, pectin exhibited a considerably higher anti-oxidative activity, with an approximately 5-fold greater DPPH radical scavenging effect compared to other polysaccharides. The DPPH radical scavenging effect of pectin increased gradually with increasing concentrations of pectin. At two different RP concentrations, 0.01 and 0.1% in ethanol, addition of pectin improved the stability of RP in a concentration dependent manner. The stabilizing effect of pectin on RP was more effective for the lower concentration of RP (0.01%, v/v). Further, degradation of RP was reduced following the addition of pectin as measured over 8 hours. From the results obtained, it can be suggested that pectin may be a promising ingredient for cosmeceutical bases designed to stabilize RP or other pharmacological agents subject to degradation by oxidation.

### Luz — 2014-10-17T12:07:09+0300

Hello,

Here is an update video on the subject:

The goal is to mimic a caloric restricted diet with low blood glucose and to increase NAD+/NAD Ratio.

To me its sounds like a ketogenic diet (which I personally love it) but instead of eliminating the carbs they add oxaloacetate to the diet.

From this paper

[http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/87165](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/87165)

It is clear that if we artificially elevate oxaloacetate we will decrease the production of ketones body in the liver.

I would like to have some opinion on benefit or not of oxaloacetate supplement.

Thanks for any inputs.

# Thread: Best way to measure temp of water

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Tms — 2014-08-26T17:19:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-measure-temp-of-water.11589/post-138947>

Hi! I'm just trying out face dunking. Definitely an invigorating experience. But Im kinda flying blind because my thermometer will not measure water temp, only my temp. Which one do I do get to measure water temp?
Thanks!
Tara

### nicld — 2014-08-26T17:27:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-measure-temp-of-water.11589/post-138949>

I use a basic meat thermometer from Walmart. Some have a pool thermometer too.

[http://www.amazon.com/Weber-6492-Or...d=1409063185&sr=8-1&keywords=meat+thermometer](http://www.amazon.com/Weber-6492-Original-Instant-Read-Thermometer/dp/B009IH0BZ0/ref=sr_1_1?ie=UTF8&qid=1409063185&sr=8-1&keywords=meat+thermometer)

### nonchalant — 2014-08-26T18:11:40+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/best-way-to-measure-temp-of-water.11589/post-138953>

Tara, so glad you tried it! It is scary, the first time, to actually put your face in that water, especially when your skin gets close enough to feel the cold air above it!  But it's surprising how easily the face can handle the cold. Just think, your body is already starting to make some changes to prepare for colder temps that may be coming.

I'd just put a few trays of ice in the bowl and let them melt about half way. But I'm sure you're curious about the temp. I bought a laser thermometer similar to this: [http://www.amazon.com/Nubee®-Temper...ometer/dp/B00CVHIJDK/ref=cm\_cr\_pr\_product\_top](http://www.amazon.com/Nubee%C2%AE-Temperature-Non-contact-Infrared-Thermometer/dp/B00CVHIJDK/ref=cm_cr_pr_product_top)

But any food thermometer that gets down low enough in temp will work.

# Thread: How many Calories and Fat does a Cold Bath burn per hour? Exercise + Ketosis vs. CT + Ketosis
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 5
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColinGorham — 2014-08-26T22:59:03+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/post-138968>

On Average how many calories and fat does CT plus a Ketogenic Diet, specifically a cold bath 50 to 55 degrees, burn per hour above BMR? I am curious what the difference in fat and calorie burning might be between 1) Exercise (60% Peak VO2) + Ketogenic Diet vs. 2) CT + Ketogenic Diet?

The reason that I ask is because during a cold water bath the total calories burned can be measured with Indirect Calorimetry and what source the energy is being derived from can be measured with RQ (Respiratory Quotient) on a scale of .72 (all fat being burned) and 1 (all carbohydrates being burned). Studies by Jeff Volek and Stephen Phinney support Jack's (and others' contentions) that a ketogenic diet clearly aids in fuel partitioning toward fat burning especially during exercise. For instance, on Average, at 60% peak VO2: With a Non-ketogenic diet exercise is 95% glycogen dependant and 5% fat dependant. With a Ketogenic diet exercise is 22% glycogen dependant and 78% fat dependant. Please read the Art & Science of Low Carbohydrate Nutrition or the Art & Science of Low Carbohydrate Performance Books for the controlled studies on this point. Peter Attia, shows this precise fuel partitioning phenomenon in his N=1 experiment with Indirect Calorimetry and RQ data here: [http://eatingacademy.com/how-a-low-carb-diet-affected-my-athletic-performance](http://eatingacademy.com/how-a-low-carb-diet-affected-my-athletic-performance)

Please try again later.", "lightbox\_start\_slideshow": "Start slideshow", "lightbox\_stop\_slideshow": "Stop slideshow", "lightbox\_full\_screen": "Full screen", "lightbox\_thumbnails": "Thumbnails", "lightbox\_download": "Download", "lightbox\_share": "Share", "lightbox\_zoom": "Zoom", "lightbox\_new\_window": "New window", "lightbox\_toggle\_sidebar": "Toggle sidebar" }

### Marie — 2014-08-27T02:03:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/post-138978>

Why are you worrying about calories?

Marie

### Jack Kruse — 2014-08-27T04:11:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/post-138989>

how mismatched are you and then you can figure it out.

### Hansen Kenimer — 2014-12-04T00:21:44+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/post-149135>

It seems to me that if:

-   The **large calorie**, **kilogram calorie**, **dietary calorie**, **nutritionist's calorie**, **nutritional calorie**, **Calorie** (capital C)[\[2\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calorie#cite_note-2) or **food calorie**(symbol: Cal) is approximately the amount of energy needed to raise the temperature of one kilogram of water by one degree Celsius. The large calorie is thus equal to 1000 small calories or one **kilocalorie** (symbol: kcal).[\[1\]](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Calorie#cite_note-Webster-1)

Then: if you knew exactly the number of kg of water you have in your tub when you got in at, say, 10 degrees C, you could measure the increase in water temperature (say it went up to 13 degrees C) when you got out and just do the math. I would think doing CT when ketogenic would get you pretty close to 100% in terms of fat burning...I would certainly think it would be considerably more than with exercise in ketosis.

Of course, as an American dealing in Fahrenheit and pounds instead of Celsius and kilos, this is considerably more work. ;-)

### kinzals — 2015-04-06T16:27:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-many-calories-and-fat-does-a-cold-bath-burn-per-hour-exercise-ketosis-vs-ct-ketosis.11591/post-160420>

It's impossible to determine exactly how many calories you burn during a particular activity. Heavier people will burn more calories than a lighter person. Some people will be more aggressive than others and they will burn more calories. Use Careot [Nutrition Tracker](http://careot.net/) tool for burning calories and fat.

**Posts:** 4
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh — 2014-08-27T03:00:18+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/interesting-and-cool-institute-for-venture-science-from-pollack-and-friends.11594/post-138985>

[http://www.theinstituteforventurescience.org/](http://www.theinstituteforventurescience.org/)

> **Funding Promising Ideas that Challenge Conventional Thinking**
> The Institute for Venture Science (IVS) is conceived to fund high-risk non-traditional scientific inquiries that are likely to yield important breakthroughs. The IVS identifies the most promising challenges to prevailing scientific paradigms. It then funds multiple research groups simultaneously to pursue each idea.
>
> A dozen groups pursuing the same idea cannot be ignored. When challenge paradigms and prevailing paradigms compete on equal footing, the better approach will prevail.
>
> The IVS strategy is aimed at producing revolutionary outcomes — outcomes that could greatly enhance the realization of a cure for cancer, a reliable predictor of earthquakes, a cheap form of clean energy and so much more. It can solve many of the world's seemingly intractable problems, while opening a new era of understanding.

### Penny — 2014-08-27T04:20:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-138991>

They tried dissolving it with DMSO, but it was too big, so they just removed it surgically - her left side is still wonky but better than before they took out the clot and her speech is slurred - I have been looking for a blog on this site that talked about strokes - I thought there was one? She is undoubtedly MTHFR 1298 and maybe 03 - has had a couple miscarriages - they will remove the rest of the clot with DMSO and told us the next 24 hours will be critical - I read in the Earthing book how that is helpful, but probably would do nothing while whe is in the hospital except maybe donate electrons by holding her hand - assuming I had any at this point...  So, if anyone has any ideas that might help her, I am all ears - or even where the stroke blog is ?
thanks!

### Penny — 2014-08-27T06:49:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-138998>

Thanks Caroline - that was a great blog - it at least answered a question I had about blue light and being on the computer all day - it seemed to me that you are damned if you look at the screen without the glasses because you're getting too much blue light and damned if you put on blue blockers because you screw up the photoperiod/circadean clock... I of course am totally happy when I'm outside and not on a computer but alas, my children are addicted - mother-in-law has all the modern death traps - a brand new big screen tv that is always on, the cordless phone with the base station 5 feet from the chair she is always in, the cell phone next to her - she had her cataracts done recently also - it's funny, well, not funny really, I was just reading this guy - again - Gestalt Genova from the phoenix rising forum who had been "Accutaned" at 15 and developed fibro and fatigue and all that stuff and he said he got better using glutamine for his leaky gut which was causing his immune system to go nuts every time he ate anything - but then he took Indole-3-carbinol and DIM because it helped his estrogen issues because he had the COMT and a couple of cyp SNPs which caused him to not break down estrogen - he also took d-glucarate - I thought he probably had missed the EMF boat like so many of them do, but didn't know the mechanism - and let's face it, I only could fathom 20% of CPC 6 -

I was thinking my mother-in-law could be more affected by the stuff in EMF 4 but it all ties in - she is deficient in electrons, hates the sun, and is surrounded by EMF and blue light - funny, I used to think that everyone's adrenals were blown that couldn't deal with the sun, but now I realize - I think it's true anyway - that it's a vitamin A story just like that second hormone 101 blog Dr. K wrote - which totally cracked me up

Well, there is a silver lining in the cloud - my sister-in-law does Reiki...

I just found a site that blocks blue light from electronic devices - some kind of filter for the device:
[https://www.lowbluelights.com/detail.asp?id=116](https://www.lowbluelights.com/detail.asp?id=116)

### caroline — 2014-08-27T08:38:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-139011>

I am pretty sure I am getting more and more confused with each passing day ...and each passing blog!

I absolutely get the big picture .....but I am floundering in the details lately.

Dr.K. did talk about ICU and all the bright lites and equipement etc. He said in his training one of the doctors would always insist on limiting the lighting etc. for the babies born early.

Could you limit the lighting and hug her a lot? ...... I suppose just rubbing her back or her feet or holding her hand would be huge what about soft music? my choice would be Pink Floyd...lol

and then there is what JK told us ....if your redox is good ...you can cope with what our modern world is dishing out!

I seem to think if we are really thinking and doing what we can ...and then thinking some more ....and always question everything......we may stand a chance....

### caroline — 2014-08-27T08:39:07+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-139012>

what about eye shades? and a MHS???

### caroline — 2014-08-27T08:45:04+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-139014>

I wish we could see a pic of you ...... I like bunnies ....but I like people much better......

I may never shut up here .... sorry!

Do you remember when Dr. K's FIL was so sick? He felt pretty terrible that he couldn't do much - and his FIL had never listened to him anyway. Even tho Jack has told me a million times that not everyone makes it ...it still hurts when it is personal. I still have to re visit that conversation with him.

Even tho we love some one so much ..... we really can only help ourselves.

Is you DH on board with all this?

### Penny — 2014-08-27T17:05:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/my-92-year-old-mother-in-law-just-had-a-stroke-there-was-a-clot-in-her-brain.11595/post-139041>

DH thinks Jack is nuts - because DH read one of the early EMF blogs - not sure which one - but DH is quite well versed in physics/electronics/chemistry - in fact, DH thinks he has solved the universal field theory - weirdly, I think he could actually wrap his head around this stuff with aplomb, but alas, you know how when you're stuck in your paradigm it is tough to think outside that box... I told another engineer last weekend when we had a party on top of a mountain about one of those electricsense interviews where the engineer got brain cancer and asked the brain surgeon why and the brain surgeon said it might be EMF - so he traced it down to his alarm clock at 12 milligaus that had been sitting 6 inches from his head for years... now he has a site and is devoted to spreading the message - but I bet if you had told him prior to that experience that it cause him problems, he would have also thought you were nuts...

so the engineer I was talking to started thinking about how he had this alarm clock by his head for the past 40 years - from a child really... and he wanted more information - so you have to chip away at people - my constant bitchfest about the blue blockers and the computer are even making headway as my oldest son told me his eyes were hurting and so he put them on -

Their friends are even asking me questions now - like why they have constant headaches and their hormones are all screwed up - 2 years ago they would roll their eyes and walk away -

I think it's the best we can hope for really -

Wow, the EMF in the room was insane... it wasn't even ICU... there is a computer 5 feet from the bed, some electronic thingy that I think causes her legs to be compressed automatically at the foot of the bed, a whole rack of outlets behind her in case of emergencies - I held her hand for awhile in the fond hopes of making a dent, what else can anyone do?

Taubes and Attia wont solve anything. Read this: [http://www.washingtonpost.com/natio...6a72f2-28c7-11e4-958c-268a320a60ce\_story.html](http://www.washingtonpost.com/national/health-science/is-it-what-we-eat-or-that-we-overeat-a-look-at-the-effort-to-figure-out-why-were-fat/2014/08/25/556a72f2-28c7-11e4-958c-268a320a60ce_story.html)

Look at the topic of the article. The question they ask is not the right question. It is not food or lack of exercise that make you fat. It is a lack of circadian signaling between the nucleus and mitochondrial DNA that does. There are very few people in our world who ever ask the right questions of science. The ones who do affect science and our future most profoundly. Taubes and Attia are not these people in my opinion.

### HoneyChild — 2014-08-27T14:41:06+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/nusi-article-from-wired-about-new-diet-research.11596/post-139032>

"Many studies have suggested that diets tend to work for two months, some for as long as six. "That's what the data show. And then everybody's weight comes roaring back." **During his own time working night shifts, Lustig gained 3st, which he never lost** and now uses exuberantly to make two points. The first is that weight is extremely hard to lose, and the second – more important, I think – is that he's no diet and fitness guru himself. He doesn't want everybody to be perfect: he's just a guy who doesn't want to surrender civilisation to diseases caused by industry. "I'm not a fitness guru," he says, puckishly. "I'm 45lb overweight!""
[http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2014/aug/24/robert-lustig-sugar-poison](http://www.theguardian.com/lifeandstyle/2014/aug/24/robert-lustig-sugar-poison)

# Thread: What exactly is the Fournier Effect?

**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 10
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Sammy — 2014-08-27T07:34:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-exactly-is-the-fournier-effect.11598/post-139003>

Hi all,
I was hoping whether someone can explain the Fournier Effect. I did not find any information about the Fournier effect by google search. I have read here and there in the forum and from what I gather it has something to do with using both cold and heat together. Also a bit confused as it seemed some were using the heat and cold simultaneously(e.g. CT with some parts of the body kept warm), while others were using cold and heat sequentially(e.g. cold showers followed by warm showers)

Would also love to hear how you all utilise the Fournier Effect as well.

Thanks :3

### prAna303 — 2014-08-27T08:17:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-exactly-is-the-fournier-effect.11598/post-139007>

Go search for: photothermal deflection spectroscopy

Also read this book, have many great details for us humans...
[http://books.google.se/books?id=eVt...=0CFIQ6AEwBw#v=snippet&q=photothermal&f=false](http://books.google.se/books?id=eVtPPpvJDOUC&pg=PA111&lpg=PA111&dq=fournier+semiconductors&source=bl&ots=q6D9kP5laU&sig=ZnreAezwaD29AkS2asaqusMVqSM&hl=sv&sa=X&ei=7mf9U4SoDInUaqO6gtAM&ved=0CFIQ6AEwBw#v=snippet&q=photothermal&f=false)

[http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photothermal\_spectroscopy](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Photothermal_spectroscopy)

### yewwei.tan — 2014-08-27T09:57:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-exactly-is-the-fournier-effect.11598/post-139020>

First off, basic principles:

-   infrared heat from warm treatment of a region expands the EZ of water in the cells that are heated
-   cooling water increases the EZ of the water, as well as increases electron flow in that water (more O2)

Obviously you can't both heat and cool a single cell at the same time. But you can do one after the other, which would theoretically increase energy in the cell via 2 different mechanisms, with the net effect being more positive.

A key point is that energy from the cold can be "transmitted along the body's topological insulator network" to other regions of the body that aren't actually being cooled, while energy from heat is restricted to the EZ expansion of water in the regions that are heated.

By that logic, you should be applying warmth to the region that you want maximal increase in energy, since it's getting the EZ increase from the heat, while simultaneously having higher energy water flow to it from the regions of the body that are being cooled. Hot+Cold can thus be said to be superior to Hot-then-Cold for this reason.

Example: for PCOS, Dr Kruse has recommended putting the lower body in warm water and then wearing an ice vest, thereby helping the ovaries with increased energetic flux.

Sidenote: the ultimate fournier effect therapy would probably be full immersion in cold water, while being bombarded from all directions by red light lasers which expand cell water EZ a lot without much change in temperature at all (less than 1 degree Celsius change according to Pollack). Add a Schumann resonator for even better effect

### Sammy — 2014-08-28T12:28:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-exactly-is-the-fournier-effect.11598/post-139134>

Thanks for the explanation Yew Wei, I was experimenting having a warm shower after CT. Not sure if it did anything though. I don't have the facilities to do hot+cold simultaneously so I can't really compare.

PrAna(lovely name by the way), I read the link but I can't say I understand

You are correct. Bear in mind though that the post you are reading is from 2014. We have come a long way since then.

A quote : " Colder temperatures increase the strength of a magnetic field. Why? Cold can have an effect on magnetism, because cold can have an effect of how fast or slow electrons move. This links the sun’s power to the magnetic field of the Earth. It also links the **_Schumann resonance_** strength to the sun. When the sun is shining the Schumann frequency is strongest. When the sun is shining the Earth’s magnetic field is weakest. This implies that the Schumann resonance strength varies inversely with the magnetic field’s strength. _**At night time,**_ **_the magnetic field is stronger on Earth._ So when you use a fake generator better be well thought out."**

### Lahelada — 2018-10-12T15:27:20+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/what-exactly-is-the-fournier-effect.11598/post-252961>

> [axel brandt said:](/goto/post?id=252731)
>
> . That means that grounding is better done at night?

Yes but no.Light and dark initiate different programs in our cells. There is no choice. Both "programs" are necessary for optimal function.
Basically you are missing sunlight as the cathode ray in your thinking. Earth is the anode. Any anode hit by a cathode ray causes electrons to be liberated.This free electron flow ceases at night.

That is presuming that you are thinking about grounding for catching electrons from above. But **y**ou are meant to do other things at night.
Grounded sleeping has benefits,though. Your sleep is "deeper" as one would say in common parlance. We would have slept on the ground in an increased magnetic field "listening" to the Schuhmann frequency . The Schuhmann frequency exists in every neural system.Alpha waves take over our "timing" while the SCN is uncoupled from photic input. The geomagnetic pulsations of the Earth are phase matched to the human brain and shared to the brain wirelessly. While we sleep the direct current in neurons changes signal direction compared to when we are awake.The DC current in the interfascial water layer changes it polarity. Magnetic fields however still re energise our protein polymers. Melatonin is necessary for this. Absence of light/absence of new electron input is necessary for melatonin to be secreted. Melatonin offsets the effect daylight had on us. Good sleep maintains myelin,which regenerates tissues in autophagy. etc. If nnemf are present little or none of this happens. "Quantum sleep" blog is a way better explanation though than what I have just written.

Side note: If ever I cannot sleep a quick stroll across a lawn,dragging my bare feet makes me sleepy, providing I am not accidentally hit by light on my way back to my bed.

### kcehealth — 2014-08-29T03:06:26+0300

### caroline — 2014-08-29T08:07:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ken-from-vancouver-canada.11607/post-139218>

Your trip sounds awesome .... please tell us more.......

How are you managing with food? I come home from Asia a total disaster.....

I keep thinking India ..... but how would I survive that.........

How many cans of sardines can a person pack??????

### kcehealth — 2014-08-29T09:03:19+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ken-from-vancouver-canada.11607/post-139221>

It was very challenging so we changed our tactic a bit so we could use the markets. So we used Bangkok as our hub for much of Thailand and into Cambodia, Loas and Burma. The big challenge was "no rice" cuz everything comes with rice. And I won't pull any punches... There were times (cuz we didn't know the good markets upon arrival in a new city) we had to partake in KFC since they have HACCP programs (food safety). So out goes the bun and I would eat the grilled breast. We always made sure we had lots of coconut oil. I brought a backwoods camp stove that uses butane or propane so we could cook up bacon (doesn't need refrigeration for a few days). From time to time we ran into a fellow doing roadside BBQ. After using up the meat I'd use the stove again to make broth including some market veg like onion and Choy.

Market buying is critical. The hostel or pension-house will often help you get to the good local markets. That's critical. This caused me to change my eating a bit. Only meat and fat until the evening. Veg sources can be of concern so washing with ACV or even the vinegars from fermented (which is everywhere)

We never got to India. Since it's a vegetarian base but other backpackers say you can find chickens. We were trying to find a way thru Burma but they just opened their borders since 1966 so we just couldn't find buses. Trains in SE Asia and India are definately the way to go. Best resource is seat61.com

At the backpack stores you can find alkalizer that fits into a store bought water bottle.

Also, pickup a cheap phone so you can swap out simcards as you get to each location. We have iPad mini plus a solar charger cuz the power is very very bad in Asia. Plus we have two mini battery packs to recharge devices.

We went super lightweight. 2shirts - lightweight fast drying. 1pants 1shorts. 3socks. 3underwear. 500ml of coconut oil (unless flying). Seasalt.

PS we did find "things" we really wanted. When it got to much in the bag... UPS back home. Better to pay some bucks verses carrying it around for a year.

Oh ya. The seasalt sounds weird but we stopped bathing with soap and bathe with seasalt (I have a small tester from a pet store to make sure it's balanced properly). Seasalt helps keep the skin bacteria in balance. Freshwater rinse after dinner. It also allowed us to use the same salt on food or in it. We found the salt was a major issue during long walking locations plus the heat.

I could go on and on but I'll stop now.

### kcehealth — 2014-08-29T09:06:22+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/ken-from-vancouver-canada.11607/post-139222>

If you're going to do it our way. You Tube was super to find how to get from place to place.

We had almost no success with voice translation apps due to accents. One Malaysian lady downloaded an app she knew worked but it spoke back to me in a very strong Australian accent.
