# Thread: evaluating epigenetic risks for my surprise pregnancy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Kids
**Posts:** 8
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### kovita — 2014-09-10T09:12:39+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140498>

i am posting here my story to hear your opinions. It is a delicate topic, but i am pragmatic in this sense and expect no less from people of this exceptional community. i got unexpectedly pregnant and have to decide about it. My story in short. Chronic lyme disease with various health issues for the last two years. Epipaleo for over a year, CT since last winter, very leptin sensitive, keeping good circadian rhytm practice, both health and labs improving over the last few months. Main complaints would be joint paints (had paresthesias and muscle aches in the past). Main blood findings Th1 shift from March 2014 without evidence of autoimmunity (had occasionally elevated autoimmune markers in the past, not any more, the labs show elevated cytotoxic functions but no markers for this cells being oriented towards my own cells). Last labs from 8/2014 improved in all markers except of higher MCV (despite of good vit.bs levels and descending homocysteine) and still suboptimal magnesiun stores (16% below, was 26% below in 3/2014). intestinal immunity in the lower normal range, lower than optimal lactobacillus population. DHEAs, vit.D, creat-bun ration excellent, hsCRP lower than 0,01. LTT for borrelia was 17 in 3/2014 as a treatment i was doing hormonal support including 15mg pregnenolone and 50mg progesterone and intravenous oxygen therapies. I think it made a difference.
I sure harbour high epigenetic risks for the embryo/fetus but the same time i have done and continue doing a lot to go against it. What would be your thoughts for me to consider while taking a decision?

### Inger — 2014-09-10T09:58:08+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140501>

I think.... because you have overcome so many health issues... your baby might gain something very valuable from it!
When you do all the good stuff all the pregnancy and beyond, I am sure it will all be an amazing experience!
Imagine... you can now make the oyster baby i was dreaming of    what a fortunate woman you are Kovita

### caroline — 2014-09-10T10:05:59+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140502>

I am sure not smart enough to weigh in on this .....but I do think our life takes extraordinary twists and turns - who are we to say what the universe has in store for us...what magic is transpiring ...

All I can do is think of you with a huge amount of love - and put my arms around you....

Think, ponder, talk, listen .....listen to Mother nature whispering to you ...I don't believe she will steer you in the wrong direction.

I can look back at my life and see all the necessary steps to where I am now....I am in complete awe..... xoxoxo

My life has been extraordinary - in spite of all the crap ....and forces way larger than me - led me here....

### Sue-UK — 2014-09-10T15:39:57+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140529>

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=140498)
>
> What would be your thoughts for me to consider while taking a decision?

Hi Kovita

Because of my own previous experience, if it were me I might consider if my thought process might be different if I didn't have lab tests to think about, just observation of my current/improving health .....

I would also think about the possibility that my body selected that particular egg out of millions available because it was most suited to my current environmental signals/health ....

Sue

### nicld — 2014-09-10T16:09:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140530>

I do not envy your situation and truly do understand the internal conflict you are going through. My hubby and I do have a significantly disabled 16 year old daughter and from what I have learned here I am sure that my lifestyle and circadian issues played a factor in that.

Knowing all that we have/are going through with her, I do not think we would change our decision. It has not been easy and some significant sacrifices have been made with our lives but then having a child is does change your life. We figured that God gave her to us for a reason and she will forever be our little angel. She has been way easier to raise then our first daughter. No boy issues, no friend issues, no body image issues, no driving, it has been great.

With that being said, I think that you are in a great place to know what you need to do to be as optimal as you can but only you can make that decision if you are in a right place.

Keeping you in my thoughts.

### Hope — 2014-09-11T17:54:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/evaluating-epigenetic-risks-for-my-surprise-pregnancy.11690/post-140647>

Hi Kovita, I am not qualified to give any input. I do want to congratulate you. Clearly your body wanted to create this baby or you wouldn't have become pregnant. My TCM practitioner would say, the spirit of that life decided to come through you. I think that is awesome though what I think doesn't matter.

I do want to encourage you to trust your body....whatever happens. And while you carry your baby, send it lots of love.....  love changes everything

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-12T01:34:05+0300

I wouldn't want to bet my life that those didn't have some kind of heavy metals bound up with the salt. Since it is not a food, I doubt they test for it. Where was the salt obtained, and how pure is it?

A biochemist I hired made me aware of this issue for chemicals in general, and his thing is to always order pharmaceutical grades when they are available. Food grade doesn't even guarantee you that there are not heavy metals and contaminants (arsenic in rice is at very toxic levels these days). This item isn't even food grade, so it's not wild speculation or worry.

# Thread: Fixing Aerobic Metabolism of Fats
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fixing-aerobic-metabolism-of-fats.11694/>
**Subforum:** Optimal Labs
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### persistence — 2014-09-11T06:49:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fixing-aerobic-metabolism-of-fats.11694/post-140624>

Based on Metametrix ION Panel, and based on other symptoms I have, I apparently do not metabolize fats efficiently for aerobic metabolism. The ION panel has several markers for carnitine deficiency that suggest I am very low on carnitine. My adipate measures at 25.0 mcg/mg creatinine where the 95% interval tops out at 11.1. My suberate measures 6.4 mcg/mg creatinine where the 95% interval tops out at 4.6. High values on those two metabolites suggest carnitine deficiency.

I tried to address this by Acetyl-L-Carnitine, 500 mg, three or four times a day. This does not appear to be substantially improving my ability to metabolize fats. On a high fat diet I am basically stuck in glycolysis and get heavy acid accumulation on low amounts of exercise (even very mild walking). Is my dose appropriate?

How does one "debug" aerobic metabolism of fats? Is there any clinical path I can follow to get more information on what specifically is broken? I would really like to get this working.

### Josh — 2014-10-18T03:14:36+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/tensegrity-4-comments.11698/post-144144>

> The effect of magnetic field on the dynamic of microspheres movement
> around a Nafion tube in Water
> Alireza A. Moayed1
> , Prof. Gerald H. Pollack1
> 1University of Washington, Seattle, US
> Abstract:
> It has been observed that the exclusion zone (EZ) around a Nafion tube or sheet, locally extends
> in length in the shape of fingers. In the experiments that have been conducted to study the effect
> of magnetic fields (MF) on EZ, it was observed that this finger orient itself toward the MF source.
> To study this phenomenon, a thin cross section of Nafion tube was placed at the bottom of a petri
> dish and filled with 1 micron microspheres and deionized water mixture. The petri dish then is
> sealed to prevent evaporation. After 30 minutes, usually a finger forms. Then a permanent magnet
> or an AC magnet is placed close to the petri dish. After 10 minutes exposure to MF, the finger
> orients itself toward the MF source location. Removal of the MF source, causes the finger changes
> its direction. It has been hypothesize that the appearance of fingers without magnetic field in the
> beginning of the experiment is related to Rayleigh–Bénard convection cells. Movement of
> microspheres inside the water is determined by Rayleigh–Bénard convection currents. When they
> move around EZ of the Nafion tube, they drag EZ with them and form a finger like shape. Since
> EZ has gel like properties, the shape of the finger after formation is conserved. The reorientation
> of these fingers due to AC MF could be related to two factors, the generated heat in water due to
> AC MF that creates a new convection current in water and the effect of electric field generated by
> AC MF on charged microspheres. The effect of permanent MF on the reorientation of finger is
> weaker than the effect of AC MF. However, the reorientation effect is still present. The effect of
> permanent magnet on finger could be related to several factors, the effect of MF on movement of
> charged microspheres, and the effect of MF on water which magnetizes the water and changes
> physical properties of water. Since the magnetization of water close to MF source is stronger than
> further points, it could generate a gradient of some force to be determined. In conclusion, it has
> been shown that AC and permanent MF can affect the movement of microspheres around EZ. The
> importance of this finding could be resulted in better water waste filtration, and better
> understanding of the effect of magnetic field on water and on biological systems.

[http://www.waterconf.org/participants-materials/posters/](http://www.waterconf.org/participants-materials/posters/)

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/hormones-linked-to-depression-and-anxiety.11699/post-140659>

Hi All, I have just started reading Dr.K's book. He states that hormonal depletion is linked to things such as depression and OCD, that after 30 years of age hormones naturally decline especially on a SAD. He believes it is not genetic. My question is if this is correct, how would a child have anxiety, depression. Could it be that a child can have decreased hormones? If not genetics, then does the mothers state of hormones possibly determine the child's?
Thank you for any input. I've had anxiety and depression since at least 8 years old, (as far back as I can remember) I do not feel like anxiety meds are the answer.
Cheers to good health!

### BTA — 2014-09-12T00:59:50+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-question.11700/post-140674>

So I read that Jack stated the epi-paleo diet can cause dehyration. I ran into someone today out shopping who said their spouse was following the Epi-Paleo diet to a tee but dropped over dead from a heart attack. The pathologist said it's very common to lose a ton of K+ on the diet. I understand the doc follows the blogs & might be a member but not sure. If this is the case - yikes

I know I've had to up my intake of K+ to 4 to 7 grams a day. The RDA is 3 grams but exercise for 20 mins & you lose 3 grams which means you need over 6 grams to offset it.

any thoughts or ideas ????

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-12T01:20:13+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/epi-paleo-diet-question.11700/post-140678>

Seafood provides DHA and a ton of K. Its not the diet. Jack does not recall saying the Epi-paleo diet cause dehydration. Any seafood diet shows longevity so the pathologist you spoke with does not seem in tune with the literature on seafood diets. Chronic potassium deficiency in the United States is rare because potassium is found in so many different types of food, including fruits, vegetables, legumes, meats and seafood. Many types of seafood are good to excellent sources of potassium, especially shellfish. For example, a 3.5-ounce portion of steamed scallops contains about 480 milligrams of potassium. Shrimp, lobster, crab and mussels all contain between about 260 and 280 milligrams of potassium per 3.5-ounce serving. Another good source of potassium is steamed or baked clams.Deep-sea fish are also good sources of potassium. A 3.5-ounce portion of baked flounder contains about 585 milligrams of potassium, and the same portion of halibut contains about 470 milligrams. Other good sources include Pacific rockfish, Pacific cod, sardines, haddock, tuna and farmed salmon and rainbow trout. In addition to potassium, most fish are rich sources of DHA, which are considered healthy types due to their anti-inflammatory properties that reduce heart disease.

# Thread: Is there a way to measure the burning of stored body fat in isolation? (Lipolysis)
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-way-to-measure-the-burning-of-stored-body-fat-in-isolation-lipolysis.11702/>
**Subforum:** Cold Thermogenesis
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ColinGorham — 2014-09-12T07:56:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/is-there-a-way-to-measure-the-burning-of-stored-body-fat-in-isolation-lipolysis.11702/post-140712>

Question: Is there a way to measure the burning of stored body fat in isolation? (Lipolysis) In other words, how can you differentiate between fatty acids emptying out of long term storage within the fat cell vs the burning of recently ingested fatty acids that have not yet been stored within the fat cell?

I currently use a Breath Ketone Analyzer called the Ketonix Sport to measure Acetone as a Ketosis/ Lipolysis proxy. A measure of Ketosis, whether, Blood beta hydroxybutyrate, Urine Ketone Bodies, or Acetone in the Breadth just means the body has toggled from glucose/ glycogen to fat (both ingested & stored) as a primary fuel of metabolism. My self-experimentation, for instance, has shown me that after 1) CT, & 2) Vigorous or Prolonged exercise sessions ketone levels fall. (likely cause the body is burning rather than excreting ketones) I often wonder if we should be measuring something in addition to ketones. It would be great if we could measure other markers instantly. (For instance: RQ (Respiratory Quotient), Insulin, Glucagon, Insulin/ Hormone Sensitive Lipase, Cortisol, Adiponectin, or some other marker or proxy for the burning of stored body fat. The terms beta oxidation and lipolysis come to mind.)

# Thread: Examples of Scaling the Microcosm to the Macrocosm
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/examples-of-scaling-the-microcosm-to-the-macrocosm.11703/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### PaulG — 2014-09-12T13:07:25+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/examples-of-scaling-the-microcosm-to-the-macrocosm.11703/post-140724>

Can we scale the [evolution of the cell](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Evolution_of_cells) to the [evolution of the universe](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Chronology_of_the_universe)?

In archaeology there are [transitional fossils](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transitional_fossil) (A **transitional fossil** is any [fossilized](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Fossil) remains of a life form that exhibits traits common to both an ancestral group and its derived descendant group.[\[1\]](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Transitional_fossil#cite_note-Herron-1) ) that lends considerable evidence to Darwin's Theory of Evolution. Of course, fossilized cells are not possible or [are they??](http://www.ox.ac.uk/media/news_stories/2011/110822.html) but I am wondering if we can infer support for the Ling's [Association Induction Hypothesis](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Association_induction_hypothesis) by looking for [homologies](http://evolutionwiki.org/wiki/Homology) in the cell physiology of other non-human species (plants, lower order animals. bacteria)

Nature and Evolution is very efficient at minimizing waste by utilizing patterns such as the golden mean and fractals that scale from [microcosm to macrocosm](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Macrocosm_and_microcosm). This is best summed up by the maxim of "As above, so below" of [hermeticism](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hermeticism)

If the Association Induction Hypothesis is true then similar mechanisms must be employed by nature at a macroscopic [scale](http://mabloom77.blogspot.com.au/2013/03/humans-scaling-universe.html). What are they? The cell is a cavity and JK talks about cavities in [Tensigrity 4](http://jackkruse.com/tensegrity-4-magnetism-electrons-sleep)
**

> Jack Kruse said:
>
> A water filled cavity is a great place to generate spherical standing waves which have identical wavelengths. We think of waves as moving through CSF water, but these waves stand still in water. The crest or amplitude of the wave still has the energy within this standing wave. This mode of action is how a guitar or violin make sounds from the standing waves on their strings above their cavity of air in the body of the instrument. Your brain works on the same physical properties. It is time for you to realize it.

> Jack Kruse said:
>
> Life was quite smart tapping the potential of water and the Earth’s magnetic field for electrons in this fashion. This points out why the hydrogen bonding network of water is critical in understanding magnetism. Since water is [naturally paramagnetic](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=GDR3gS5ddh4), it changes its hydrogen bonding network based upon the magnetic field it is found within. This means that our mitochondria alone, can alter the water chemistry around themselves, because mitochondria make their own magnetic fields when there ATPase rotates in the innermitochondrial membrane. These membranes also have the largest electric fields in membranes [as I laid out here](http://jackkruse.com/ee-6-quantum-cell-theory-life-collective-phenomena/). This is due to the action of electric currents and the reduction of oxygen at its terminal step of electron chain transport. Nothing in the universe has the ability to transfer energy as well as water does or this easily to create complexity by increasing electron density within its structure. This helps explain why water makes up 71 percent of the earth surface and most of our cell’s insides as well. Coincidence? Doubtful.

See also**
[**Obcells as Proto-Organisms: Membrane Heredity, Lithophosphorylation, and**
**the Origins of the Genetic Code, the First Cells, and Photosynthesis**](http://www2.bgsu.edu/departments/chem/faculty/leontis/chem447/PDF_files/Cavalier_Smith_2001.pdf)

**[The origin of cells: a symbiosis between genes, catalysts, and membranes](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pubmed/3454290).**

**[Why Are Cells Powered by Proton Gradients?](http://www.nature.com/scitable/topicpage/why-are-cells-powered-by-proton-gradients-14373960)**

# Thread: The "Off Call" Midwife….surfing, walking barefoot, living life instead
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/>
**Subforum:** My Optimal Journal
**Posts:** 23
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Greenisland — 2014-09-12T18:26:46+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/post-140749>

Hey everyone. I have been poking around for a couple of months so here I am. 11 months ago I went to the doc for my physical and got flagged for metabolic syndrome. What a shocker for a former pro snowboarder and Division I athlete. This was after 5 years of attending lots of births, getting a degree, homeschooling, having a teenager in the house. I was lethargic, burned out, not sleeping more than 4 hours a night and carrying 30 plus pounds. I made some changes, lost 20 pounds and had been battling the last ten when I found Kruse and Co. I was counting calories and macros when I first got here, now I am epi-paleo, doing CT sporadically and aspiring to go ketogenic. I won't be attending births until April at least. I have figured out I am highly insulin resistant, but my docs won't give me metformin because I'm normal weight, my A1C is 5.5 and I'm very fit. They don't care my fasting blood sugar is 95-105 or that it jumps up to 170 if I have a modest carby meal. So in two weeks I will travel 400 miles to go to an functional medicine NP. Will post my labs when I get them all done.

Today:
BAB: Large pile of Wellness Meats canadian bacon ends with butter and steamed onions. Bulletproof coffee.

### Greenisland — 2014-09-15T01:45:01+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/post-141007>

Yesterday did a high intensity workout in the morning. Played on the beach with the puppy in the afternoon. Ate seaweed off the beach. But did not get into to the ocean. Stayed up late with a houseguest and ate crappy. Grrrr. I have to stick to my lifestyle despite living with a person that eats late, eats tons of grains, is attached to the phone.

New plan: Feed the chickens a soy and corn free diet (adding salmon to their diet) and see if I can tolerate the eggs better. I feel sick if I eat more than 2 a day.

Today so far:
Up with the sun, walked barefoot in the rain at sunrise.
BAB: Smoked sablefish. 1 raw egg. Bulletproof coffee.
(Not big ass enough. Was hungry by 1. Sablefish (black cod) is super fatty and it's hard to eat enough of it to get the protein required. Might have to mix it with halibut tomorrow. Will pull out a steak tonight.
L: Nuts, pork, 1 avocado, cucumber from the garden.

PM Plans:

Surfing or Yoga class this afternoon...
Kids in bed by 9
CT 9-9:30 if I don't surf
Bed at 10
Sleep like the dead.

a) surf, cold water swim, strength workout or HIIT 2-3 times a week
b) yoga or pilates 1-2 times a week.

This afternoon I plan to surf, walk on the sand barefoot, eat seaweed.

Accomplishment of the day so far: I turned off the WIFI.

BAB: large T-bone steak from seaweed and sea grass fed beef. We get our meat from a herd of wild cattle planted here in the 1930s on an uninhabited island near us-Sitkinak Island, and harvested with great care. Lovely pics on their website.

### Greenisland — 2014-09-18T22:00:47+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/post-141355>

Yes, I did! It was great. I agree. It's all about the circadian rhythms. Fortunately, my circadian rhythms are humming. I have been falling dead asleep at 9:30-10 and wake up without an alarm right before sunrise for the last month or so. Not eating after 5 PM is key for me. I get up and take the puppy out to potty in the morning and walk barefoot on the wet grass watch the sun come up. Beach day every 3 days and get a full day of electrons. Today I'm going to try to do some ocean CT before Bikram yoga. The heat nearly suffocates me so I am going to try to get my hypothermia on before I go in. The most uncomfortable part of CT for me is the rewarming so this might work great… or it might kill me…  One or the other. If I don't log in again you will know I'm dead and that it was a bad idea…

BAB: 1 dozen fresh oysters from a farm just a couple hundred miles away. Score!!! Our local fishmonger says he will now be getting WEEKLY shipments. Yay! They taste soooo much better than the jarred variety. Also drank two cups of bulletproof with an egg from my backyard chickens, cinnamon, Kerrygold butter plus collagen protein.

Eating oysters is a workout. Let me tell you. It's like the pistachio effect. It took me an hour to shuck my dozen oysters for breakfast. Sheesh! I know I'll get better at it. Maybe I'll check out a YouTube video on it. Any tips?

They taste soooo much better than the jarred variety. Also drank two cups of bulletproof with an egg from my backyard chickens, cinnamon, Kerrygold butter plus collagen protein.
>
> Eating oysters is a workout. Let me tell you. It's like the pistachio effect. It took me an hour to shuck my dozen oysters for breakfast. Sheesh! I know I'll get better at it. Maybe I'll check out a YouTube video on it. Any tips?

Oysters:
Find the spot to place the Oyster Knife. It has a dull point and protective shield for your hand. It is essential imho. That spot is where you can see the two shells merge. Rotate the knife back and forth (ie clockwise and counter clockwise) Short little motions. at the same time press the knife in. All of a sudden sometimes, the knife slides in. Rotate big and shell pops open. I like a leather glove for hand that holds the oyster... Be gentle after knife goes in or you will bust pieces of shell into oyster meat. Maybe 15 or 20 seconds per oyster.
Hope this helps you!
Brother John

### Greenisland — 2014-09-20T07:44:00+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/post-141443>

OK. Ill get the tool. But it's hard to see where the the two shells merge. Will try some different tools from the workshop in the meantime.

YES hot yoga is a bit too much. I did 15 min of CT before the class and I was the only one to complete all 26 poses and lasted the 90 minutes in the 105 degree high humidity room. Out of 11 only 2 of us made it to the final 4 and i was the last man standing. Everyone else was a lump of sweat on their mats. I think if I did 45-an hour of CT before the class I could breeze through it. But today I felt like I had gone out drinking the night before. The yogis say that its detoxing as the high heat and inverted poses flush out the toxins in the liver and lymphatic system. But it makes my rosacea flare. And I ate all day long refeeding from it. Its pretty hellish, but I do it in name of hormetism and its great for fat loss. Also I crave it on clammy long cold dark winter days.

Caroline you are so sweet. Yes, I probobly should do a leptin reset.

Going to see the obgyn on monday to talk hormones and an internist and a functional med np the following weeko I should have a lot more info soon. Did my blood draw this morning for my fasting insulin and hs-crp.

### Greenisland — 2014-09-30T01:01:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/the-off-call-midwife%E2%80%A6-surfing-walking-barefoot-living-life-instead.11705/post-142198>

I saw the Functional Med. NP. Wonderful experience. Spent over two hours in her office. She put me on 65mg Westhroid and testosterone cream. Will add progesterone cream as needed, depending on my P/E ratio when it comes in. She also thinks I have yeast overgrowth which causes my chemical sensitivities/food allergies and blood sugar swings. Put me on an anti fungal for a month and a amylose and sugar free diet. Not a big change from but now I've cut out my veering off the epi-paleo. I don't feel like I can nab a french fry from my kids plate or eat the end off the banana before I feed them anymore. I already feel much more clear headed. Today I cut my coffee intake down by 3/4. Today is my first day on the full dose. Hopefully I will start sweating soon. And growing hair. My legs are nearly bare with no shaving. It takes me a 20 minutes in a 105 degree room to start sweating.

### Martin — 2014-09-15T17:46:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-ks-webinar.11707/post-141076>

BTA, what's your environment like? Moldy structures you live or work in? I'm assuming by your language you've been reading up on Shoe's work.

Here's my take: if one is still in an inflammatory environment, then many protocols will not work as well (if at all!) if constantly exposed. I think you probably know this from your listing labs, etc. Strive to get out of the exposure all while doing things to increase one's redox potential. Eat DHA like a great white, get your CT on to improve mito voltages, raise aMSH,and turn over the fat cell's stores of biotoxins, etc., all while improving one's hydration to grease the cellular wheels. Avoid nnEMF's, which includes artificial light.

Some patients can pass it due to being visually tuned into the world. Many artists and such can pass it and still have TGF-b1, C4a, etc., high, while non-visual people _generally_ don't pass. I think this is due to how visual people have used their vision throughout their lives and epigenetic changes have strengthened this ability. This is the bell-curve in action. Some of us are outliers to that system of taking the middle 68% of the curve and wanting to treat everyone the same damn way.

What is your product?

NSH

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-16T00:33:15+0300

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=141097)

> Raising alpha MSH is easy.........cold and one herb.......its listed in Louise journal.

For those wondering about this, the post referenced is here -- [http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/louises-journal.11608/page-2#post-139704](http://forum.jackkruse.com/index.php?threads/louises-journal.11608/page-2#post-139704) . The herb in question is Mitragyna Speciosa (or kratom) -- [http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitragyna\_speciosa](http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mitragyna_speciosa)

Jane Plain on her 'ItsTheWoo' blog wrote about this quite a bit in the recent past. The 3 most informative blogs from that series are probably:

-   [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/kratom-naturally-occuring-msh-agonist.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/kratom-naturally-occuring-msh-agonist.html)

-   [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/mirtragynine-in-kratom-is-absolutely.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/mirtragynine-in-kratom-is-absolutely.html)

-   [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html)

3 random thoughts from me:

-   Dr K has talked about CT being particularly ineffective for people on Beta Blockers. While you shouldn't be on beta blockers to begin, I suspect kratom will work even for such people
-   Kratom seems to be a calcium-channel blocker. Wonder what the implications are
-   It's not illegal in the US (yet). It is illegal to possess in Australia

### yewwei.tan — 2014-09-16T01:56:24+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/dr-ks-webinar.11707/post-141108>

> [BTA said:](/goto/post?id=141104)
>
> Thanks - interesting stuff. Never heard of it. I hope it raises MSH levels. I have no clue what form to take? Powder, liquid ect... or how much?
>
> I would hope Dr K could expand on how the ancient pathway plays a role in this?
>
> Does low MSH levels related to drug addiction/abuse?
>
> I'm in alot of pain with alot of muscle cramping & always rev'ed up feeling - can't calm down.

Well, I don't know much about the topic, but Jane Plain's experiment is definitely a good starting point to answer some of your questions.

From the post: [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html)

-   **It's better to take 1 large dose than several small doses throughout the day**. The latter is activating.

-   I am 125 pounds. For me to get a proper endorphin effect/calming/inhibiting benefit I must take **2.5-3g**

-   I am HYPER sensitive to the stimulant strains of kratom. I do not know if they are adulterated with stimulants, or if that is the effects of the plant, but they have literally the polar opposite effect as the calming ones.

Jane used different strains of Kratom -- [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/more-kratom-experiences-days-3-4-and-5.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/more-kratom-experiences-days-3-4-and-5.html) but **all the gram doses refer to powder extracts of the plant**
I believe she thinks that it's the opiates that cause the potentially addictive behaviour, but that the addictive potential is not that high to begin with assuming proper dosing.

I personally do not understand enough about low MSH and it's potential to stimulate addictive behaviour.

> Jane Plain on her 'ItsTheWoo' blog wrote about this quite a bit in the recent past. The 3 most informative blogs from that series are probably:
>

>
> -   [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/mirtragynine-in-kratom-is-absolutely.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/mirtragynine-in-kratom-is-absolutely.html)
>
> -   [http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html](http://itsthewooo.blogspot.com/2014/09/i-love-kratom-so-much.html)
>
> 3 random thoughts from me:
>
> -   Dr K has talked about CT being particularly ineffective for people on Beta Blockers. While you shouldn't be on beta blockers to begin, I suspect kratom will work even for such people
> -   Kratom seems to be a calcium-channel blocker. Wonder what the implications are
> -   It's not illegal in the US (yet). It is illegal to possess in Australia

Fuck australia and its over-regulation

# Thread: Mobile Phone Companies Facing Lawsuits
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mobile-phone-companies-facing-lawsuits.11710/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Josh (Paleo Osteo) — 2014-09-14T00:46:09+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mobile-phone-companies-facing-lawsuits.11710/post-140892>

[http://stopsmartmeters.com.au/2014/...-biggest-public-health-cover-up-of-our-times/](http://stopsmartmeters.com.au/2014/09/13/mobile-firms-face-billion-dollar-lawsuits-will-this-mark-the-end-of-the-biggest-public-health-cover-up-of-our-times/)

[This story starts in Italy in 2012 with Italy’s supreme court upholding a ruling that said there was a link between a business executive’s brain tumour and his heavy mobile phone usage. The court ruled that scientific evidence in support of the Italian man’s claim was reliable and based on research that was independent and “unlike some others, was not co-financed by the same companies that produce mobile telephones.”

Fast forward to 2014 and move to the United States, where some of the world’s largest mobile phone companies are facing billion-dollar lawsuits brought by brain tumour victims. The biggest ever legal assault on the industry will seek to prove that mobile phone handsets can cause cancer and that FIRMS MAY HAVE DELIBERATELY COVERED UP CRUCIAL MEDICAL INFORMATION.

If the American litigation is successful, mobile phone companies will face crippling bills for compensation. What will this mean for Australian mobile carriers? How will this affect the legitimacy of the Victorian power distributors’ decision to use wireless technologies for their smart meters?

As investors digest the news, billions have already been wiped off the share values of some international wireless industry giants.

The pressure is now firmly on and it may well lead to the bubble of secrecy bursting and the truth FINALLY leaking out that wireless technologies are not so safe after all. It will be interesting to watch how the wireless industry’s spin doctors attempt to down play this announcement.](http://stopsmartmeters.com.au/2014/09/13/mobile-firms-face-billion-dollar-lawsuits-will-this-mark-the-end-of-the-biggest-public-health-cover-up-of-our-times/)

### caroline — 2014-09-14T01:33:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/mobile-phone-companies-facing-lawsuits.11710/post-140900>

Thanks Josh....

the writing has been on the wall for a long time now.

So if EMF is bad, blue light is bad, bad diet is bad, circadian mismatch is bad and so on.... then how come prisoners who spend 23.5 hours a day in a small cell, never workout, very bad diet that's high in carbs, don't sleep well, high levels of EMF, high levels of blue, and so on - don't have neolithic disease?

Or firefighters who work 96 horus on and 96 hours off. or police officers. All of these folks are exposed to massive EMF, bad diet and ect.... yet no mention of thousands of firefighters or prisoner dropping over dead.

It would be interesting to conduct some research into this.

[https://www.google.com/search?q=shi...obile&sourceid=chrome-mobile&espvd=1&ie=UTF-8](https://www.google.com/search?q=shift+workers+health&oq=shift+workers&aqs=chrome.3.69i57j0l3.5326j0j4&client=chrome-mobile&sourceid=chrome-mobile&espvd=1&ie=UTF-8)

### NeilBB — 2014-09-14T06:42:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/emf-question.11711/post-140925>

It's actually an important distinction between "dropping dead" and chronic disease. We humans are very adaptive. Very resilient. It is built into our epigenetic machinery. We can "adapt" to a wide variety of chronic insults for a surprisingly long time. But not without a cost. A cost to our health, our children's health, our environment's health.

But it happens slowly, imperceptibly, so people don't make the causal connection. Our resilience is a blessing and a curse. In many ways it might be better if we did just "drop dead" instead. Things would be much simpler, the planet would be healthier, and my job would be a lot less painful.

I can't tell you how many times I've told "patient X" that his particular habit (prescription drug abuse, poor sleep habits, shitty diet, blue light, whatever) is the cause of his ill health, only to have him argue with me, saying basically, "It can't be THAT. I've been doing THAT for years. It must be something else. What is it doc?"

# Thread: How Legit is Keto Clarity by Jimmy Moore?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/>
**Subforum:** The Epi-Paleo Diet
**Posts:** 11
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Winter Mermaid — 2014-09-14T15:43:54+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140953>

The weather is getting colder and I'm getting ready to leave the re-set behind (it's been about six weeks and I've got the signs) and I'd like to "do ketosis right." On the suggestion of a member here (I apologize, I cannot remember your forum name - you are a lovely lady with curly dark hair I think?) I picked up Jimmy Moore's book, Keto Clarity.

According to his book, I should be eating only 52 grams of protein a day (1 gram for each kilogram of my ideal bodyweight, which is 58 kg, minus 10%), and between 20 and 50 grams of carbs depending on what level I can tolerate and still be in ketosis - the rest should be fat.

The main benefit I see to this plan would be with such a low protein amount for the day, I would need to switch to all-seafood to get adequate DHA from whole-food sources alone. I do think I tend to eat way too much protein for my needs, especially since this can get converted back to glucose if you overdo it on a low carb diet.

I am asking because I've heard a lot of negative things about Jimmy Moore on these forums and I'm not sure if that criticism extends to this book. I would love to hear people's opinions/experiences.

### Winter Mermaid — 2014-09-14T16:05:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140955>

I'd also like to add that I HATE needles, so I don't think I can bring myself to check blood ketones and blood glucose with a monitor several times a day, which JM suggests. He does say if I followed those macronutrient ratios it would be pretty certain I'd be in nutritional ketosis. I suppose I would force myself to use the glucose/ketone meter if it was really required, but it's not something I want to incorporate into my lifestyle unless I was really convinced it was necessary.

### Lahelada — 2014-09-14T16:32:33+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140956>

I dont know the book but dont stress about the macro nutrient ratio. Keep carbs low ( no carbs grow in winter where you live) protein regulates itself in hunger terms after a while when you eat sufficient fats. Numbers on their own lie ,be it macros ,calories or body weight calculations. I was just thinking this morning that having a scale in your bathroom is a symptom in itself. I think the same goes for ketone testers. You will know when you are in ketosis because your body odour will change,pee will smell acetone like for example,your energy levels go up and are stable. Just start,you will know.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-14T18:32:15+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140967>

Jimmy is a marketer and not a scientist. He tries to make friends with scientists and becomes a parrot for what he thinks he understands. Most of what he says is OK but he gets a lot wrong. Caveat emptor.

### Winter Mermaid — 2014-09-14T18:52:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140969>

Thanks for replies, Lahelada and Dr. K.

Counting calories is way too tedious, but I feel like counting ~50 grams of protein and ~50 grams of carbs is entirely manageable. I'll see how I feel with that ratio and adjust accordingly. I know it's not just about the food.

One thing I notice about higher fat and lower protein (so far) is that about an hour after a meal I feel a spontaneous surge of energy that makes me want to get up and move- dance, walk, bustle around doing household chores - and I think I'll calibrate my "exercise" to these surges. They never happen first thing in the morning, only after a lot of DHA and other fats. Duck fat has been my friend lately!

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-14T19:04:27+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140971>

Duck fat is one of my go to fats in autumn and winter.

### Winter Mermaid — 2014-09-14T19:16:52+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140973>

> [Jack Kruse said:](/goto/post?id=140971)
>
> Duck fat is one of my go to fats in autumn and winter.

I have become sensitive to the differences in different fats - a large serving of oily fish (DHA) creates a libidinal surge within 30 minutes of eating. But duck fat is the only non-DHA fat that has made this noticeable of a difference: I literally want to jump up and down doing stuff. Almost like my cells can access the energy from it faster. Butter and lard feel "slower" and more heavy. I can't prove this scientifically, but there's my N = 1.

This morning I had onions, garlic and mushrooms sauteed in lots of duck fat (plus a raw tuna steak). So delicious.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-14T19:41:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-140978>

WM when you understand QED.........RCT dont matter as much as N=1. Radical but true...........and I am going to show that in due time.

### Danco3636 — 2014-09-16T18:48:53+0300

### ColinGorham — 2014-09-27T09:17:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-legit-is-keto-clarity-by-jimmy-moore.11712/post-142018>

I read Keto Clarity and I would highly recommend it. Jimmy Moore has done nearly a 1000 interviews concerning low carb diets over the years. Keto Clarity deals with a ketogenic diets specifically and he gets the perspectives of scientists, physicians, and others with significant experience with ketogenic diets. Jimmy is nice guy and his book has considerable value.

### kovita — 2014-09-14T17:45:49+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/sometimes-the-best-biohack-is-taking-a-break.11713/post-140960>

great blog, Gretchen. I am sure than happiness is far more important than ketones in your urine. To me it looks like that some (if not many) women do not do well going long periods of time ketotic. I think ther is time for everything and your hormones would be much happier with some carbs around. I am not big paleo fun, because i do not believe in food as an absolute cure but more in food as a part of your overall approach. if i think about the real paleo women than The first thing that comes to my mind as that women and children spent most of the days collecting plants, fruits and roots. I red somewhere lately that in that times it is estimated that more than 400 edible plants were availabe for human consumption in the old world . Imagine the variety, 400! would the lack of variety in today's diet be as well contributing to our health issues mainly leaky gut? I am sure there were seasons wher carbs could be very important part of diet of women and children. This does not mean we can just do the same today with the same results. i firmly believe in controlling carefully carbs, but than again, it looks like there is time for it in one's life as well.

### Shijin13 — 2014-09-14T18:01:12+0300

> [kovita said:](/goto/post?id=140960)
>
> great blog, Gretchen. I am sure than happiness is far more important than ketones in your urine. To me it looks like that some (if not many) women do not do well going long periods of time ketotic. I think ther is time for everything and your hormones would be much happier with some carbs around. I am not big paleo fun, because i do not believe in food as an absolute cure but more in food as a part of your overall approach. if i think about the real paleo women than The first thing that comes to my mind as that women and children spent most of the days collecting plants, fruits and roots. I red somewhere lately that in that times it is estimated that more than 400 edible plants were availabe for human consumption in the old world . Imagine the variety, 400! would the lack of variety in today's diet be as well contributing to our health issues mainly leaky gut? I am sure there were seasons wher carbs could be very important part of diet of women and children. This does not mean we can just do the same today with the same results. i firmly believe in controlling carefully carbs, but than again, it looks like there is time for it in one's life as well.

I too am discovering that my own health journey doesn't mirror what others are going through. this morning I mixed things up and had a beautiful acorn squash with some sausage for breakfast - and I feel wonderful... sometimes we've got to change things up... these changes don't mean I've tossed out what Jack is teaching us. I'm trying to figure out how to dial everything in for me. B/c my N=1 is different from everyone else's

I think your success in what you've done health wise has lead you to where you're at in your journey... as has mine... thanks for the comments

# Thread: Short video of bacteria, biofilms & quorum sensing
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-video-of-bacteria-biofilms-quorum-sensing.11714/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Jonathin — 2014-09-14T17:39:05+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-video-of-bacteria-biofilms-quorum-sensing.11714/post-140959>

This animation illustrates how bacteria form a biofilm. A biofilm - more commonly know as "slime" - is a surface-attached community of microorganisms. Formation of a biofilm involves a series of bacterial processes, including bacterial signalling and quorum sensing, production of pili and secretion of an extracellular polymer matrix.

### Martha Ray — 2019-11-08T03:00:46+0200
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/short-video-of-bacteria-biofilms-quorum-sensing.11714/post-281994>

I have studied Candida in the past to no conclusions. I tried a candida diet for a short while but it was so hard I was unable to stick with it. I took a candida product called _Candex_ for a good long while and felt that it helped me but with time I lost interest in fighting candida.

I was just re listened to some videos by Dr. Thomas Janossy one from a Summit I listened to and another presentation he did on _You Tube_ titled, _Our Toxic World_. A little hard to follow due to his accent. I think he is Hungarian but he is from Canada, seems to be known for his detox protocol especially in Australia and with those using detoxing to treat Autism.
So not sure how Fluoride, aluminum, mercury and other heavy metals mesh with Optimal Health.
Since he mentioned Biofilms ....I googled Biofilms Jack Kruse and this post came up.
Dr. Janossy mentioned Biofilms and that reminded me of a product by _Food Research_ a 100% food product called _**Biofilm Detox.**_ It looks effective for parasites as well. [https://www.buyfoodresearch.com/products/biofilm-detox?\_pos=1&\_sid=34a3ab91f&\_ss=r](https://www.buyfoodresearch.com/products/biofilm-detox?_pos=1&_sid=34a3ab91f&_ss=r) I have studied parasites and heavy metals as well to no conclusions ie no idea if these are an issue for me and no idea how to use a protocol for them.
I am not in favor of synthetic supplements but am interested in herbs and food vs drugs for medicine. Not competing with what Light, Water and Magnetism accomplish but to support the body against toxins while not getting in the way of what the body does best. I am a newbie to the work of Dr. Jack Kruse and this OHF and still finding my way meshing what I am learning here with what I have wondered about before....

Three issues I have never specifically addressed with my health are possible issues with parasites, heavy metals and candida. I am sensitive to some foods, most all drugs and chemicals and EMF. Detoxing has not been a priority for me.

How to test to find out if Biofilm busting and Detox are needful ? And then, if needful, how to choose a protocol to treat?

# Thread: Fuchs' dystrophy
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fuchs-dystrophy.11716/>
**Subforum:** The Cave
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Marie — 2014-09-15T04:04:58+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fuchs-dystrophy.11716/post-141021>

(I'm not sure where to post this, please move if it is in the wrong area.)

I'm interested in how the eyes may indicate (inflammatory) issues in the brain and liver. My understanding is that Fuch's Dystrophy is relatively rare, currently. But, given metabolic syndrome and an increase in inflammation, shouldn't we be seeing a dramatic increase of these sorts of eye problems, specifically due to blue light toxicity and computer use in youth?

If so, is mitigating blue light only part of the issue and liver toxicity and brain inflammation need to be addressed as well?

Or is this such a specifically genetic issue, one would have to address specific pathways?

(I have a student in her late 20s who has been diagnosed. She shows all the markers for long term inflammation and potentially some genetic markers. I'd hate for her to be blind in 10 years!)

Marie

### Marie — 2014-09-15T05:54:37+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/fuchs-dystrophy.11716/post-141037>

Never mind.

It looks like blue blockers, glutathione, B-Complex, and saline drops are the way to go...

It does sound like a sign of the times...

It is very frustrating to look out into a sea of students and see how they are already falling apart.

Marie

**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ldl-optimal-mens-progesterone-level.11717/post-141039>

LDL: 8.3mmol/L (range .7)
HDL: 2.3mmol/L (range 0.9-2)
Progesterone: 4nmol/L (no range)
DHEA-S: 13.3umol/L (range 2.5-13)
Serum cortisiol: 586nmol/L at 11:30 (range for 9:00 200-700 nmol/L)
Ferritin: 371ug/L (range 20-300)
BUN/creatinine: 24
Since adopting a paleo diet in the last year my LDL has skyrocketed, but my HDL has simultaneously doubled. What is optimal progesterone level for men (im 20 years old), cant find a consistent range looking through the threads? If progesterone is low then i might have pregnenolone steal. Any comments appreciated.

### nonchalant — 2014-09-17T17:02:28+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/high-ldl-optimal-mens-progesterone-level.11717/post-141260>

Hi Christos; I'm not sure about an optimal progesterone level for men.

### Christos — 2014-09-18T05:26:00+0300

Triclycerides: 0.6mmol/L (range 0.3-2mmol/L)
Reverse osmosis rain/tap water is my water RX at the moment. Have kill switched my bedroom, no more mobile, cable internet and phone, and am getting my 20 gauss magnetico soon. Thinking LDL is high due to high BUN/creatinine, here is an exert from the EMF RX:
**"5. Why does the classic paleo diet make your LDL rise so often?**
The answer is simple. Dehydration is favored when you eat lots of protein and starchy carbs. A large amount of protein and starchy carbs draws water and even will make your temperature rise. For every degree your temperature increases your basal metabolic rate increase 13%. This increase in temperature, causes water dehydration in your carbon nanotubes. If you add back water that is fluoridated you will not improve. The water should be spring, mineral, or treated with reverse osmosis or you will have severe low carb flu present. ironically this is what the paleosphere calls it. I do not."
My BUN/creatinine is down from 35 so its coming down. If my progesterone is bad then I'm gonna get salivary ASI/melatonin done.

# Thread: Could upping your thyroid hormones accelerate aging?
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/>
**Subforum:** Heal Your Hormones
**Posts:** 28
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Greenisland — 2014-09-15T21:57:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141090>

I was wondering if upping your thyroid to the top quartile could possibly be speed your metabolism, and thus accelerate aging. If a calorie restriction increases lifespan and slows aging, wouldn't ramping up your thyroid, increasing food intake and speeding metabolic processes do the opposite?

### cantweight — 2014-09-15T22:05:44+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141091>

> [Greenisland said:](/goto/post?id=141090)
>
> I was wondering if upping your thyroid to the top quartile could possibly be speed your metabolism, and thus accelerate aging. If a calorie restriction increases lifespan and slows aging, wouldn't ramping up your thyroid, increasing food intake and speeding metabolic processes do the opposite?

Its like you are in my head....seriously. I was wondering the same.

Feeling good on calorie restriction/IF. Wondering how much of my energy was being depleted simply through digestion

I am only a few weeks off of my thyroid meds after taking them since 2006. I had been trying to work myself off of them for the last year (following gut instinct) but every time I dropped the dose my docs would freak and tell me it needed to be back to 180mg. I stopped them completely over 2 weeks ago now and I am feeling better by the day. My skin is improving, my mood, my energy....still a little lump in the throat thing going on but other than that I feel great.

### NeilBB — 2014-09-15T23:54:35+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141096>

I think these are fair questions. Specifically, there seems to normally be a strong seasonal variation in mammalian T3 levels. This is not necessarily pathological, despite what the paleo world seems to think. It seems to be part of seasonal mammalian design... [http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4064697/](http://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC4064697/)

I don't think it's all that easy to figure out how thyroid hormone should be "replaced," especially in view of chronobiology and ketosis, living in the altered field of the modern world. I hope that Jack re-addresses this issue in more detail because it's an enormous issue...

### Greenisland — 2014-09-16T00:22:34+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141100>

I am wondering about it as I know with my low thyroid I move more slowly, rest and relax a lot compared to my high energy burn out husband that is aging much faster than I am. More anecdotal curiosity: My grandmother had lots of thyroid symptoms. She used to board horses and called herself "an easy keeper", meaning, a horse that you don't have to feed very much. We used to blame her for giving us the "fat" gene. She lived a lifestyle of calorie restriction and was a lifelong Weight Watchers member. She looked great into her 80s and lived to 100.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-16T16:01:32+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141153>

Well I went on a bender this AM in the circadian biohackers site about this very issue because it sets the tone for the next blog. MS.

>
> Well I went on a bender this AM in the circadian biohackers site about this very issue because it sets the tone for the next blog. MS.

I just read it. It's clear that hormones are an intermediary between the field/environment and your own physiology AND that it's dangerous to exogenously mess with them. That has been clear for a while.

But Jack, you have in the past recommended optimizing certain hormones to upper 25% of range using bio-identicals. I think that's what's confusing people, myself included. So would you say that there still is a short-term role for bio-identical optimization of hormones like T3 as other environmental parameters are being adjusted? Or is it always dangerous? What about people who no longer have a functional thyroid at all? What should they do?

### NeilBB — 2014-09-16T16:50:45+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141161>

And regarding the thymus, can you explain why we see the opposite of involution, in fact, we see hyperplasia (and sometimes neoplasia) of the thymus in another common autoimmune disease, myasthenia gravis?

### Shijin13 — 2014-09-16T17:05:26+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/could-upping-your-thyroid-hormones-accelerate-aging.11719/post-141163>

>
> I just read it. It's clear that hormones are an intermediary between the field/environment and your own physiology AND that it's dangerous to exogenously mess with them. That has been clear for a while.
>
> But Jack, you have in the past recommended optimizing certain hormones to upper 25% of range using bio-identicals. I think that's what's confusing people, myself included. So would you say that there still is a short-term role for bio-identical optimization of hormones like T3 as other environmental parameters are being adjusted? Or is it always dangerous? What about people who no longer have a functional thyroid at all? What should they do?

@[NeilBB](https://forum.jackkruse.com/members/6879/) my thoughts are is it depends on the specifics of the individual and their zipcode. Jack has said that supplementation of D in the winter isn't a good thing, for most people. Yet he's told me that due to where I live, and what I do and based upon my hormones - I need year round supplementation of D.

I think this topic is relevant to what happened to me last winter with my thyroid even though I was in ketosis. hypo- symptoms, with hyper labs. I felt like crap, couldn't handle CT very well, gained 30lbs. my labs were so-so. I know in the past Jack has said that we evolved to have low T3 levels across the circadian cycles. So intuitively that tells me even though we've cranked my thyroid meds year round - I might need to cycle them across the seasons. I'm considering testing this theory this fall/winter and see how that goes...

I would think Thyroid stress would be greatest during Summer - longer light cycles, more carbs - hence the upregulation of T3. where as in the winter it would down regulate, as a result of the Vitamin A cycle. so too much exogenous T4/T3 during winter will cascade the extra into rT3, even when in Ketosis thus causing electron loss and inefficient operation of mitochondria and the system.

who know's if I'm on or off on this... this is what my intuition is telling me based upon what Jack's been teaching us. who knows if I'm right or if I'm wrong.

### NeilBB — 2014-09-16T17:26:47+0300

Yeah, I think you are right. Messing with hormones is very problematic because the targets are always moving, with the environment and with the seasons. But I think sometimes the benefits of replacement outweigh the risks for certain people in certain situations. The trouble is, the conventional and functional medicine approach don't take all these variables into account. I would just like to see Jack address it further, maybe not from the standpoint of "historically optimal" as much as from the standpoint of incremental medical fine-tuning in a non-optimal world.

I, myself, have used oral Vitamin D as well as tanning beds in the winter in the past. I always seemed to benefit from it... But I think it must confuse chronobiology and prevent full cold adaptation. So I am considering abandoning it this winter...

Tests like this are the future of medicine. People will soon be able to test for and spot cancers long before they manifest themselves using current methods. Testing can already help prevent diabetes, heart and blood vessel illness, prostate cancer, and other diseases.

The new testing technology is a real hot potato for the FDA. Regulators are often suspicious of new products. The agency is also more influenced by older firms offering old technology. So I have to exchange for just a sheet again or go to cover only my legs, this works too. Last night I woke up a few times with cold skin, but it wasn't so bad. Maybe what Jack said is starting slowly...

### Chrysanthemama — 2014-10-23T04:46:43+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/how-to-do-ct-during-sleep.11723/post-144790>

I live in the mountains, and it's been growing steadily cooler each day for the past month. This is the most beautiful Fall I've experienced in decades. Really, nothing like this one. So incredibly beautiful. The timing of it all is as if its in perfect regression. Each day a bit cooler in the morning, and a bit cooler in the afternoon. The color storm has been incredible. I still have my windows slightly open at night and the radiant heat turned to the lowest setting. My bedroom is 10 degrees cooler than the rest of the house no matter what season (lucky me!). I sleep with a sheet, and a light-weight down blanket rolled next to me. Even though I just got a puppy a couple weeks ago, I'm still sleeping so good. I'm completely comfortable with just the sheet for most of the night. The past couple days, I've covered my knees only, with my arms and feet outside the sheet. Feels great. I have not been doing any CT at all, other than swimming outside 2x a week (which is not normal for my town, but our large, indoor aquatic center is closed until the end of November, and my own little private outdoor pool is being leased by the County until it reopens. ) My outdoor, natural, cold-spring CTing will start in earnest soon. My hubbie has already started. I just don't like the dang predatory water beetles! It needs to be way colder for me to go int he spring.

I am peri-menopausal. Finally got my labs back this week and got a program adjustment from Dzugan. The have reduced my Tri-Est and increased my Progesterone, DHEA and Pregnenalone. Hopefully it will help with this head pain.

He has me on a magnesium, valerian root, progesterone and DHEA cocktail when I feel my headache coming on (which is every day). So far it has not given me relief.

Based on these labs they have increased my progesterone, decreased my estrogen, increased my testosterone and bumped my pregnenalone.

I might add I am on Tizanadine 4mg per night as a preventative for headaches (not working yet) and .125mg of Xanax each night to help me sleep. I still wake up several times during the night. I go to bed and wake up at the same time each day, regardless of the day.

Progesterone: 0.7 ng/ml
Estrogens, Total: 412 pg/ml
C-Reactive Protein: 1.1 mg/L
Seratonin, Serum: 128 ng/ml

Pregnenalone, MS: 54 ng/dL
DHEA-Sulfate: 88.4 ug/dL
Cortisol: 11.5 ug/dL
Vitamin D3 25-Hydroxy: 32 ng/mL

# Thread: When you swim in EMF....
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-swim-in-emf.11729/>
**Subforum:** The EMF Rx
**Posts:** 7
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### Krapule — 2014-09-18T03:04:11+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/when-you-swim-in-emf.11729/post-141303>

Hi everyone,
just want to share some experiences...
Two years ago I discovered what effect the emf were having on me when I unplugged my wifi and my cell phone (neuropathy, arrhythmia and musculo-squelletic pain and cramps).
I have been pretty good at mitigating the effects (mainly avoidance ) but at work it's not easy. I work in an operating room and it's pretty much a EMF pit. Neon lights, cell phones, wifi and all the machines all around.
Using a epi-paleoish diet, drinking a lot of RO water, blue blockers, CoQ10, seafood 3-5 times a week I was doing not too bad (even using a portable schumann resonator at work). But recently some symptoms are reappearing e.g. arrhythmia. The probable cause: I worked more hours in the last 3 weeks (like two 24 hours in hospital in three days) than I was working in the last months. This shows to me that my tolerance is pretty thin in regard to the emf...
In my workplace it's crazy to see how many people are struggling with obesity, tendinitis, autoimmune diseases and some vague complains... Some people are getting my message slowly (usually those with the worst problems) but I am certainly view as a crazy bastard by my colleagues who are on their iPhone all the time.
Anyway, the message here is that you might have to make drastic changes in your lifestyle if you want to get better (if not only survive!). I'm considering reducing my hours (with a reduction in revenues...) and eventually simply changing job. I thought I'd do my job until my 60's...now I'm considering that I might not do this work in my 50's, I'm 43...
Time to get some grounding before going to bed. This is a nice group of people that I'm very happy to be a part of.
Night

# Thread: Brain Responses to Food and Weight Loss - Research Review
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-responses-to-food-and-weight-loss-research-review.11731/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 1
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ssj3 — 2014-09-18T11:29:16+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/brain-responses-to-food-and-weight-loss-research-review.11731/post-141327>

_The Study: Brain responses to food and weight loss, by Behary and Miras, in Experimental Physiology, 2014_
[http://www.researchgate.net/publication/265514402\_Brain\_responses\_to\_food\_and\_weight\_loss](http://www.researchgate.net/publication/265514402_Brain_responses_to_food_and_weight_loss)

**Review by Chris Beardsley and Bret Contreras**

**What's the background?**
Studying brain responses to food is challenging. However, functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) and positron emission tomography (PET) are tools that have been recently developed for appetite research. FMRI provides information on brain activity by detecting changes in blood flow. PET uses radioactive tracers that detect surrogate markers of neuronal activation, including cerebral blood flow and glucose uptake, and also provide information on neurotransmission and neuro-receptor availability.
**What did the reviewers find?
_In healthy people_**
The reviewers explored the findings of the new fMRI and PET studies regarding brain responses to food in healthy people. From fMRI studies, they observed that in healthy adults, certain parts of the brain are activated preferentially in response to high-calorie food compared to low-calorie food cues. They also noted that increases in brain activation when switching from the fed to the fasted state measured using fMRI correlate with the increase in appeal bias towards high-calorie food. From PET studies, they observed that the release of dopamine in the brain was correlated with the subjective pleasantness of food.
**_In obese people_**
The reviewers explored the findings of the new fMRI and PET studies into the differences in brain responses to food in healthy and obese people. From PET studies, they observed that the activation of the hypothalamus in response to food is more reduced in healthy individuals than in the obese, indicating that a meal is less satiating for the obese. Similarly, many fMRI studies have found greater anticipatory responses in the brain to visual food cues in obese subjects than in healthy individuals. However, they also noted conflicting findings.
**_During weight loss_**
The reviewers observed that although few neuroimaging studies have examined periods of weight loss. However, one study found that an 8-week, low-calorie diet was associated with higher activation of brain reward systems in response to food pictures. Another trial exploring a lifestyle intervention observed higher activation in brain regions involved in food cue processing compared to a control group. Increased reward, perception and preoccupation with food could lead to reduced weight loss and even weight regain.
**What are the practical implications?**
The human brain responds to the rewarding value of food and promotes the consumption of energy-dense food. Also, diets lead to compensatory brain activation, which may reduce their efficacy. This implies that the psychological aspects of dieting should not be neglected when devising lifestyle interventions for weight loss.


══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
# Thread: Physiological links between circadian rhythms, metabolism and nutrition
**Thread URL:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physiological-links-between-circadian-rhythms-metabolism-and-nutrition.11732/>
**Subforum:** The New Monster Thread
**Posts:** 2
══════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════════
### ssj3 — 2014-09-18T11:33:42+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/physiological-links-between-circadian-rhythms-metabolism-and-nutrition.11732/post-141329>

[http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2014.078295/abstract](http://onlinelibrary.wiley.com/doi/10.1113/expphysiol.2014.078295/abstract)

**New Findings**

-   What is the topic of this review?
    This review provides a discussion of the links between circadian, metabolic and nutritional physiology, with a particular focus on mouse and human studies.
-   What advances does it highlight?
    Links between circadian biology and metabolism exist from the molecular level through to whole-organism physiology. Advances highlighted include the following: interactions between circadian clock genes/proteins and intracellular metabolic pathways; identification of the function of clocks in specific peripheral tissues; effects of timed feeding; and the variable effect of obesity on rhythms.

Circadian rhythms, metabolism and nutrition are closely interlinked. A great deal of recent research has investigated not only how aspects of metabolic physiology are driven by circadian clocks, but also how these circadian clocks are themselves sensitive to metabolic change. At the cellular level, novel feedback loops have been identified that couple circadian ‘clock genes’ and their proteins to expression of nuclear receptors, regulation of redox state and other major pathways. Using targeted disruption of circadian clocks, mouse models are providing novel insight into the role of tissue-specific clocks in glucose homeostasis and body weight regulation. The relationship between circadian rhythms and obesity appears complex, with variable alteration of rhythms in obese individuals. However, it is clear from animal studies that the timing and nutritional composition of meals can regulate circadian rhythms, particularly in peripheral tissues. Translation of these findings to human physiology now represents an important goal.

### Jack Kruse — 2014-09-18T15:25:59+0300

### spalmi — 2014-09-18T13:03:41+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snp-genes.11733/post-141337>

Does anyone know if their is any research that says an SNP can be changed? Or if Jack has a theory on how they can be changed? If your not familiar, and SNP is a single-nucleotide polymorphism and it is a variation within the DNA. These variations can be inherited and likely epigenetics plays a role. From my understanding they are fixed at birth and can not be changed. Although via epigenetics their strength of expression can be changed.

### bionaut — 2014-09-18T15:11:02+0300
**Source:** <https://forum.jackkruse.com/threads/snp-genes.11733/post-141341>

Gentetic data is copied from cell to cell in mitosis. It is much easier to make a change and then duplicate that change 1 trillion times as you form into an adult. To make a global change you would have to do the reverse of this process, rewire a trillion cells. Viruses do this in targeted fashion. Technically we could use a virus to fix snp's in the future. However this is a moot point if our environmental inputs suck. For example if you do not have a mthfr snp and you exercise a lot (using the cp pathway) you will crush your methyl groups in your body in effect having the output of asnp without actually having one.

If you are interested in how genetic data is changed read osf2.

Mitigating SNP's through nutrigenomics can yield benefits but be willing to put the time into testing and research it is a moving target. I am doing it currently but it is after I had worked through other protocols on this site.
